later in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/02/11/man-found-shot-to-death-in-oakland-neighborhood" target="_blank" >3700 block of South Lake Park Avenue</a>, Wentworth District police officers responded to a call of a person shot and found an unidentified man with an apparent gunshot wound to the head, also lying in the street a few feet away from a vehicle with its doors open, according to another police report.</p><p>The man, who police described as a “John Doe,” was pronounced dead at 5:03 a.m. by paramedics on the scene.</p><p>After going through all the evidence on the scene, detectives found the vehicle left on the scene with its doors open was the same car used in the murder and carjacking of the 22-year-old, the report said.</p><p>Area 3 and Area 1 detectives are investigating and nobody has been arrested.<br></p>
articles.author: Sun-Times Wire

articles.title: 2 teens arrested after brawl during basketball game at Bronzeville high school prompts large police response
articles.summary: <p>Charges were pending for two 16-year-olds who were arrested when a brawl erupted after a basketball game at Dunbar Vocational Career Academy, prompting a large police response Wednesday night at the Bronzeville school. </p><p>Officers responded to the school, 3000 S. King Dr., for the fight where a 16-year-old boy and 16-year-old girl were both arrested as officers were trying to intervene, Chicago police said. </p><p>In a note to community members, Dunbar principal Richard McMillan said that the fight involved school community members and staffers who "helped de-escalate the situation and also notified the Chicago Police Department for additional support."</p><p>"Safety continues to be my top priority," McMillan said in the note. "While I cannot provide additional details for privacy reasons, I want to assure you that this situation is being handled in accordance with CPS policies."</p><p>"We will continue striving to help our students reach their full potential and exemplify our core values both in the classroom and in their extracurricular pursuits," McMillan added. </p><p>Charges were pending. </p>
articles.author: Sun-Times Wire

articles.title: Top mayoral aide says Chicago's struggling taxi cab industry needs a fare hike
articles.summary: <p>Chicago needs to raise taxi cab fares for the first time since 2016 to save a once-thriving industry decimated by ridesharing and the pandemic, a top mayoral aide said Thursday.</p><p>“The tax industry is very dear to us. It has a lot of value in Chicago. It was here before” Uber and Lyft, Business Affairs and Consumer Protection Commissioner Ivan Capifali told the Sun-Times. “We want to make sure that they survive.”</p><p>Capifali essentially urged the City Council to approve the cab fare hike that Mayor Brandon Johnson introduced without fanfare in late September while public attention was focused on the battle over his 2026 budget.</p><p>The mayor’s plan would maintain the cost of entering a cab, known as the “flag pull” at $3.25 for the first one-ninth of a mile, but provide substantial relief for cab drivers in other ways. </p><p>They include a $2.50 rush hour fee for rides between 3:30 p.m. and 7 p.m., a $1 overnight fee for rides between 8 p.m. and 6 a.m and increased charges for both time and distance.</p><p>Instead of 25 cents for each additional one-ninth mile, passengers would pay 31 cents. Another 25 cents would be tacked onto the fare for every 36 seconds instead of 20 cents.</p><p>The ordinance has languished in committee for months, but Capifali said that needs to change to accommodate the "high cost of everything," including fuel, insurance and maintenance.</p><p>“They haven’t seen any fare increase in a while and that has led to other problems… We have received complaints from consumers that some taxi drivers are requesting a flat fee. That sometimes feels like they’re getting gouged. We have to make sure that doesn’t happen. Increasing the cab fare could be a solution to that," he said.</p><p>John Moberg, president of Checker, Yellow and American United Cab, said the pending increase is long overdue. Chicago cab drivers got some relief in 2022 when then-Mayor Lori Lightfoot imposed a temporary fuel surcharge. But it was only in place for about a year.</p><p>“Rideshare hurt us. But COVID was almost like the nail in the coffin. We have almost no business in the downtown or North Shore anymore,” Moberg said. “Insurance has gone up every year for the last several years. That’s our biggest expense. The industry needs a raise to stay competitive and stay available. It’s been a long time.”</p><p>Chicago’s once-thriving taxi cab industry has been shrinking ever since the unregulated advent of ride-hailing services like Lyft and Uber.</p><p>It took years before the city finally stepped in and regulated that industry. By that time, the value of taxi cab medallions already had plummeted. <br>A medallion that once sold for as much as $400,000 is now valued at $5,000.</p><p>The pool of available drivers also is shrinking — and aging. Anyone who got laid off or needs to supplement their income can “go to Uber and be driving tomorrow," while it takes two or three weeks of classes to be cleared to drive a cab, Moberg said.</p><p>“We don’t have new blood with younger people coming in. It’s the same people who have been there for years and they’re slowly retiring and dying off. That’s probably our biggest handicap," Moberg said. </p><p>In 2017, the taxi industry got what advocates then called a “death blow." That’s when the U.S. Supreme Court let stand a federal appeals court ruling that snuffed out an attempt by the cab companies to level what they called an uneven playing field that favored Uber, whose investors included then-Mayor Rahm Emanuel’s brother.</p><p>The appeals court ruling essentially said the business models of taxis and ride-hailing services are different and, therefore, they can continue to operate under different sets of rules in Chicago.</p><p>That decision validated a 2014 city ordinance letting Uber and Lyft operate without taxi medallions, city-regulated fares, driver fingerprinting or other regulations cab companies and their drivers must follow. Uber and Lyft had long maintained that a background check based on FBI fingerprinting would discriminate against minorities.</p><p>In 2016, a divided City Council agreed to license ride-hailing drivers but opted not to require fingerprinting after Uber and Lyft threatened to abandon the Chicago market.</p><p>On Thursday, Moberg renewed his appeal for "the same regulations for both" the taxicab and rideshare industries.</p><p>“Their cars don’t have to be inspected. Ours do. They can go to any gas station and get a certificate. Our cars have to go to inspection lanes two or three times a year,” Moberg said. “Is the public safer in a rideshare car? Same thing with drivers."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4c0000" name="html-embed-module-4c0000"></a><iframe allow="monetization" frameborder="0" height="200" scrolling="no" src="https://play.prx.org/e?ge=prx_12963_8c00227b-d907-4999-8cf3-431b53c1af58&uf=https%3A%2F%2Fpublicfeeds.net%2Ff%2F12963%2Ffeed-rss.xml" style="min-width: 300px;" width="100%"> </iframe> </div> </div><p></p>
articles.author: Fran Spielman

articles.title: Voters cast ballots at Loop Supersite as early voting kicks off
articles.summary: <p>Voters looking to avoid long lines cast their ballots Thursday morning in the Loop as early voting opened for the Illinois primary election.</p><p>Kamilah Banks-Word didn’t want to wait to vote, so she stopped by the new voting supersite at 137 S. State St. on her way to work.</p><p>“Voting makes a difference in terms of how our society runs,” said Banks-Word, 47. “I think a lot of us are not aware of how important the primary and midterm elections are. Most people just vote for the presidency, and we see how that turned out.”</p><p>Banks-Word, of the South Loop, said she was voting to protect people’s rights across the country and wanted everyone to feel safe wherever they are.</p><p>“Everything's at stake at this point,” she said. “We've seen how everything has been going downhill, how people are being harassed by different political groups and law enforcement agents.”</p><p>Banks-Word wasn’t the only one encouraged to vote because of the Trump administration’s aggressive immigration enforcement campaign, which has sent hundreds of federal agents into cities across the country — including Chicago.</p><p>In early December, the Department of Homeland Security said at least 4,400 people had been detained during Operation Midway Blitz, the Chicago area campaign.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2e0000" class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Erick El, of Uptown, hadn’t voted in years but felt it was time to do something to overturn elected officials who he said were “tearing America apart.”</p><p>“We need to get Trump out of office,” said El, 54. “He's ripping families apart. People are losing their lives.”</p><p>The federal funding cuts to the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, or SNAP, and Medicaid also pushed El to cast his vote.</p><p>“People need their health insurance, and people need to eat,” El said.</p><p>Among the people in Chicago <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/06/walmart-amazon-cps-top-employers-illinois-workers-still-need-snap-benefits-trump-work-requirements-lose-benefits" target="_blank" >living in SNAP households</a> who are 22 or older, 47% are employed but still need government support to help buy groceries, according to 2024 U.S. Census data analyzed by WBEZ.</p><p>Early voting for the March 17 election started Thursday at the supersite and at the Chicago Board of Elections office at 69 W. Washington St. Early voting in all Chicago wards begins March 2.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f188f2d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6448x3619+0+340/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0e%2F24%2F2d2a142048dc80433274e35554ab%2Fearlyvoting-260213-6.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/866cfde/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6448x3619+0+340/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0e%2F24%2F2d2a142048dc80433274e35554ab%2Fearlyvoting-260213-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Carley Smelser, an election judge, assists Erick El in casting his ballot Thursday for the primary election at the early voting supersite in the Loop.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Don’t wait until the last second,” Chicago Board of Elections Chair Marisel Hernandez said at a morning media briefing at the supersite.</p><p>Hernandez also said the board would ensure that all voters feel safe casting their ballots after a former White House aide said federal agents should be at polling sites.</p><p>“Every voter has the right to register to vote and then vote free of intimidation, threats and coercion,” Hernandez said. “We have federal and state laws that prohibit any type of intimidation of voters during elections, and in fact, these laws specifically prohibit the presence of federal officers at any polling place.”</p><p>White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said last week that she <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/live/donald-trump-news-updates-2-5-2026" target="_blank" >“can’t guarantee”</a> a federal agent wouldn’t be near a polling location.</p><p>“I mean, that’s, frankly, a very silly hypothetical question,” said Leavitt. “But what I can tell you is, I haven’t heard the president discuss any formal plans to put ICE outside of polling locations. It’s a disingenuous question.”</p><p>Hernandez said all election judges know to call the board and 911 if they see any unusual activity near voting sites.</p><p>Officials also reminded voters casting ballots by mail to return them quickly after the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/02/04/usps-postmark-change-could-threaten-mail-in-voters-cook-county-officials-warn" target="_blank" >U.S. Postal Service announced</a> that it could not guarantee ballots would be postmarked before Election Day.</p><p>Under the new policy, mail will be postmarked, then date-stamped, on the day it is processed at the post office, rather than the day it is dropped in a mailbox.</p><p>Because mail is often processed days after it is dropped off, if ballots are postmarked after Election Day it could jeopardize whether votes are counted. Mail-in ballots must be postmarked by Election Day and received within 14 days to be counted.</p><p>“Don't risk a late postmark on your ballot,” said Hernandez. “Your vote needs to be counted.”</p>
articles.author: Cindy Hernandez

articles.title: Long-sought Chicago police staffing study recommends adding more cops, hiring civilians
articles.summary: <p>A much-anticipated study on Chicago police staffing urges more hiring to better respond to community needs and offers a new model for how to best deploy officers with the resources available. </p><p>The study, conducted by Matrix Consulting Group, was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2024/2/5/24062649/how-best-deploy-chicago-police-officers-city-council-committee-vote" target="_blank" >commissioned by the City Council</a> in February 2024 to find ways to close gaps between police response times in different neighborhoods. </p><p>The group quickly determined the police department had “real and uneven staffing pressures,” with workloads varying significantly depending on district and unit, according to <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagopolice.org/wp-content/uploads/WFA-ES.pdf" target="_blank" >an executive summary</a> released this week. </p><p>That resulted in inconsistent service and limited supervisory oversight in “high-demand areas,” the firm found. “This study examined every bureau, every unit and every function, down to the individual,” Allyson Clark Henson, the police department's executive director of constitutional policing and reform, told the Sun-Times.</p><p>“They provided us a foundational analytical framework that then allows us to guide a phased implementation, prioritizing what we think is most important to happen first.”</p><p>The study recommends shifting 600 cops out of jobs that could be done by people without police powers, then filling many of those positions with civilians. It also calls for hiring an additional 270 patrol officers and 90 patrol sergeants. </p><p>Instead of keeping a fixed number of officers in each district, the study argues that staffing should be adjusted regularly based on workloads. The department would also tackle challenges with oversight, with the study finding that “meaningful supervision becomes difficult” when sergeants are responsible for too many officers.</p><p>Robert Boik, senior vice president of public safety at the Civic Committee of the Commercial Club of Chicago, one of the study’s sponsors, said a supervisor at a police district could be overseeing close to 20 officers at any given time. </p><p>“We've invested a lot in accountability structures, but the most important layer of accountability is that relationship between the supervisor and the employee,” Boik said in an interview. “You could be the best manager in the world, but 20 is too much.”</p><p>Clark Henson and Boik said the key takeaway of the study is the long-term analytical model it provides for determining staffing. </p><p>“For years, CPD has deployed based on a set of internal metrics, and the study really provides a replicable, mathematically-based model that can serve as the basis for staffing for the first time ever and for years to come,” said Boik, Clark Henson’s predecessor. </p><p>The staffing analysis was required under <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/9/6/18327050/what-is-a-consent-decree" target="_blank" >a federal consent decree</a> mandating sweeping reforms in the wake of the police killing of Laquan McDonald. Similar studies were commissioned in 2016 and 2019, but were never completed, and preliminary results weren’t made public.</p><p>Six funders, including the Civic Committee, stepped up to fund the study, which ended up costing $780,000, according to Boik. A final report is expected to be released to the public in the coming weeks. </p><p>Police officials now have to weigh the recommendations while considering budget constraints and collective bargaining agreements. </p><p>“I'm looking forward to the final product,” said police Supt. Larry Snelling. “I don't expect it to be 100% perfect. I do expect that there may be some things that we need to look at and talk about, which is why we have this draft right now.”</p><p><i>Read the executive summary:</i></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://docs.google.com/viewer?embedded=true&url=https://www.chicagopolice.org/wp-content/uploads/WFA-ES.pdf"></iframe> </div> </div></div><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2024/2/5/24062649/how-best-deploy-chicago-police-officers-city-council-committee-vote" target="_blank" >City Council committee OKs study on how best to deploy Chicago police as resources shrink, some crimes spike</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/10/25/chicago-police-department-exodus-suburbs-recruiting" target="_blank" >Chicago Police Department exodus: New cops are leaving in droves, Sun-Times investigation finds</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/11/05/chicago-police-hiring-slowdown-larry-snelling-brandon-johnson-budget" target="_blank" >CPD to slow hiring, pause academy training next summer to cut costs, superintendent says</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
articles.author: Sophie Sherry

articles.title: Man facing attempted murder charges in Back of the Yards shooting that left one person wounded
articles.summary: <p>A man is facing attempted murder charges in a shooting that took place in Back of the Yards last month, according to Chicago police.</p><p>Jonathan Smith, 30, faces two counts of attempted murder, two counts of aggravated battery for discharging a firearm into a vehicle and two counts of aggravated battery by discharging a firearm, police said.</p><p>Smith is alleged to have shot a 23-year-old man who was sitting in a parked car in the 4800 block of South Elizabeth Street at 9:16 p.m. Jan. 24, police said. The wounded man was taken to University of Chicago Medical Center in good condition.</p><p>He is also accused of shooting at a 28-year-old man and a 4-year-old child who were inside another vehicle, police said.</p><p>Smith is due back in court Friday.</p>
articles.author: Sun-Times Wire

articles.title: Preckwinkle calls on top Cook County prosecutor to investigate ICE, feds involved in Chicago area shootings
articles.summary: <p>Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle, along with more than half of the county’s commissioners, have called on Cook County State’s Attorney Eileen O’Neil Burke to file charges against federal immigration agents involved in shootings that took place last year in the Chicago area — though Burke says her office has no jurisdiction.</p><p>The statement points to the fatal shooting of Silverio Villegas González, who was shot by federal immigration agents last September in suburban Franklin Park. The statement also points to the shooting that wounded Marimar Martinez, who survived after being shot multiple times by a federal agent last year. It asks the state’s attorney to investigate any and “all available charges” and “use of excessive force” in the shootings.</p><p>They also point to the historic disproportionate use of force by law enforcement against Black residents, and said justice must be applied evenly because, “No officer and no agency is above the law.”</p><p>“The Trump Administration’s immigration enforcement agenda has produced a clear and alarming pattern of violence,” the statement said. “We support thorough law enforcement investigations wherever federal agents have violated the law. We urge the Cook County State’s Attorney to act swiftly, proactively and transparently to ensure accountability and deliver justice for the families and communities who have been harmed.”</p><p>It comes just over a week after Illinois House Democrats <a class="Link" href="https://ilhousedems.com/2026/02/03/elected-officials-call-on-cook-county-states-attorney-eileen-oneil-burke-to-investigate-the-fatal-shooting-of-silverio-villegas-gonzalez-and-the-near-fatal-shooting-of-marimar-martin/" target="_blank" ><u>released a similar call to action</u></a> aimed at the state’s attorney’s office.</p><p>The Cook County State’s Attorney’s office said though Burke is “deeply concerned” about the “abhorrent behavior” of Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents nationwide, her office “does not conduct independent investigations into criminal conduct and lacks jurisdiction over federal agencies, except in extremely narrow and limited circumstances.”</p><p>Burke said her team has drafted a protocol for how local legal systems can respond to federal agents in a way that’s “clear and grounded in law,” which the office said has been shared with local and state law enforcement, as well as other prosecutors for feedback.</p><p>“We are committed to doing all we can under the law to support and review law enforcement investigations of that conduct and prosecute when appropriate,” Burke's office said in a statement.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/06/eileen-oneill-burke-brandon-johnson-immigration-ice-law" >County’s top prosecutor calls Mayor Brandon Johnson’s ICE executive order ‘wholly inappropriate’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/02/03/mayor-brandon-johnson-executive-order-accountability-ice-agents-operation-midway-blitz-states-attorney-eileen-oneill-burke" >Mayor Johnson defends executive order aimed at holding ICE accountable for alleged abuses</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Burke has found herself between local leaders grasping at ways to hold federal agents accountable for wounding and killing residents, federal laws that often block those actions, and President Donald Trump's administration which has blocked use of force investigations. </p><p>Last week, Burke told her staff that Mayor Brandon Johnson’s recent <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/02/03/mayor-brandon-johnson-executive-order-accountability-ice-agents-operation-midway-blitz-states-attorney-eileen-oneill-burke" target="_blank" ><u>executive order</u></a> — which orders CPD to document potential crimes committed by federal agents — is inappropriate and could jeopardize criminal prosecutions.</p><p>Johnson <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/02/03/mayor-brandon-johnson-executive-order-accountability-ice-agents-operation-midway-blitz-states-attorney-eileen-oneill-burke" target="_blank" ><u>defended the move</u></a>, and said he was trying to help Burke by providing evidence for prosecutions of federal agents, which he claimed she has spoken about publicly.</p><p>It’s one of many moves state, county and local officials have taken in attempts to curb federal agents in the Chicago area, though many have faced legal uncertainty, and experts have cast doubt on one executive order Johnson signed in August that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/08/30/mayor-johnson-protecting-chicago-initiative-executive-order" target="_blank" ><u>threatened lawsuits against federal agents</u></a>.</p><p>In November, now former Cook County Circuit Chief Judge Timothy Evans <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/14/top-cook-county-judge-bars-civil-arrests-at-courthouses-citing-chilling-effect" target="_blank" ><u>signed an order</u></a> barring civil arrests, or those without a warrant signed by a judge, at county courts, though federal officials vowed to violate it anyways.</p>
articles.author: Violet Miller

articles.title: Longtime incumbent Preckwinkle faces stiff challenge from Ald. Reilly in Cook County board president primary
articles.summary: <p>Chicago Ald. Brendan Reilly, one of the most conservative members of the City Council, who bills himself as an independent voice, wants a new gig: to run Cook County government.</p><p>He’s looking to unseat longtime County Board President Toni Preckwinkle, who doubles as the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2024/03/14/toni-preckwinkle-political-muscle-democrats-march-primary-election" ><u>powerful leader</u> </a>of the Cook County Democratic Party, in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/how-to-vote-illinois-primary" ><u>general primary</u> </a>on March 17, Preckwinkle’s 79th birthday.</p><p>She oversees one of the biggest counties in the U.S., including the county’s jail, vast court system and large public health system that has a mission to treat patients <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2025/05/23/how-medicaid-cuts-from-congress-could-affect-every-cook-county-resident" target="_blank" ><u>no matter if they can pay</u></a>. The county employs more than 20,000 people and has a roughly <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/11/20/cook-county-board-toni-preckwinkle-10-billion-2026-budget" target="_blank" ><u>$10 billion annual budget</u></a> that must be approved by a 17-member board of separately elected commissioners. Preckwinkle also oversees the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2024/04/27/palos-preserves-gets-10-million-makeover" target="_blank" ><u>Forest Preserves</u></a> of Cook County.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8e0000" name="image-8e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/48521c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7392x4149+0+391/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fac%2Fc44b3d5b4d9894dcc51cdafbc2b5%2Fmayorbudget-101725-50.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc1a541/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7392x4149+0+391/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fac%2Fc44b3d5b4d9894dcc51cdafbc2b5%2Fmayorbudget-101725-50.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8864ddb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7392x4149+0+391/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fac%2Fc44b3d5b4d9894dcc51cdafbc2b5%2Fmayorbudget-101725-50.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Ald. Brendan Reilly (42nd) speaks with reporters after Mayor Brandon Johnson presented his proposed 2026 budget during a Chicago City Council meeting Oct. 16, 2025." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8864ddb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7392x4149+0+391/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fac%2Fc44b3d5b4d9894dcc51cdafbc2b5%2Fmayorbudget-101725-50.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3bdf7cf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7392x4149+0+391/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fac%2Fc44b3d5b4d9894dcc51cdafbc2b5%2Fmayorbudget-101725-50.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8864ddb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7392x4149+0+391/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fac%2Fc44b3d5b4d9894dcc51cdafbc2b5%2Fmayorbudget-101725-50.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ald. Brendan Reilly (42nd) speaks with reporters after Mayor Brandon Johnson presented his proposed 2026 budget during a Chicago City Council meeting Oct. 16, 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Reilly, 54, who has represented downtown Chicago for roughly 19 years, is hammering Preckwinkle over a property tax system upgrade that, according to the Chicago Tribune and Injustice Watch, has been plagued by delays. Property tax bills have gone out late, causing school districts across the region to <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/12/19/property-tax-payment-delay-costs-chicago-public-schools-almost-a-quarter-million-dollars-a-day" target="_blank" ><u>take out loans</u></a> with interest, as they waited to collect their share of property taxes.</p><p>“While my opponent has had a great career in public service, she’s stayed a bit too long,” Reilly said. “Cook County government is broken in a number of areas that are making it a lot less affordable to be able to live here.”</p><p>Reilly said he would terminate the county’s contract with Tyler Technologies, which has been working to upgrade the technology behind the property tax system for years, if needed.</p><p>Preckwinkle countered she’s one of several separately elected officials who oversee a complicated tax system. Other elected officials, such as the county assessor and treasurer, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/02/06/what-to-know-democratic-primary-cook-county-assessor-fritz-kaegi-pat-hynes" target="_blank" ><u>determine property values</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2025/11/18/cook-county-treasurer-maria-pappas-mayoral-campaign-chicago-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" ><u>mail tax bills</u></a>.</p><p>“My view has been to try to get people to work together to solve the problems,” Preckwinkle said. “You can’t do that if you point fingers and throw people under the bus.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9a0000" name="image-9a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0dd08e9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6553x3678+0+347/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fb8%2Fbd8f18ff4322a669b5ea9c3c0b4c%2Fmasstransit-121725-018.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fe635ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6553x3678+0+347/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fb8%2Fbd8f18ff4322a669b5ea9c3c0b4c%2Fmasstransit-121725-018.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8db14f5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6553x3678+0+347/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fb8%2Fbd8f18ff4322a669b5ea9c3c0b4c%2Fmasstransit-121725-018.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle listens to Gov. JB Pritzker speak after signing the Northern Illinois Transit Authority Act during a ceremony at Union Station Dec. 16, 2025. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8db14f5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6553x3678+0+347/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fb8%2Fbd8f18ff4322a669b5ea9c3c0b4c%2Fmasstransit-121725-018.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e51784a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6553x3678+0+347/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fb8%2Fbd8f18ff4322a669b5ea9c3c0b4c%2Fmasstransit-121725-018.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8db14f5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6553x3678+0+347/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fb8%2Fbd8f18ff4322a669b5ea9c3c0b4c%2Fmasstransit-121725-018.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle listens to Gov. JB Pritzker speak after signing the Northern Illinois Transit Authority Act during a ceremony at Union Station Dec. 16, 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Preckwinkle is known for running a tight financial ship. She hasn’t raised the county’s portion of property taxes during her 16-year tenure, though a <a class="Link" href="https://news.wttw.com/2017/10/16/toni-preckwinkle-soda-tax-repeal-county-budget-shortfall" target="_blank" ><u>soda tax she supported years ago was repealed</u></a> after a huge backlash. The county’s pension fund is funded around 66%, <a class="Link" href="https://www.civicfed.org/dashboard/localgovtpensions" target="_blank" ><u>more than double</u></a> some of the city’s pension funds. The county has received four bond rating upgrades since 2021, while Chicago is mired in debt.</p><p>Preckwinkle underscores that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2025/12/19/chicago-city-council-approves-alternate-revenue-plan-minus-mayor-brandon-johnsons-corporate-head-tax" target="_blank" ><u>Reilly and a group</u></a> of conservative and moderate alders recently muscled through a Chicago budget that includes a host of tax hikes, including a property tax increase for Chicago public libraries. While the budget avoided a new tax on the city’s largest corporations, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/city-hall/2025/12/12/corporate-head-tax-brandon-johnson-2026-proposed-budget-job-killer-claim" target="_blank" ><u>detested by Chicago’s business community</u></a>, it “puts the burden entirely on taxpayers while exempting labor from any shared burden,” and included “only a handful of efficiencies and cost savings,” the Civic Federation, a non-partisan government watchdog, <a class="Link" href="https://civicfed.org/chicagos-fy2026-adopted-budget" target="_blank" ><u>wrote in an analysis</u></a>.</p><p>Roughly $5 billion of the city’s $16.6 billion budget this year will go toward pension and debt payments, after decades of mismanagement. That burden contributed to a nearly $1.2 billion gap between revenue and expenses in 2026, as the city has limited avenues to raise money aside from property tax and fee hikes.</p><p>Preckwinkle is a former history teacher and longtime Chicago alderperson representing the South Side before she was elected county board president in 2010. She uses her political power and progressive values to shape how the county spends money. She has championed <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/cook-county/2025/04/08/cook-county-leaders-are-touting-the-success-of-their-guaranteed-income-pilot-program" target="_blank" ><u>guaranteed income </u></a>for low to moderate income residents, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2025/06/05/cook-county-erases-nearly-665-million-in-medical-debt-for-more-than-a-half-million-residents" target="_blank" ><u>erased more than $600 million of medical debt</u></a>, and helped <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/the-end-of-cash-bail-in-illinois" target="_blank" ><u>eliminate cash bail</u></a> in Illinois. She has mentored many into higher office.</p><p>Reilly got his start in public service in the 1990s and worked as an aide to <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2025/02/13/what-former-illinois-house-speaker-michael-madigans-conviction-means-for-his-legacy" target="_blank" ><u>now-convicted </u></a>former Illinois House Speaker Michael Madigan. He left for the private sector and worked as an AT&T executive before defeating long-serving City Council incumbent, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2020/6/11/21288690/former-downtown-ald-burt-natarus-dies-council-obituary-carriages-valet-street-musicians" target="_blank" ><u>Burt Natarus</u></a>, in 2007.</p><p>Reilly hasn’t faced an opponent since. In 2020, he <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2020/10/27/21537277/alderman-brendan-reilly-states-attorney-kim-foxx-republican-pat-obrien-cook-county" target="_blank" ><u>broke with the Democratic Party</u></a> to endorse Republican Patrick O’Brien over Kim Foxx, Preckwinkle’s former chief of staff, for Cook County state’s attorney.</p><h3>Preckwinkle runs again because of Trump</h3><p>Preckwinkle said she was inspired to run for a fifth, four-year term to guard against Republican President Donald Trump. His One Big Beautiful Bill calls for <a class="Link" href="https://www.kff.org/medicaid/allocating-cbos-estimates-of-federal-medicaid-spending-reductions-across-the-states-enacted-reconciliation-package/" target="_blank" ><u>sweeping cuts</u></a> to public programs that support thousands of county residents, and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2025/05/23/how-medicaid-cuts-from-congress-could-affect-every-cook-county-resident" target="_blank" ><u>could put a big dent</u></a> in the overall county budget.</p><p>As immigration agents detained people off the streets and fired <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/21/judge-hands-down-critical-review-of-federal-immigration-agents-unprecedented-use-of-force" target="_blank" ><u>pepper balls and tear gas</u></a> at protesters throughout Chicago and the suburbs, she issued an <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/16/preckwinkle-issues-executive-order-to-create-ice-free-zones-in-cook-county" target="_blank" ><u>executive order</u></a> to create “ICE-free zones” in Cook County.</p><p>“Trump’s declared war on us,” Preckwinkle said. “We need somebody who’s going to stand up to him and stand for our residents and the rule of law. He’s not that man,” she said of Reilly.</p><p>She points to his support for a measure aimed at creating carveouts in Chicago’s sanctuary law that prohibit police from helping with civil immigration enforcement. Reilly was one of 11 Council members who pushed for a vote on the now-stalled measure, after a chorus of advocacy organizations <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/01/10/donald-trump-deportation-plan-ice-immigration-advocates-chicago-police" target="_blank" ><u>warned it</u></a> would open the door to constitutional rights violations and legal challenges.</p><p>In a statement, Reilly said he supports abolishing U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement and returning the agency to its “core functions during the Obama administration.” He said he would defend the <a class="Link" href="https://www.cookcountyil.gov/news/president-preckwinkle-signs-executive-order-banning-use-cook-county-owned-property-and" target="_blank" ><u>county’s restrictions</u></a> on using local resources for certain federal immigration enforcement. But he supports carveouts for people arrested or convicted for felonies such as sex crimes involving children or gang-related violence.</p><p>One of the biggest challenges the county faces is bracing for an estimated almost $300 million in losses per year, largely due to people losing their health insurance. Low-income residents who have public Medicaid health insurance will have <a class="Link" href="https://hfs.illinois.gov/info/fedresctr/medicaidfedpolicychfs.html" target="_blank" ><u>new work requirements</u></a> next year and more frequent checks to make sure they qualify, while others might drop insurance they bought on the government exchanges because <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/health/health-subsidies-expire-launching-millions-of-americans-into-2026-with-steep-insurance-hikes" target="_blank" ><u>subsidies are gone</u></a>.</p><p>People might still show up for medical care at Cook County Health’s two hospitals and several clinics, but might not be able to afford to pay what could be big bills. That means less money flowing to the county. The majority of the county’s patients have Medicaid or no insurance, records show. Dr. Erik Mikaitis, CEO of CCH, said the amount of uninsured patients is already swelling.</p><p>Reilly said the county should have planned for Medicaid losses earlier.</p><p>“You don’t wait for federal cuts to happen,” Reilly said. “You build a firewall before they hit.”</p><p>County leaders say they have been preparing. The health system started tightening its belt last year, with 10% cuts across all departments that will continue through 2026. They froze about 200 vacant positions. The county has set aside around $320 million in reserves, partly in case federal grants for everything from transportation to public health get cut, and nearly $70 million for potential Medicaid losses, a county spokesman said.</p><p>Reilly suggests pre-ranking medical services to keep or cut, prioritizing the ER and trauma care. He declined to say what he would cut, deferring to the health system.</p><p>Mikaitis said there are contingency plans — not cutting services but potentially consolidating them, such as offering a service in three clinics instead of five.</p><h3>Reilly suggests a Cook County ID</h3><p>In a statement, Reilly said he wants to make sure “Cook County services are for Cook County residents,” and that the county isn’t absorbing costs from other counties. He points to <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2020/03/12/treating-uninsured-patients-is-wreaking-havoc-on-cook-county-finances" target="_blank" ><u>WBEZ reporting</u></a> that underscores the impact on county finances for providing the most charity care, or discounted care, of any hospital in Illinois — around $217 million at the county’s John H. Stroger Jr. Hospital in 2024, a WBEZ analysis of the most recent <a class="Link" href="https://hfsrb.illinois.gov/inventories-data.html" target="_blank" ><u>state records</u></a> shows. In some cases, private hospitals stabilize patients, then refer them to the county for follow-up care.</p><p>“Verified residency and referral data would allow the county and the Attorney General to audit community benefit reports, enforce state discount laws, and hold private systems accountable when costs are shifted onto the public safety net,” Reilly said.</p><p>A health system spokeswoman said only about 4% of patients live outside the county. The county’s financial assistance program, CareLink, is only for county residents, and <a class="Link" href="https://healthcarereportcard.illinois.gov/hospital-uninsured-patient-discount-act" target="_blank" ><u>an Illinois law</u></a> caps how much any hospital can collect from uninsured patients who live in the state. Patients who live outside of Illinois and pay out of pocket could qualify for a discount.</p><p>Reilly said a countywide ID, which county commissioners have <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/7/5/18342498/two-cook-county-commissioners-proposing-county-i-d-card" target="_blank" ><u>floated before</u></a>, would help people prove residency for county services, such as medical care. The idea is not to restrict care or duplicate existing ID programs, like Chicago’s CityKey, Reilly said. The county ID would be for suburban residents, he suggests.</p><p>Whoever wins the Democratic primary would likely win in the November general election. There is <a class="Link" href="https://www.cookcountyclerkil.gov/elections/results-and-election-data/election-results/candidate-filing" target="_blank" ><u>no Republican candidate</u></a> for county board president. Michael Murphy is running as a Libertarian. Preckwinkle last won with about 69% of the vote in 2022 against Bob Fioretti.</p><p>Should she win, Preckwinkle said it would be her last term as county board president. She said she also plans to run for just one more, two-year term this spring to lead the Democratic Party.</p><p><i>Kristen Schorsch and Mariah Woelfel cover government for WBEZ. </i><br></p>
articles.author: Mariah Woelfel | WBEZ

articles.title: Model shop that provided the first draft of many great Chicago buildings closes
articles.summary: <p>For 35 years, Columbian Model & Exhibit Works has given the city glimpses of the future.</p><p>The River West company designed and built architectural models for developers and architects that provided an advance look — often with astonishing accuracy and detail — of the buildings, neighborhoods, skyscrapers and places planned for the city.</p><p>Their vast catalog includes models of the Lakeshore East high-rise development, along the south edge of Wacker Drive just west of DuSable Lake Shore Drive; the Jeanne Gang-designed St. Regis tower, at 363 E. Wacker Drive; and the huge 1/16th depiction of the 20-acre Obama Presidential Center campus.</p><p>But that all came to an end this month when the venerable company shut its doors for good.</p><p>Columbian Model President Catherine Tinker decided at the beginning of the year to retire. And she said her partners didn't want to continue the company, fearful that technological advancements in creating virtual models would ultimately eat away their business.</p><p>"They are concerned that because of the digital revolution, there's not enough work or physical models anymore," she said. "It's difficult. We're cleaning out the shop. It's difficult."</p><h3>From attorney to model-maker</h3><p>In the city of "no little plans," physical architectural models were once a big thing. </p><p>Former Mayor Richard J. Daley, attempting to show progress, would hold news conferences standing before impressive scale models of a proposed central area with new high-rises and development from 18th Street to North Avenue.</p><p>Over the decades, the models were our first look at many well-known projects around town from Crown Hall at the Illinois Institute of Technology, Marina City, Sears Tower, Central Station, the Harold Washington Library entries, Millennium Park and more.</p><p>That's less so now. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7a7792d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3765x2113+0+277/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2Fe6%2F1cfe7262465caeadea2e7a7017d5%2Fimg-0913.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Former President Barack Obama examines a model of the Obama Presidential Center campus made by Columbian Model & Exhibit Works.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided by the Obama Foundation</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In addition to tech advancements, Tinker said a tepid Chicago real estate market has caused fewer developers to pay for large models needed to attract buyers and investors.</p><p>Model making is also exacting work that requires materials and textures that can mimic the look of steel, brick and stone, while working with an architect to get the scale, color and details just right.</p><p>"It has to tell the story that the developer or [whomever] is trying to tell," Columbian Model Senior Designer Bill Bauman said. "But it's at a smaller scale, so the detail has to be finessed. So that you still get the idea without making it identical to full size."</p><p>It's a line of work Tinker fell into by chance 35 years ago.</p><p>A former trial attorney, she got introduced to model making when she got legal exhibits made for court cases.</p><p>She represented Morton Thiokol in a case against the NASA contractor, following the 1986 Space Shuttle Challenger tragedy. That's when the spacecraft exploded 73 seconds after liftoff when its rocket booster O-rings failed. The shuttle's seven crew members were killed.</p><p>"We actually got little models of the O-ring made that we shipped over to England so that we could explain the case to the guys from Lloyd's [of London insurance company] because they were the ones that ultimately were paying for everything," Tinker said.</p><p>Tinker said she turned to the model-making business after getting "burned out" with the legal profession — despite having no experience fabricating the objects.</p><p>"Originally, I was going to make trial exhibits," she said. "But then the architectural model-making part just sort of grew organically. So we ended up being architectural scale model makers."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel Enhancement" data-align-center> <a aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p></p><h3>A ‘sad’ end</h3><p>The Obama Presidential Center model is on display at the Obama Foundation headquarters. The work was widely shown and broadcast before the actual campus, scheduled to open in the spring, took shape.</p><p>"We were very disappointed that we never got to meet the Obamas," Tinker said. "I really thought they might come over [to the office] but they didn't."</p><p>Columbian Model's work also includes the Chicago Architecture Center's huge — 320 square feet — Chicago Model built in 2009.</p><p>The work shows 400 blocks of the city and downtown and is detailed with more than 1,000 buildings. It's updated every few months by Columbian Model.</p><p>Chicago Architecture Center Chief Marketing Officer Lauren Bakos said, "It made me sad," when she heard the shop was closing.</p><p>"I understand that technology has made it easier to do things digitally and use augmented reality and all of that," she said. "But to show off these structures that are being developed in Chicago, I think it's a really beautiful art. And it's sad that it's kind of a dying one."</p><p>Bakos said the company left the CAC with a few models to plug into the big exhibit, including one of the revamped Thompson Center.</p><p>"So we're on the hunt for someone else who can take over that project," she said.</p><p>And Columbian Model is looking for a new home for all its work. Bauman said the company has thrown away some of its models, including a few of the Lakeshore East towers.</p><p>"We've lost a lot them, but we still we have a dozen or so," Bauman said.</p><p>Tinker said, "There are smaller models that were on shelves that I need find to homes find for."</p>
articles.author: Lee Bey

articles.title: Man stabbed on CTA Green Line train near 47th Street
articles.summary: <p>A man was stabbed on a CTA Green Line train Thursday night on the South Side, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The man, 44, was riding the train about 10:45 p.m. when someone swung a sharp object at him, police said in a statement. Authorities arrived to the 47th Street station, in the 300 block of West 47th Street, and found the man with face injuries.</p><p>He was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center and listed in fair condition, police said. </p><p>Area 1 detectives were investigating.</p>
articles.author: Sun-Times Wire

articles.title: CPD squad car collides with CTA bus in Ukrainian Village; 2 officers among 3 hospitalized
articles.summary: <p>Two officers were among three people hospitalized after a Chicago police squad car collided with a CTA bus early Friday in Ukrainian Village on the Northwest Side, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The officers were traveling south on Hoyne Avenue when they "came into contact" with the CTA bus going west on Chicago Avenue around 12:15 a.m., police said in a statement.</p><p>A passenger on the bus was taken to Stroger Hospital with a minor injury and was in good condition, police said. Two officers were hospitalized for observation and were in good condition.</p>
articles.author: Sun-Times Wire

articles.title: Should Chicago install roundabouts or more traffic circles? Here's what you told us
articles.summary: <p>We asked readers whether Chicago should be like the suburbs and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/02/06/roundabouts-traffic-chicago-suburbs-illinois-department-transportation" >install roundabouts</a> or <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/curious-city/2025/07/16/why-chicago-goes-round-and-round-with-traffic-circles" >more traffic circles</a>. Here's what you told us, lightly edited for clarity:</p><p>"Sadly, Chicago drivers are unable to navigate the traffic circles we have now, so adding more would make traffic more dangerous, if that's even possible."<br><i>— Tom Covell</i></p><p>"They'd never do it simply since it will eat into their red light cam money."<br><i>— Graham Stegall </i></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> streets. ... They can easily be implemented ... creating a safer environment for city residents!"<br><i>— Jaime Duque</i></p><p>"The learning curve is too big, and people will be flattened."<br><i>— Christine Bock</i></p><p>"I live alongside a roundabout in Lake County. ... Before the roundabout, traffic would back up for a quarter to half a mile while waiting to traverse the intersection during rush hours. Now, traffic of three to four cars may accumulate for seconds during the same time spans."<br><i>— Mike Lyne</i></p><p>"Drive the back streets of Rogers Park and get back to us. I'd focus on the streets between Pratt [Boulevard] and Devon [Avenue]. Oof. Oof. Oof."<br><i>— Suzanne Till</i></p><p>"[Roundabouts are] 80% safer while improving traffic flow and lowering emissions."<br><i>— Michael O'Leary</i></p><p>"Roundabouts could help with some of the multiway intersections like where Broadway meets Bryn Mawr and Ridge [avenues]."<br><i>— Michael Ross</i></p><p>"They're not really a solution that works with a lot of pedestrians or dense urban areas. They're for cars and work well where cars are all you expect to use an intersection."<br><i>— Chris Gray</i></p><p>"They are confusing. Without stop signs, you don't know where the driver is heading."<br><i>— Carol Kraines</i></p><p>"The Northwest Side already has some on neighborhood streets. No one uses them correctly, some driving completely straight over them. And they are too tight on the narrow streets."<br><i>— Brian Kuenstler</i></p><p>"Maybe at the six corners intersections. I don't know if there's enough room at other intersections."<br><i>— Ron Dobis</i></p><p>"It’s a grid for a reason."<br><i>— Kevan Davis</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/02/06/roundabouts-traffic-chicago-suburbs-illinois-department-transportation" >Roundabouts are increasingly popping up in Chicago’s suburbs, seen as an answer to traffic problems</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/curious-city/2025/07/16/why-chicago-goes-round-and-round-with-traffic-circles" >Why Chicago goes round and round with traffic circles</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>

articles.title: Chicago murals: Hundreds of paintings grace Oak Park's Green Line embankment — and more arrive yearly
articles.summary: <p>Colorful images of turtles, monsters, parrots, people and more cover the walls supporting the Green Line as it goes through the heart of Oak Park. About 230 are on either side of the embankment.</p><p>Since 2010, the Oak Park Area Arts Council has worked with village officials to commission 20 to 30 murals a year to be painted in the frames of concrete. They are found on aptly named North Boulevard and South Boulevard — on the north and south sides of the L tracks — from downtown Oak Park to the CTA Green Line Austin station.</p><p>“There’s still a lot more to do,” says Camille Wilson White, executive director of the Oak Park Area Arts Council. “We are proud of these murals. People love them.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-480000" name="image-480000"></a> Line CTA station in Oak Park.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>One of those people is a muralist who goes by the artist name Hink. They grew up in Oak Park looking at the murals and graduated from Oak Park River Forest High School. Hink added their own panel a few years ago. It features an abstract image of a pinkish-purple girl with flowers and creatures coming out of their head. It’s next to the Oak Park Green Line stop.</p><p>“All the things inside of you, these creatures or these flowers, everything is unique or beautiful to a person. That’s what that one’s about,” Hink says. “It was a super positive spin on loving yourself and embracing individuality and such. It was pretty cool to do that character in Oak Park.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-420000" name="module-420000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>How to get to the Oak Park murals</h3><br>To see the murals, Camille Wilson White recommends taking the CTA Green Line to the Austin stop and walking west on North Boulevard to South Humphrey Avenue. Duck through the viaduct and continue west on South Boulevard to North Harlem Avenue near downtown. Turn north toward North Boulevard and follow it east until it ends at North East Avenue. Or bring a bike and ride it, she says.</div> </div><p>Wilson White says the collection contains murals by dozens of artists who come from Oak Park, the greater Chicago area and across the United States. Some local artists have added their artwork multiple times. The artists are paid $1,500 for their work, and must bring their own paint. The village and arts council ensure the mural frame is primed and, when it is finished, the new artwork is covered with an anti-graffiti coating to protect it.</p><p>The selection process is competitive, Wilson White says. She doesn’t keep a list of those who have participated, and each year’s group is chosen anew from the submissions the village receives.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div Green Line last summer, near the intersection of Home Avenue and South Boulevard. It <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DM4Kd_FAEoB/" target="_blank" >features two children</a> seeking cover under a large, multi-colored goose of sorts, with two ghost-like features dropping down from above. She called her work a political piece and part of an ongoing body of work about generational trauma and seeking to protect our children — "a silent protest of how things are not safe for our kids." articles.title: Preckwinkle pide a la fiscal del Condado de Cook investigar a ICE por los tiroteos en el área de Chicago
articles.summary: <p>La presidenta de la Junta del Condado de Cook, Toni Preckwinkle, junto con más de la mitad de los comisionados del condado, ha llamado a la fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook, Eileen O’Neil Burke, a presentar cargos contra los agentes de inmigración federales involucrados en los tiroteos ocurridos el año pasado en el área de Chicago, aunque Burke dice que su oficina no tiene jurisdicción.</p><p>La declaración señala el tiroteo fatal de Silverio Villegas González, a quien los agentes de inmigración federales le dispararon en septiembre pasado en el suburbio de Franklin Park. La declaración también menciona el tiroteo que hirió a Marimar Martínez, quien sobrevivió después de que el año pasado un agente federal le disparó varias veces. Se pide a la fiscal del estado que investigue los “cargos disponibles” y el “uso excesivo de fuerza” en los tiroteos.</p><p>También señalan el uso desproporcionado de fuerza por parte de la policía contra residentes negros, y afirmaron que la justicia debe aplicarse de manera equitativa porque “ningún oficial y ninguna agencia está por encima de la ley”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-4f0000" name="module-4f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“La agenda de aplicación de la ley de inmigración de la administración de Trump ha producido un patrón claro y alarmante de violencia”, dice la declaración. “Apoyamos las investigaciones exhaustivas de la aplicación de la ley dondequiera que los agentes federales hayan violado la ley. Instamos a la fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook a actuar de manera rápida, proactiva y transparente para garantizar la rendición de cuentas y brindar justicia a las familias y comunidades que han sido perjudicadas”.</p><p>Esto ocurre poco más de una semana después de que los demócratas de la Cámara de Representantes de Illinois <a class="Link" href="https://ilhousedems.com/2026/02/03/elected-officials-call-on-cook-county-states-attorney-eileen-oneil-burke-to-investigate-the-fatal-shooting-of-silverio-villegas-gonzalez-and-the-near-fatal-shooting-of-marimar-martin/" target="_blank" >publicaran un llamado a la acción similar</a> dirigido a la oficina de la fiscal del estado.</p><p>La oficina de la fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook dijo que, aunque Burke está “profundamente preocupada” por el “comportamiento aberrante” de los agentes de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas a nivel nacional, su oficina “no realiza investigaciones independientes sobre conductas criminales y carece de jurisdicción sobre las agencias federales, excepto en circunstancias extremadamente limitadas”.</p><p>Burke destacó que su equipo ha redactado un protocolo sobre cómo los sistemas legales locales pueden responder a los agentes federales de manera “clara y basada en la ley”, el cual ha sido compartido con las fuerzas del orden locales y estatales, así como con otros fiscales para recibir retroalimentación.</p><p>“Estamos comprometidos a hacer todo lo posible, dentro de la ley, para apoyar y revisar las investigaciones sobre la aplicación de la ley en relación con esa conducta y enjuiciar cuando sea apropiado”, dijo la oficina de Burke en un comunicado.</p><p>Burke está entre los líderes locales que buscan maneras de responsabilizar a los agentes federales por herir y matar a residentes, las leyes federales que a menudo bloquean esas acciones, y la administración del presidente Donald Trump que ha impedido las investigaciones sobre el uso de la fuerza.</p><p>La semana pasada, Burke dijo a su personal que la reciente orden ejecutiva del alcalde Brandon Johnson —que ordena al Departamento de Policía de Chicago (CPD) documentar posibles delitos cometidos por agentes federales— es inapropiada y podría poner en peligro los enjuiciamientos penales.</p><p>Johnson <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/11/alcalde-johnson-dice-que-no-intenta-controlar-que-casos-de-abuso-de-ice-se-mandan-a-la-fiscal-del-estado" target="_blank" >defendió la medida</a> y dijo que intentaba ayudar a Burke al proporcionar evidencia para los enjuiciamientos de agentes federales, lo cual afirmó que ella había mencionado públicamente.</p><p>Es uno de los muchos esfuerzos que funcionarios estatales, del condado y locales han llevado a cabo en un intento de frenar a los agentes federales en el área de Chicago, aunque muchos han enfrentado incertidumbre legal, y expertos han puesto en duda una orden ejecutiva que Johnson firmó en agosto que <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/09/02/el-alcalde-johnson-firma-orden-ejecutiva-para-proteger-a-chicago-de-agentes-y-tropas-federales" target="_blank" >amenazaba con demandas contra agentes federales</a>.</p><p>En noviembre, el ahora exjuez presidente del Circuito del Condado de Cook, Timothy Evans, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/14/top-cook-county-judge-bars-civil-arrests-at-courthouses-citing-chilling-effect" target="_blank" >firmó una orden</a> que prohíbe los arrestos civiles, o aquellos sin una orden firmada por un juez, en las cortes del condado, aunque los funcionarios federales se comprometieron a violarla de todos modos.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
articles.author: Violet Miller

articles.title: Mistakes stall back pay for hundreds of Chicago firefighters and paramedics
articles.summary: <p>Hundreds of Chicago firefighters and paramedics are still waiting for four years’ worth of retroactive pay raises after Mayor Brandon Johnson’s administration fumbled the ball in distributing the checks.</p><p>Pat Cleary, president of the Chicago firefighters union, said the city’s failure to deliver back pay checks by the Dec. 30 deadline means the city must pay 4.5% annual interest on payments as high as $35,000.</p><p>The problem started when the city waited until mid-January — at least two weeks after the deadline — “to start cutting checks for anybody,” Cleary said. When checks were finally in the mail, the Johnson administration “realized that they screwed up and didn’t take out pension deductions.”</p><p>“Some of these people had 20 or 30 grand deposited into deferred [compensation] to get the tax savings and then they had it removed because the city screwed up the pension deductions. The city took that money back,” Cleary told the Chicago Sun-Times. “Other people had checks mailed to homes that they no longer live in. And the city is saying, `We’re not gonna cut another check until we get that first check back.’ Well, our members don’t live there. How can you get that check back? We don’t know where it is.”</p><p>A letter attached to the firefighters contract states that retroactive paychecks delivered after the Dec. 30 deadline must include interest at an annual rate of 4.5%.</p><p>“They’re saying they don’t owe us that interest. They’re not acknowledging a letter in our contract that states if you don’t pay us within 75 days, you owe us 4.5%-per-annum. They’re saying they don’t owe us that,” Cleary said. “You do. It’s in the contract. We filed a grievance, and we’re gonna take them to arbitration. We’re gonna win that. It’s a no-brainer.”</p><p>City Comptroller Michael Belsky said in a statement that he is "aware of the issue" involving retro pay and has been "working with the union to resolve it." He refused to elaborate, citing the union grievance and potential litigation that could come from it.</p><p></p><p>Cleary said there is no excuse for forcing firefighters and paramedics who waited four years for a new contract and the pay raises that were supposed to come with it to wait even longer for the lump-sum payments they are owed.</p><p>“It just shows the incompetence of the city. How do you forget to take out pension deductions? How is that possible? We went without a contract for four years,” he said. “This should have been ready to go right when we ratified the contract. All they should have done was press the button and say, 'Let’s go.'" </p><p>Ald. Matt O’Shea (19th), whose far Southwest Side ward is home to scores of firefighters and paramedics, called the payroll snafu “yet another example of the calamity that is this administration. They knew they had an agreement … months ago. They dragged this out. They didn’t communicate. When they finally cut the checks, how many mistakes were made, whether it’s an incorrect address or not taking proper dollars out.” </p><p>Ald. Nick Sposato (38th), a former Chicago firefighter, called the payroll snafu “disappointing” and “kind of sad.”</p><p>The Johnson administration was already under fire for borrowing $186 million to cover the retroactive paychecks after the City Council was told repeatedly that the money to resolve the firefighters contract was set aside in every one of Mayor Brandon Johnson’s three budgets.</p><p>“That’s why it’s very disappointing. Because you knew this was coming. You weren’t ambushed or anything. So you had to be prepared,” Sposato said. “Even if one person really needed the money — or they were putting it all in deferred [compensation], and now they can’t because it came in a different year — there’s a whole boatload of problems that could result from this.”</p><p>Cleary has characterized the six-year contract as a “status quo” agreement because it includes none of the union concessions that Johnson and his two predecessors talked about, but never pursued. </p><p>The contract includes pay raises of up to 20%, depending on the rate of inflation, and a $2,500 cash bonus to match the increases awarded to rank-and-file police officers.</p><p>On the day the City Council unanimously approved the agreement, Cleary argued that the three-year loan made it clear that Johnson slow-walked negotiations on a new firefighters contract to avoid paying the $186 million in back pay and to “spend that money elsewhere.”</p>
articles.author: Fran Spielman

articles.title: Is CPS superintendent search ‘on track’ or ‘stalled’? Depends who you ask on school board
articles.summary: <p>The president of the Chicago Board of Education says the board is on track to find a permanent superintendent by the start of next school year.</p><p>But not everyone seems to agree: six of the 21 board members said in a statement that the process has “stalled and major decisions have been put on hold.”</p><p>The letter, first made public by the Chicago Tribune and Chalkbeat, and later provided to the Chicago Sun-Times, was signed by elected members Jessica Biggs, Che "Rhymefest" Smith, Therese Boyle, Ellen Rosenfeld, Carlos A. Rivas, Jr. and Angel Gutierrez.</p><p>The group called the decision to part ways with the search firm hired to help find a new CEO “a sad development.” They accused Mayor Brandon Johnson “and his allies” of interfering in the search process and creating dysfunction that made it “impossible” for the firm to finish the job. They likened the situation to “sabotage.”</p><p>But the mayor’s office said that claim was “baseless and inaccurate” and that, so far, they have not been presented with any candidates. Board president Sean Harden, who was appointed by Johnson, called the statement divisive.</p><p>“We are supposed to be showing up and supporting students as trustees,” Harden said in an interview “They should ask themselves if [their] actions really are in the best interest of the district.”</p><p>This is the latest complication in a search process that began last spring shortly after the school board fired former CPS CEO Pedro Martinez without cause. The board initially set out to find a new leader by the end of December, and appeared to be on track when it narrowed its search down to a few finalists in November. But there was little movement after the names of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/11/05/cps-ceo-search-macquline-king" target="_blank" ><u>candidates were leaked</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/11/07/school-leaders-from-new-york-city-and-denver-finalists-cps-top-job-ceo-superintendent" target="_blank" ><u>one of them withdrew his name</u></a> from consideration.</p><p>The board now says it will “bring in the next leader for a full orientation and transition planning with CPS leadership” before the <a class="Link" href="https://www.cpsboe.org/supt-ceo-search" target="_blank" ><u>start of the next school year</u></a>.</p><p>Adding another layer of complication: All of this is happening as the school board governance is about to change. Currently, the board is a mix of 10 elected members and 11 appointed by Johnson, but it will move to a fully elected body after voters head to the polls later this year. The board is heading the hiring process for the district’s next leader after years of mayoral control.</p><p>The six board members who signed the statement called on the district to retain interim CEO Macquline King “through the 2027 school year so a fully-elected board can have a say in a permanent leader for the District.” King applied to the permanent position, but did not make the first cut of finalists.</p><p>Harden said the termination of the search firm’s contract is another step in the process and the board is on track to identify the next leader by next school year. He thanked the firm, Alma Advisory Group, for its efforts, which he said went beyond what they were initially asked to do.</p><p>“They offered extensive support, conducted intake screening of over 100 candidates, provided interview training to each board member based on best practices and participated in countless meetings,” Harden wrote in a statement.</p><p>Harden said the board stopped accepting applications for the superintendent role on Feb. 6 and will work closely with Alma over the next 30 days “to ensure a seamless transition into final interviews.”</p><p>It wasn’t immediately clear how many candidates are left or whether the district would release their names to the public.</p><p>“We’ve provided critical insights and support throughout this process, and we wish them well as they move forward into the next phase of their search,” a spokesperson for the firm wrote in a statement.</p>
articles.author: Sarah Karp | WBEZ

articles.title: Mayor Johnson vetoes hemp ban, as measure's sponsor, Ald. Quinn, throws in the towel
articles.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson on Friday vetoed a City Council-approved <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/01/21/chicago-hemp-ban-thc-beverages-topical-marty-quinn-brandon-johnson" >ban on hemp-derived products</a> to save Chicago's burgeoning hemp industry, and the chief sponsor of the ban said he won’t try for an override.</p><p>Southwest Side Ald. Marty Quinn’s decision to throw in the towel means that hemp-derived products can continue to be sold in Chicago — at least until a federal ban takes effect later this year.</p><p>The ban on most hemp products was aimed at keeping unregulated intoxicants out of reach from minors. Johnson used his first veto to kill a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/07/16/snap-curfew-veto-upheld-mayor-johnson-city-council-vote" >snap curfew ordinance</a> and made it stick by preventing a 34-vote override. His second veto will also stand.</p><p>Quinn (13th) muscled the ban through the City Council by a vote of 32 to 16. But he said he won’t pursue an override he knows he can’t win.</p><p>“I don’t have the votes… I’m at 32. I don’t have 34. That’s where it’s at. It’s not going to change,” said Quinn, who learned his vote-counting skills while serving as chief lieutenant for now convicted and imprisoned former Illinois House Speaker Michael Madigan, D-Chicago. “I still have the ban on the Southwest Side in the Midway [Airport] region and I would anticipate other alders doing the same thing in their wards” in addition to the seven wards where hemp products are already banned.</p><p>Quinn said the mayor will now have to live with the consequences of “placing business over the safety of kids.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5a0000" name="image-5a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490"
