data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A poster, which was set up by the Joint Civic Committee of Italian Americans, is displayed at Arrigo Park in the Little Italy neighborhood in 2021.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times (file)</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>She was made the patron saint of immigrants in 1950, four years after more than 100,000 people attended a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/11/11/18471565/at-the-shrine-of-first-u-s-saint-who-came-to-america-as-an-immigrant" target="_blank" >celebratory mass at Soldier Field</a> for her receiving sainthood. That recognition came three decades after her death from malaria at age 67 at Columbus Hospital in Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/11/11/18471565/at-the-shrine-of-first-u-s-saint-who-came-to-america-as-an-immigrant" target="_blank" >which she had founded</a> in 1905.</p><p>“When Mother Frances Xavier Cabrini came to Chicago in 1899, she didn’t just serve immigrant families, she built institutions that transformed lives,” said Mayor Brandon Johnson in a statement. “She founded schools, orphanages, and hospitals that cared for Italian immigrants facing hardship, and she ensured that resources flowed back into the neighborhoods that needed them most. Her work reflects Chicago at its best: a city that rises by lifting others. This monument at Arrigo Park will honor her enduring legacy and all of the communities who continue to shape our city.” </p><p>Former Mayor Lori Lightfoot <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2020/7/27/21340773/christopher-columbus-statues-grant-arrigo-park-removal-protests" target="_blank" >removed the park’s Columbus statue</a>, as well as two other Columbus statues in the city, in 2020 after they became targets of vandalism during the civic unrest following the murder of George Floyd in Minneapolis.</p><p>The Columbus statue in Arrigo Park has been loaned to the Joint Civic Committee of Italian Americans for display in an upcoming Chicago Museum of Italian Immigration on Taylor Street. The deal is part of a settlement between the group and the park district resolving <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2022/8/3/23290728/christopher-columbus-statues-chicago-grant-arrigo-park-protests-talian-american-lawsuit-lightfoot" target="_blank" >a long-running lawsuit</a> following the statue's removal.</p><p>The last remnants of the Columbus statue were <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2025/07/24/columbus-statue-pedestal-removal-grant-park-rotating-art" target="_blank" >removed from Grant Park in July</a>, and were expected to be replaced by rotating art installations to “celebrate Chicago’s diverse communities.”</p><p>“Mother Cabrini’s dedication to advancing equity, justice and inclusivity for the marginalized immigrant communities aligns with the Park District’s promise to make parks accessible to all,” Chicago Park District General Supt. and CEO Carlos Ramirez-Rosa said in a statement.</p>
articles.author: Violet Miller

articles.title: Chicago police officer ‘inadvertently’ fired Taser a year before fatally shooting his partner
articles.summary: <p>The Chicago cop who city officials say unintentionally shot and killed his partner during a foot pursuit last summer had previously told his bosses that he “inadvertently” fired his Taser after a high-speed car chase he failed to initially report a year earlier.</p><p>The car chase ended when the driver of a fleeing Jeep hit the train crossing at 89th Street so fast that it flew into the air and then crashed into six cars, according to records obtained by Illinois Answers Project and the Chicago Sun-Times. No one was seriously injured.</p><p>The crash and Officer Carlos Baker’s accidental firing of the Taser and failure to report the chase raise new questions about why he subsequently, in early 2025, was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/01/15/chicago-police-department-carlos-baker-krystal-rivera-gresham-district-jon-hein" target="_blank" >allowed to join a tactical team</a>, a competitive position, in the Gresham District on the South Side.</p><p>Chief of Patrol Jon Hein rejected Baker’s first application to the tactical team in the spring of 2024, citing Baker’s poor disciplinary record. The car crash happened seven weeks later, in June 2024. In January 2025, Baker applied a second time, again after Gresham District Cmdr. Michel Tate recommended him. This time, Hein approved it even though police brass knew about the additional infractions.</p><p>In June 2025, three months after joining the tactical team, Baker <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/05/chicago-police-officer-shot-cpd-crime-shooting" target="_blank" >shot and killed his partner</a>, Officer Krystal Rivera, as they chased a man into an apartment building.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-900000" name="image-900000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/59a6671/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/be6c73e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/212dcef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicago police Officer Carlos Baker." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/212dcef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7bf2ec5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/212dcef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago police Officer Carlos Baker.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Police Department</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A lawyer for Rivera’s family wouldn’t comment. Baker’s attorney didn’t respond to questions.</p><p>The car chase happened as Baker and his partner were patrolling the area of 83rd Street and Racine Avenue because of recent license-plate reader "hits identifying stolen vehicles in … the area.” They said they spotted a Jeep Grand Cherokee they recognized from burglaries and that had sped away from them earlier in the day, a police report shows.</p><p>Baker was driving, according to video footage from his body-worn camera and department records. The pursuit happened within a roughly one-square-mile residential area bounded by 79th and 89th streets and Halsted Street and Ashland Avenue.</p><p>The Jeep reached speeds of up to 90 miles an hour, and Baker’s police SUV hit 85 mph, records show. Baker and his partner didn't turn on their car’s emergency lights or in-car camera, so the crash wasn’t captured on video.</p><p>And the officers didn’t notify anyone that they were involved in a high-speed chase until after the crash, records show.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-140000" name="image-140000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fca99f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1612x905+0+200/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F11%2Fa90f34ee4901bf85489959cfd97e%2Fbaker-crash-diagram.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9458f9b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1612x905+0+200/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F11%2Fa90f34ee4901bf85489959cfd97e%2Fbaker-crash-diagram.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4735062/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1612x905+0+200/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F11%2Fa90f34ee4901bf85489959cfd97e%2Fbaker-crash-diagram.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A police diagram of the seven-vehicle crash near 89th Street and Racine Avenue on June 19, 2024. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4735062/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1612x905+0+200/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F11%2Fa90f34ee4901bf85489959cfd97e%2Fbaker-crash-diagram.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/356104a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1612x905+0+200/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F11%2Fa90f34ee4901bf85489959cfd97e%2Fbaker-crash-diagram.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4735062/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1612x905+0+200/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F11%2Fa90f34ee4901bf85489959cfd97e%2Fbaker-crash-diagram.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A police diagram of the seven-vehicle crash near 89th Street and Racine Avenue on June 19, 2024. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Police Department</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Footage from Baker’s body camera shows him opening the driver’s-side door as the SUV is still moving and car horns are blaring amid the wreckage. He and his partner then start chasing an 18-year-old man down a gangway, according to the video.</p><p>The footage shows Baker firing his Taser once at the teen after the younger man hopped a fence. The prongs “only [made] contact with” the man’s sweatshirt, though, and he kept running down the alley, according to the video and police records.</p><p>Baker then pulled the trigger of his Taser a second time — “inadvertently,” he wrote in a report — as he tried to scale the fence next to a chained gate while holding the Taser in his right hand.</p><p>Baker’s partner found the teenager sitting on the front steps of a house with wrecked cars surrounding Baker’s marked police vehicle.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/kDnZwZ9N6p8?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Chicago police officer ‘inadvertently’ fired Taser a year before fatally shooting his partner"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Baker said he made his first Taser trigger pull because he believed the man he was chasing was “armed,” according to a use-of-force report filled out after the chase. His second trigger pull, described as unintentional, was disclosed in the same report.</p><p>“Inadvertently, another trigger pull was activated on the Taser when I climbed the fence to locate offender,” Baker wrote.</p><p>About a week after the crash, a lieutenant filed a complaint against Baker, saying he never heard Baker and his partner report over the police radio that they were chasing the Jeep, as department policy requires. It doesn’t appear that any supervisor filed a complaint against Baker for mishandling the Taser, but his lieutenant and sergeant talked to him about “the importance of weapon retention,” police records show.</p><p>Baker told a sergeant he thought he followed the department’s balancing test for car chases, which requires officers to weigh the potential capture of criminals against the risk of hurting bystanders. The teenage driver was convicted of receiving or possessing a stolen vehicle and was sentenced to probation.</p><p>The city of Chicago has paid more than $100 million in recent years to pedestrians, drivers and passengers killed or <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2021/5/12/22425231/lori-lightfoot-chicago-police-vehicle-pursuit-policy-emails" target="_blank" >injured during police pursuits</a> — though the car chase policy <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2022/6/21/23177164/chicago-police-department-final-foot-pursuit-policy-after-2-fatal-shootings-by-officers" target="_blank" >has been overhauled</a>, often after those settlements.</p><p>An internal investigation into the car chase was closed on Jan. 15, 2025, with investigators agreeing with an assessment by the department’s Traffic Review Board that found both officers violated orders governing car chases and recommended discipline against Baker and his partner in September 2024.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a70000" name="image-a70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e3406ec/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1383x776+362+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2F20%2F5697c1bd4e9c9b844dad0489ce5c%2Fbaker-bia-chase-report.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ddfbc63/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1383x776+362+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2F20%2F5697c1bd4e9c9b844dad0489ce5c%2Fbaker-bia-chase-report.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6e2f5ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1383x776+362+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2F20%2F5697c1bd4e9c9b844dad0489ce5c%2Fbaker-bia-chase-report.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A portion of the Bureau of Internal Affairs report closing the investigation into violations by Officer Carolos Baker's and his partner of the Chicago Police Department's vehicle pursuit policy. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6e2f5ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1383x776+362+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2F20%2F5697c1bd4e9c9b844dad0489ce5c%2Fbaker-bia-chase-report.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/668e310/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1383x776+362+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2F20%2F5697c1bd4e9c9b844dad0489ce5c%2Fbaker-bia-chase-report.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6e2f5ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1383x776+362+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F05%2F20%2F5697c1bd4e9c9b844dad0489ce5c%2Fbaker-bia-chase-report.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A portion of the Bureau of Internal Affairs report closing the investigation into Officer Carlos Baker’s and his partner’s violations of the Chicago Police Department’s vehicle pursuit policy. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Police Department</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Baker was docked two days of pay over the crash, records show.</p><p>Eight days after the internal investigation was closed, he submitted his second application to the tactical team. He was brought onto the team in the spring.</p><p>Police officials have declined to answer questions about why Baker’s appointment was approved after he accrued additional disciplinary complaints between his two applications to join the tactical team and would not discuss the new revelations. Baker also had been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/01/15/chicago-police-department-carlos-baker-krystal-rivera-gresham-district-jon-hein" target="_blank" >found to have been insubordinate</a> and to have called his sergeant an expletive in the time between the two applications.</p><p>Tate, the district commander who nominated Baker to the team both times, has since been promoted to deputy chief. He has declined to comment on why he sponsored him.</p><p>Rivera’s shooting death remains under investigation. Officials have said little about the circumstances, aside from <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/05/chicago-police-officer-shot-cpd-crime-shooting" target="_blank" >calling it unintentional</a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f40000" name="image-f40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e72a198/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f65cc27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae1d218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Officer Krystal Rivera after he police academy graduation ceremony iinOctober2021 at Navy Pier. She was shot and killed by her partner, Officer Carlos Baker, during a foot pursuit in June 2025." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae1d218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9f7f3c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae1d218/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F36%2F0109ae2e8c536907fe9f8c44dde2%2Fmerlin-101780167.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Officer Krystal Rivera after the police academy graduation ceremony in October 2021 at Navy Pier. She was shot and killed by her partner, Officer Carlos Baker, during a foot pursuit in June 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Body-camera footage and police reports related to such an incident normally must be released within 60 days under the city’s video release policy, but a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/09/10/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-shooting-judge-barbara-lynette-dawkins" target="_blank" >judge has blocked the release</a> of those materials in this case.</p><p>Rivera’s family has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/12/10/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-wrongful-death-lawsuit" target="_blank" >sued the city</a>, saying Baker “left her to die” after shooting her. They said Rivera had threatened to expose their romantic relationship, which she had recently ended after learning Baker had a long-term, live-in girlfriend.</p><p>The family has said that Baker <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/12/11/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-wrongful-death-lawsuit" target="_blank" >could have been fired</a> long before the shooting for racking up disciplinary complaints when he was still a probationary officer.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/06/27/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-gresham-district-police-discipline-copa-civilian-office-police-accountability" target="_blank" >Chicago cop who inadvertently shot and killed his partner has a lengthy disciplinary record in a short career</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/08/15/chicago-police-department-carlos-baker-krystal-rivera-shooting" target="_blank" >Chicago cop who fatally shot partner Krystal Rivera is stripped of his police powers — but not for that</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/01/15/chicago-police-department-carlos-baker-krystal-rivera-gresham-district-jon-hein" target="_blank" >Chicago police brass rejected troubled cop’s move to elite unit before he fatally shot partner</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
articles.author: Tom Schuba

articles.title: How changes to the SALT deduction affect your taxes
articles.summary: <p>The so-called One, Big, Beautiful Bill Act brought sweeping changes to the country’s tax code, creating over $1 trillion in tax breaks while cutting funding to social safety net programs like Medicaid and the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program.</p><p>But one change stands out: A higher cap on the state and local tax deduction, which many Democratic lawmakers have pushed for since 2018.</p><p>For years, filers were able to deduct 100% of the state and local taxes that they paid. But in 2017, Republican lawmakers, under President Donald Trump’s first term, imposed a $10,000 limit on SALT deductions with the passage of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act.</p><p>In the years that followed, many elected officials opposed the $10,000 cap, which remained in place under former President Joe Biden.</p><p>“It never should have been put in place to begin with,” Gov. JB Pritzker said at a news conference last July. “I think raising it is a good thing for the state of Illinois.”</p><p>The SALT deduction changes will most likely help wealthier taxpayers in high-tax states like Illinois, especially if they have many expenses to itemize. Because of Illinois’ high property taxes, some people who claimed the standard deduction may now find it worthwhile to itemize.</p><p>Under the old cap, 14.7% of Illinois homeowners had property tax bills that exceeded $10,000. In the Chicago metro area, that figure jumped to 21.8%, according to Realtor.com. Rising reassessments have made bills even higher, particularly on the South and West sides.</p><p>Here’s what you need to know about how the SALT deduction could affect your federal tax return.</p><h3>Defining the SALT deduction</h3><p>The SALT deduction helps prevent double taxation — ensuring filers aren’t paying federal tax on income already taxed by state and local governments, which helps support services like schools, roads and public safety. The deduction is only available to filers who itemize.</p><p>The tax break includes property taxes plus local and state income taxes, or state and local sales taxes. Filers have to choose between deducting income taxes or sales taxes; it can’t be both.</p><p>Trump’s new law increased the cap from $10,000 to $40,000 for most filers with up to $500,000 in household income.</p><p>The cap will increase annually by 1% through 2029, then fall back to $10,000 for the 2030 tax year. When a filer’s modified adjusted gross income reaches $500,000, the cap begins to phase out, reducing their deduction by 30% of their excess MAGI (modified adjusted gross income). But the cap doesn’t fall below, so filers who are fully phased out can still deduct up to $10,000.</p><h3>Changes under Trump’s terms</h3><p>During Trump's first administration, the SALT deduction raised more revenue to offset large tax cuts, placing greater costs on taxpayers in high-tax Democratic-leaning states such as California, New York and New Jersey.</p><p>“The tax cuts that Trump and the congressional Republicans wanted to put in place came with a price tag. … It was a way to essentially raise taxes on certain high net worth individuals by taking away or limiting a previous ability that they had to pay less in taxes,” said Justin Marlowe, municipal finance professor at the University of Chicago.</p><p>While the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act created a SALT cap, it nearly doubled the standard deduction, and about 30 million more people across the country opted to take the standard deduction rather than itemize, according to the Bipartisan Policy Center. Between 2017 and 2018, the standard deduction rose from $6,500 to $12,000 for single filers, from $13,000 to $24,000 for married filing jointly and from $9,550 to $18,000 for head of household.</p><p>The One, Big, Beautiful Bill Act, signed in July 2025, made many tax changes from the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act permanent, while introducing new tax breaks like "no tax on tips" and a senior deduction of up to $6,000 for those 65 and older.</p><h3>Those most likely to benefit</h3><p>The previous $10,000 cap made the SALT deduction far less relevant for most taxpayers, said Nikhita Airi, research associate at the Tax Policy Center.</p><p>The center estimates about 10% of taxpayers nationwide will benefit from it in 2026.</p><p>Middle- and high-income households are most likely to benefit, particularly in high-tax states such as California, New Jersey and New York, where residents pay more to finance state and local services.</p><p>Joel Berner, economist for Realtor.com, said the higher cap will make a big difference for some homeowners, especially in high-tax areas.</p><p>Illinois has some of the highest property taxes in the country, according to the Tax Foundation. In Cook County, the median property tax bill was $6,053, with median bills ranging from $6,000 to $8,000 across counties in northeastern Illinois.</p><p>“A lot of people pay high property taxes in the city and all over the suburbs, so this will allow more people to take advantage [and] get a higher deduction,” said Traci Wall, SALT manager at accounting firm RSM.</p><p>For some, the previous cap “didn’t even cover all their property taxes, and they lost out on the state income tax deduction,” she said.</p><p>Studies have indicated that raising the SALT cap could increase homeownership rates and lead people to spend more on property purchases or renovations, thereby increasing property values. Berner said he believes it will boost home values in Chicago and Illinois.</p><p>“When you’re not paying the same amount of property taxes, those savings get capitalized into the value of the home. People with more money in their pockets may be more willing to move. People who’ve been sitting on artificially low tax appraisal values and don’t want to give that up might now be more willing to move, so we anticipate a little more inventory for sale, slightly higher prices and more activity,” Berner said.</p><p>But it could have mixed effects on housing affordability, as higher-income taxpayers may now be less interested in moving, Marlowe said.</p><p>“It becomes a concern because you’ve given people who own property an additional incentive to stay in their properties and invest and drive property values up, and that certainly is not going to help the affordability problem in a lot of communities,” he said.</p><p>Whether SALT deductions encourage people to move to higher property tax jurisdictions is unclear, he added.</p><h3>Itemize vs. the standard deduction</h3><p>Many take the standard deduction because it’s easier, requiring less work to document every expense.</p><p>For the 2025 tax year, the standard deduction increased to $15,750 for singles, $23,625 for heads of household and $31,500 for joint households.</p><p>But recent changes may make itemizing more appealing to households facing a higher property tax burden, said Mark Mirsky, certified public accountant and tax partner at Aprio.</p><p>In addition to the SALT deduction, Illinois taxpayers can deduct their home’s mortgage interest, some medical expenses, charitable contributions and other expenses allowed by the IRS.</p><p>Since the president's tax and spending bill passed before the IRS updated its federal tax withholding tables, many tax filers will likely receive bigger refunds than usual this year, Mirsky said. But whether individual taxpayers actually benefit from the new SALT cap depends on how much they can itemize beyond the standard deduction, he said.</p><p>“I think that many taxpayers in their heads are thinking, ‘I’m paying less tax, given the new bill,’ but it’s clearly situational," he said. "Refunds just mean you had more taken out of a check or that you paid the government more than you should have in advance.”</p><h3>Things to consider when taking the SALT deduction</h3><p>If you’d like to claim the SALT deduction, Wall said it’s time to start documenting everything, from charitable contributions to mortgage interest and property tax statements.</p><p>“If your income taxes [and] property taxes are going to put you over [the standard deduction] or near it, you may want to consider some additional charitable giving [and] getting that in a particular tax year,” she said.</p><p>If all of those combined don’t get you to the standard deduction, don’t itemize.</p><p>The SALT deduction also only benefits households with incomes below $500,000. Those above the income threshold may want to lower their modified adjusted gross income by contributing to retirement or health care savings accounts or documenting capital losses, Mirsky said.</p><p>Charitable giving will not reduce your adjusted gross income, but will increase your total itemized deductions and potentially provide a greater tax benefit for your giving.</p><p>Michael Thatcher, CEO of Charity Navigator, is seeing more donors bunch multiple years’ worth of charitable contributions into a single year to increase their itemized deductions, while taking the standard deduction in other years.</p><p>Bunching charitable donations rather than spreading them over multiple years allows filers to deduct the most in the long run.</p><p>Since 2022, many Illinoisans who own flow-through entities, such as partnerships or S corporations, have used the Pass-through Entity Tax to pay their state income taxes at the entity level while deducting them on their federal return, allowing them to deduct income above the cap. With the $40,000 SALT cap, Mirsky recommends speaking with a tax adviser to determine whether to continue this strategy.</p>
articles.author: Reema Saleh

articles.title: Big money expected to flow in November’s historic Chicago school board race
articles.summary: <p>After a summer of tense budget debates, a handful of Chicago school board members that blocked the district from borrowing to erase a deficit got a little gift from a man with big pockets.</p><p>This fall, billionaire Michael Sacks contributed the legal limit of $7,300 to seven incumbent board members’ campaign funds. It was part of a donation spree in which Sacks also gave $400 to many City Council members who went against Mayor Brandon Johnson to pass an alternative city budget.</p><p>The business executive known for giving big to former Mayor Rahm Emanuel’s campaigns is gearing up for more. Through a spokesperson, Sacks says “he will continue to proudly support independent<i> </i>CPS Board candidates who make decisions in the best interest of CPS school children and their families.” Sacks is also a major donor to a new super PAC called the Common Ground Collective, which plans to back certain school board candidates.</p><p>So far, the SEIU Illinois Council PAC is one of the few other donors that has given to multiple school board members in the past six months. The union’s political action committee, which can contribute more than individuals, gave $10,000 to five of the same incumbents as Sacks.</p><p>As Chicago heads into a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/12/08/few-residents-know-chicago-will-have-a-fully-elected-school-board-poll-finds" target="_blank" ><u>monumental school board election this fall</u></a>, these early donations may offer a glimpse at some of the race’s potential big players and the issues that motivate them.</p><p>Many expect spending on this election cycle to exceed what was spent on Chicago’s first-ever school board race in 2024. That topped $13 million <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/chicago/2025/01/27/ctu-incs-spent-millions-on-school-board-races/" target="_blank" ><u>according to an analysis by Chalkbeat Chicago</u></a>, but involved just 10 seats. Now all 21 seats are up for grabs, and the at-large board president will have to run a citywide campaign.</p><p>“We are gearing up to another eight-figures race,” said Hal Woods, a chief of policy at the parent advocacy organization Kids First Chicago.</p><p>So far, the only person to announce a run for school board president, <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/chicago/2025/10/15/sendhil-revuluri-running-for-chicago-school-board-president/" target="_blank" ><u>Sendhil Revuluri</u></a>, has raised $190,000. The former school board member donated $68,000 to himself, and the rest has come from individuals, including the founder of dating app OKCupid and his wife, Sam and Jessica Yagan.</p><p>Among the incumbent board members, Carlos Rivas has the most money, with about $40,000. Rivas represents the Southwest Side and was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/chicago-school-board/2024/10/22/cps-school-board-the-race-in-the-near-northwest-sides-district-3" target="_blank" ><u>well supported by pro-charter school forces in the last election</u></a>.</p><p>Woods says he thinks labor-backed and pro-charter school factions will battle like they did in 2024, but some new entities also will play a role, like the Common Ground Collective. The business community has come out strongly against Mayor Johnson and his allies on the school board, painting them as financially irresponsible for wanting to spend, tax and borrow rather than cut.</p><p>At the same time, the Chicago Teachers Union and SEIU-Local 73 may not get behind the same candidates, as they did in the first election. The labor groups that represent the most staff in Chicago Public Schools have had some public conflicts, though SEIU’s president points out they still have a lot of aligned interests.</p><h3><b>Big money lining up for school board race</b></h3><p>In the 2024 school board election, two political committees bolstered by millionaires and billionaires actively contributed. Both the INCS Action Committee, affiliated with the Illinois Network of Charter Schools, and a new entity, The Urban Center, were supportive of charter schools and wanted to limit the power of the Chicago Teachers Union, which opposes the private management of public schools.</p><p>Both groups intend to get involved this year, as do two other groups with PACs and super PACs supported by super-wealthy donors. Already, INCS’ super PAC has $3.5 million on hand — more than any other committee.</p><p>All these groups may be better prepared this time around. In the 2024 race, the state legislature did not <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2024/03/07/state-lawmakers-approve-elected-school-board-what-comes-next" target="_blank" ><u>approve a district map until March</u></a>, giving candidates little time to mount a campaign by November.</p><p>Organizations now have had more time to find potential candidates, Woods says, and they’re also teaming up. The leaders of INCS and The Urban Center, for example, say they have been identifying potential candidates and are exploring ways to partner.</p><p>Andrew Broy, who runs INCS, the advocacy organization for charter schools, says they’ve raised a lot of money because several elections are coming up, including races for Congress and Chicago’s mayor, in addition to the school board.</p><p>Broy says he thinks big-money donors are ready to pony up for the board race for reasons beyond their support for charter schools. INCS’ political action committees favor publicly funded, privately run charters, but are also interested in school accountability and other public education issues, he says.</p><p>“There's a consensus in the city, among many people, that the current trajectory of the board has not been good for students, and so there's interest to change,” he says.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5d0000" name="image-5d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1f600a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6603x3706+0+349/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F12%2F00d22fb14297a4bdd80bd0b74b22%2Fcpsrally-092524-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a01f55/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6603x3706+0+349/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F12%2F00d22fb14297a4bdd80bd0b74b22%2Fcpsrally-092524-01.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/206265d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6603x3706+0+349/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F12%2F00d22fb14297a4bdd80bd0b74b22%2Fcpsrally-092524-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A man wearing glasses speaks into a row of microphones surrounded by other people holding umbrellas and the flag of Mexico." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/206265d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6603x3706+0+349/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F12%2F00d22fb14297a4bdd80bd0b74b22%2Fcpsrally-092524-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/74a2753/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6603x3706+0+349/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F12%2F00d22fb14297a4bdd80bd0b74b22%2Fcpsrally-092524-01.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/206265d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6603x3706+0+349/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F99%2F12%2F00d22fb14297a4bdd80bd0b74b22%2Fcpsrally-092524-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Juan Rangel, CEO of The Urban Center, spoke in support of CPS CEO Pedro Martinez at a news conference in September 2024. His group plans to be active in this year’s historic Chicago school board race.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Urban Center’s political groups haven’t raised much but CEO Juan Rangel says he has been vetting and recruiting candidates. His group is strategizing about whether to be involved in all board races or to choose more competitive ones.</p><p>Rangel, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/1/30/18384640/the-watchdogs-uno-s-secret-spending-spree" target="_blank" ><u>a former charter school operator</u></a> who more recently ran a private school scholarship organization, joined with former CPS CEO and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2023/3/28/23660614/chicago-election-mayor-paul-vallas-city-club-house-on-fire-crime-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" ><u>recent mayoral candidate</u></a> Paul Vallas to form The Urban Center.</p><p>Rangel says he learned how hard it is for individual candidates to run a successful campaign, and he says his organization will be helpful in “empowering” them by creating fliers or running commercials to support them.</p><p>Two new organizations could add even more money to the school board races.</p><p>The Common Ground Collective is run by two former cabinet members of former CPS CEO Pedro Martinez. The school board dramatically ousted Martinez from his position in December 2024 after he pushed back on borrowing to keep the cash-strapped district afloat.</p><p>Like Sacks, the Common Ground Collective said in a statement it will get behind “independent Chicago School board candidates” who support “high-quality education and responsible budgeting.”</p><p>While Sacks’ spokesperson says the Common Ground Collective is supported by many in the business community, Sacks has donated more than half of the $1 million the organization has raised.</p><p>One Future Illinois is also looking to get involved. The group’s chairman is investment banker Steve Koch, who served as deputy mayor under Emanuel. In a text message, the president of One Future Illinois’ PAC, Michael Ruemmler, said the group will be watching the school board races closely and plans to be engaged. As of late December, the PAC had raised $89,000.</p><p>“A quality education system is fundamental to having a thriving, growing city and that’s what we are devoted to building,” he wrote.</p><h3><b>Labor unions also likely to play prominent role in race</b></h3><p>These big-money committees may hope to find an unexpected ally in the SEIU’s political committee. SEIU is seen as a progressive union that has backed many of the same candidates as the CTU, but they’ve had public fights over who should represent <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2024/12/03/chicago-teachers-union-narrows-contract-demands-in-negotiations-with-cps" target="_blank" ><u>aides for special education students</u></a> and hourly workers, such as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/chicago-school-board/2024/10/22/cps-school-board-the-race-in-the-near-northwest-sides-district-3" target="_blank" ><u>tutors or recess monitors</u></a>.</p><p>SEIU Local 73 President Dian Palmer says not to make too much of the $10,000 that the union recently gave to five board members who also got donations from Sacks. Palmer says these incumbents had fundraisers that she and other SEIU leaders wanted to support.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-be0000" name="image-be0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0944429/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+226/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc1%2F7d%2Fafafade840859abfa9f51c8c9598%2Fcpsboard-062725-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a81f43/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+226/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc1%2F7d%2Fafafade840859abfa9f51c8c9598%2Fcpsboard-062725-19.jpg data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>SEIU Local 73 President Dian Palmer says her union and the Chicago Teachers Union have a lot of common interests in the upcoming school board race.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>And these donations were separate from the union’s endorsement process, which involves a committee and will focus on finding candidates that are “supportive of membership and understand our issues,” Palmer said.</p><p>Palmer says she thinks the SEIU Local 73 and the CTU are looking for many of the same things.</p><p>“We want good schools and for students to have the best education, but we also want our members to have a good work life,” she said.</p><p>Hilario Dominguez, a political director for the CTU, also downplays the differences between SEIU and CTU. He says the big fight is against Sacks, other super-wealthy donors and pro-charter forces.</p><p>“It's going to be a tough battle for the future of our schools and our members are going to do everything we can," he said.</p><p>The CTU’s PACs have about $600,000, but they get replenished regularly through union dues. The CTU also has related political committees, including United Working Families, which has about $200,000 on hand.</p><p>Dominguez says while the union has been encouraging certain people to run, the CTU will not donate any money until after the union goes through an endorsement process.</p><p>Regardless of how much they raise, he says the CTU-backed candidates will have an army of members behind them, who will “get the word out.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-dc0000" name="html-embed-module-dc0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-table" data-src="visualisation/27702369"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/27702369/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="table visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p></p>

articles.title: New records reveal more on cop who killed partner
articles.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: Carlos Baker, the Chicago cop who officials say unintentionally shot and killed his partner, failed to report a previous pursuit that ended with him "inadvertently" firing a Taser.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: Gov. JB Pritzker's State of the State address, what's next for the Lincoln Yards site and more news you need to know.</p><p>📧 <b>Subscribe</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletter-hub.suntimes.com/" class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Police Department</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-t/" target="_blank" >Chicago police officer ‘inadvertently’ fired Taser a year before fatally shooting partner</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-i/" target="_blank" >Peter Nickeas</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-d/" target="_blank" >Casey Toner</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-h/" target="_blank" >Tom Schuba</a></h5><p><b>Unreported chase</b>: Carlos Baker, the Chicago cop who city officials say unintentionally shot and killed his partner during a foot pursuit last summer, previously told his bosses he "inadvertently" fired his Taser after a high-speed car chase that he failed to initially report in June 2024. In that incident, a fleeing Jeep hit a train crossing at 89th Street, flew into the air and crashed into six cars. Baker then chased the driver on foot and fired the Taser. </p><p><b>At issue</b>: The crash, Baker’s accidental firing of the Taser and his failure to report the chase raise new questions about why, in early 2025, Baker was <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-k/" target="_blank" >allowed to join a tactical team</a> in the Gresham District on the South Side.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Chief of Patrol Jon Hein rejected Baker’s first application to the team in spring 2024, citing a poor disciplinary record. The car crash happened seven weeks later. In January 2025, Baker applied a second time, again after Gresham District Cmdr. Michel Tate recommended him. This time, Hein approved, even though police brass knew about additional infractions. In June 2025, three months after joining the tactical team, Baker <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-u/" target="_blank" >shot and killed his partner</a>, Officer Krystal Rivera, as they chased a man into a building.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>SPRINGFIELD ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div JB Pritzker addresses the Illinois General Assembly.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mitchell Armentrout/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-b/" target="_blank" >Gov. Pritzker’s $56B budget proposal includes new tax on social media companies</a></h3><p></p><h5>Reporting by <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-n/" target="_blank" >Mitchell Armentrout</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-p/" target="_blank" >Matt Trunfio</a></h5><p><b>State of the State</b>: During his annual State of the State address before the General Assembly at the Illinois State Capitol on Wednesday, Gov. JB Pritzker made the case for his $56 billion state budget proposal, which leans on a new tax on big social media companies to help weather gloomy economic straits. </p><p><b>His agenda</b>: Pritzker outlined other highlights of his spring legislative agenda, including a push to cut red tape on local zoning laws to allow for more duplexes and granny flats to increase housing supply and lower prices. He’ll also advocate for a ban on cellphones for students in schools, more social media privacy protections for children, a two-year pause on new data center tax credits, and a crackdown on junk fees on tickets and other online purchases added at checkout.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Pritzker’s budget address didn’t approach the fiery oratory he unleashed last year when he likened the second Trump administration to the early days of Nazi Germany. Nor is he rocking the boat much with the eighth spending proposal of his tenure, a mostly status quo budget that allows Illinois Democrats to highlight cost-of-living concerns in a midterm election year as Pritzker seeks a rare third term and mulls a 2028 presidential run.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-x/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>CITY COUNCIL RECAP 🏛️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" Jesse Jackson "with action," and urged Chicagoans to do just that to honor "one of the most consequential civil rights leaders of our time."</p><p><b>New project OKd</b>: The Council <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-f/" target="_blank" >gave final zoning approval Wednesday to Foundry Park, JDL Development’s new, scaled-back vision</a> for North Side industrial land along the Chicago River that once was supposed to be home to the Lincoln Yards megaproject. Still to be decided is how much support the project gets from tax increment financing.</p><p><b>Hemp ban falters</b>: Alderpersons were sharply divided as <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-z/" target="_blank" >they upheld Johnson’s veto of a ban on hemp-derived products</a>, despite warnings that the unregulated products are endangering children. The decision means hemp-derived products can continue to be sold in Chicago — until a federal ban takes effect later this year.</p><p><b>Parking scofflaw plan rejected</b>: Additionally, the Council <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-v/" target="_blank" >rejected a plan that would have allowed Chicagoans to use their cellphones to provide recorded evidence</a> of bus, bike lane and crosswalk parking violations. </p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-e/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><b>What is your hope for the </b><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-s/" target="_blank" ><b>new Foundry Park project</b></a><b>?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Respond to this newsletter</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). Jesse Jackson’s family vows to continue fight for justice, equality</a></h3><p></p><h5>Reporting by <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-jy/" target="_blank" >Cindy Hernandez</a></h5><p><b>Family remembers</b>: Though Rev. Jesse Jackson was widely recognized as a civil rights icon, first and foremost he was a parent who "took fatherhood very seriously" and loved his family. That's how his children memorialized him Wednesday, sharing emotional tributes during a news conference outside the reverend’s South Shore home.</p><p><b>Funeral plans</b>: Family have scheduled Jackson’s funeral arrangements for next week. He will lie in state Feb. 25-26 at Rainbow PUSH’s Kenwood headquarters, 930 E. 50th St. A "People’s Celebration" will be held 10 a.m. Feb. 27 at House of Hope, 752 E. 114th St. Homegoing services are scheduled for 10 a.m. Feb. 28 at Rainbow PUSH; doors open 9 a.m.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-jj/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>RELIGION ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7f0000" name="image-7f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4a9d0b4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6328x3551+0+334/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe0%2F64%2F328f741944dfaecc2006120be3fd%2Framadan-260219-013.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0dda989/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6328x3551+0+334/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe0%2F64%2F328f741944dfaecc2006120be3fd%2Framadan-260219-013.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/970e36c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6328x3551+0+334/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe0%2F64%2F328f741944dfaecc2006120be3fd%2Framadan-260219-013.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Mohamad Taisir Albik leads prayer to mark the end of the first day of Ramadan fasting at the Downtown Islamic Center of Chicago on Wednesday, Feb. 18, 2026. href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-jt/" target="_blank" >Chicagoans lean on community, religion as Lent and Ramadan begin</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-ji/" target="_blank" >Elleiana Green</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-jd/" target="_blank" >Mohammad Samra</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-jh/" target="_blank" >Mary Norkol</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-jk/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a></h5><p><b>Ramadan starts</b>: The call to prayer echoed Wednesday night through a packed hall of the Downtown Islamic Center of Chicago as Muslims from across the area gathered to <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-ju/" target="_blank" >break their first fast of Ramadan</a>. Silence fell as dozens bowed their heads in prayer before breaking their fast with dates and water. Ramadan, which began Tuesday evening, is observed by Muslims worldwide as a month of prayer, spiritual reflection and fasting from sunrise to sunset.</p><p><b>Renewed focus</b>: After prayer, worshippers came together for a communal meal and a renewed focus on safety, turning to each other for support amid heightened immigration enforcement. "A lot of us are children of immigrants, or [are] immigrants," said Rana Jaber, a board member at the center. "We let our community know that this is a safe space for them." </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8a0000" name="image-8a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0e97f60/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6820x3828+0+361/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffe%2F06%2F6d9662ad437ea456f50c8dd63f21%2Fashwednesday-260219-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/732dbb2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6820x3828+0+361/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffe%2F06%2F6d9662ad437ea456f50c8dd63f21%2Fashwednesday-260219-02.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f9a5c73/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6820x3828+0+361/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffe%2F06%2F6d9662ad437ea456f50c8dd63f21%2Fashwednesday-260219-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Tom Dowling prays after receiving the imposition of ashes at Holy Name Cathedral on Ash Wednesday. Louis Cameli called for congregants to embrace repentance.</p><p><b>Broadview observances</b>: In suburban Broadview, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-tr/" target="_blank" >faith leaders active in opposing the Trump administration’s immigration practices entered the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement center</a> after months of being denied access. A federal judge's ruling allowed the group to provide communion and ashes for services. </p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-ty/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX 🏀🏒⚾</h4><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-79930514-0da1-11f1-880b-272831a6a6d3"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-tj/" target="_blank" ><b>Bulls press on</b></a>: As play continues tonight, the Bulls must approach the rest of the season with guarded optimism, writes Joe Cowley.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-tt/" target="_blank" ><b>Hawks back at it</b></a>: The Blackhawks debuted an adjusted defensive system in their first practice after the Olympic break.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-ti/" target="_blank" ><b>Pereira’s potential</b></a>: Everson Pereira, who the White Sox acquired from Tampa Bay, is showing potential early in spring camp.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-td/" target="_blank" ><b>New union boss</b></a>: Bruce Meyer was promoted to interim executive director of the baseball players’ association Wednesday. </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-ir/" target="_blank" >Lincoln Park Zoo welcomes Qi the red panda</a></h3><p></p><h5>Reporting by <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-iy/" target="_blank" >Elleiana Green</a></h5><p>Lincoln Park Zoo has welcomed its newest resident: Qi, a 7-year-old red panda brought to the zoo for a ‘species survival plan.’</p><p>Qi arrived from New York’s Prospect Park Zoo nearly a month ago and had been in quarantine until Friday, when he was placed in his permanent habitat at Lincoln Park Zoo.</p><p>Since his arrival, Qi has made himself right at home, said Cassy Kutilek, curator of large mammals and carnivores.</p><p>Qi, who was brought to Lincoln Park Zoo on a breeding recommendation, will be a new mate for Nisha, an 8-year-old female red panda who has been there several years. Kutilek says the arrival is perfectly timed, as red pandas typically breed between January and March.</p><p>On Tuesday, the pair met for the first time, and Kutilek says they seem to be compatible based on their genetics and behaviors.</p><p>"So far, it’s been a love connection," Kutilek said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-ij/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjrduiy-l-ii/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
