data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a9ba0e6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2012x1129+0+150/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2Fdc%2Fce88e478467f912d949a940487b3%2Fscreenshot-2026-02-23-at-11-22-42-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Among the recent campaign contributions to Illinois Secretary of State Alexi Giannoulias from Perry Mandera and businesses affiliated with him." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a9ba0e6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2012x1129+0+150/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2Fdc%2Fce88e478467f912d949a940487b3%2Fscreenshot-2026-02-23-at-11-22-42-am.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/29dc857/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2012x1129+0+150/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2Fdc%2Fce88e478467f912d949a940487b3%2Fscreenshot-2026-02-23-at-11-22-42-am.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a9ba0e6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2012x1129+0+150/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2Fdc%2Fce88e478467f912d949a940487b3%2Fscreenshot-2026-02-23-at-11-22-42-am.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Among the recent campaign contributions to Illinois Secretary of State Alexi Giannoulias from Perry Mandera and businesses affiliated with him.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Illinois State Board of Elections</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Mandera’s Custom Companies has also contributed money to a trade group representing the trucking industry that in turn has given campaign money to Giannoulias, whose agency licenses and regulates truckers in Illinois. The group previously gave to White’s campaign.</p><p>Mandera’s business portfolio has also included an interest in legalized marijuana, starting with medical weed and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cannabis/2019/12/3/20994147/perry-mandera-herbal-care-center-marijuana-recreational-weed-license" target="_blank" >eventually getting into recreational sales</a>.</p><p>Mandera and individuals and businesses affiliated with him have been prolific campaign givers over the years to a variety of other candidates and causes, from President Donald Trump, a Republican, to outgoing U.S. Rep. Jesus “Chuy” Garcia, an Illinois Democrat. Custom Companies gave more than $600,000 over the years to state and local campaigns, records show.</p><p>Mandera also has been a donor to charities that have included Loyola Academy, a Catholic high school in Wilmette.</p><p>Two decades ago while Giannoulias was running for Illinois treasurer, questions emerged about loans made by his family’s business, the now-shuttered Broadway Bank where he worked, to reputed crime figures.</p><p>Giannoulias initially defended one of the loans and the borrower — reputed mob figure Michael “Jaws” Giorango — in an interview with the Chicago Tribune. He later walked that back, telling the Chicago Sun-Times: “I should have taken more time to look at their background.”</p><p>"While we did our job as bankers to make sure that they were creditworthy borrowers and the value of the collateral was adequate, I probably should have looked into it more."</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/01/29/lobbyist-john-kelly-possible-chicago-mayor-bid-brandon-johnson-ballys-white-sox-katten-muchin-election" target="_blank" >Veteran lobbyist with clients who regularly intersect with City Hall weighs run for Chicago mayor</a> </li> </ul> </div> <div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/07/19/dupage-county-states-attorney-bob-berlin-campaign-fundraiser-donation-palumbo-john-kelly" target="_blank" >Banned contractors participate in campaign fundraiser for DuPage County state’s attorney</a> </li> </ul> </div> <div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/01/16/suburban-strip-club-blackjacks-gentlemens-club-south-elgin-kane-county-liquor-commission-video-gambling" target="_blank" >Suburban strip club with sordid past wins back liquor license over objections by some locals</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
articles.author: Robert Herguth

articles.title: Man and dog shot in West Pullman
articles.summary: <p>Police are looking for an assailant in a red car who opened fire, shooting a man and his dog Tuesday morning in the West Pullman neighborhood. </p><p>It happened about 7:25 a.m. in the 300 block of East 117th Street, according to police. </p><p>The victim, a 50-year-old male was outside with his dog when a gunman exited a red car, approached on foot and opened fire, hitting the man in the abdomen and buttocks and the dog in its shoulder, police said. </p><p>The man was taken to Advocate Christ Medical Center in Oak Lawn, where he was in good condition. The dog was also in good condition, police said. </p><p>The shooter returned to the rec car and sped away. </p><p>Area 2 detectives are investigating.<br></p>
articles.author: Sun-Times Wire

articles.title: Arrest in murder of Uber driver on West Side
articles.summary: <p>Chicago police have one person in custody Tuesday after the killing of 28-year-old Daniel L. Figueroa, who was delivering food when the attack happened on the city's West Side. </p><p>No charges have been announced but a Chicago police spokesperson confirmed the arrest of a "person of interest."</p><p>Figueroa, 28, was in the 5500 block of West Flournoy Street when <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/02/23/death-of-downers-grove-man-found-on-austin-street-probed-as-homicide-police-say" target="_blank" >he was attacked about 2:10 a.m. Monday, police said. </a></p><p>Figueroa, of suburban Westmont, was taken to Mount Sinai Hospital where he was pronounced dead at 2:46 a.m., according to the Cook County medical examiner's office. A cause and manner of his death was not immediately known. An autopsy was scheduled. </p><p>“We’re incredibly saddened by this tragic loss, and our thoughts are with their family and loved ones as they navigate this unimaginably difficult time," an emailed statement from Uber said. "We’ve reached out to police and will assist them however we can.”<br></p>
articles.author: Sun-Times Wire

articles.title: No matter where Bears end up, Chicago stakes claim for $630 million for Soldier Field overhaul
articles.summary: <p>Chicago is no longer part of the conversation to build the domed stadium needed to keep the Bears in Illinois and stave off a move to Indiana. <br>But the Chicago Park District wants a piece of the action — if the team scores the state help it needs to build a dome in Arlington Heights.</p><p>With or without the Bears, the Chicago Park District wants $630 million to renovate and refresh Soldier Field, and ease the transportation bottleneck that makes it difficult to get in and out of the Museum Campus.</p><p>Roughly $500 million would be spent on long-sought infrastructure improvements. The remaining $130 million would be used for replacement of stadium seats, an upgrade of Soldier Field’s concessions, restrooms, locker rooms, video and sound systems, and an overhaul of luxury skyboxes and club level lounges.</p><p>The new revamp would also include structural, mechanical, electrical, plumbing and exterior improvements, even as Chicago taxpayers remain saddled with $467 million in outstanding debt linked to the 2001 renovation of Soldier Field. </p><p>The wish list of improvements was devised by Legends Global, a company that manages nearly a dozen NFL stadiums and is now leading the development of Alicante Park, a new sports and entertainment complex being built outside the Valencia, Spain.</p><p>The overhaul would include fencing and façade work, as well as unspecified improvements to the parking lots surrounding Soldier Field.</p><p>The transportation improvements are the same as those floated by Mayor Brandon Johnson when he teamed up with Bears President Kevin Warren on a plan to build a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2024/04/23/bears-new-stadium-dome-lakefront-soldier-field" >domed lakefront stadium</a> on a parking lot adjacent to Soldier Field.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-340000" name="image-340000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2ee9de4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F2e%2F99cd06a24ca3a620ff0cb49ba0d3%2Fbearsstadium-042424-06.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a008c96/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F2e%2F99cd06a24ca3a620ff0cb49ba0d3%2Fbearsstadium-042424-06.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba2bb57/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F2e%2F99cd06a24ca3a620ff0cb49ba0d3%2Fbearsstadium-042424-06.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A rendering of a proposed new stadium for the Chicago Bears on the lakefront." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba2bb57/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F2e%2F99cd06a24ca3a620ff0cb49ba0d3%2Fbearsstadium-042424-06.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/57c0c26/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F2e%2F99cd06a24ca3a620ff0cb49ba0d3%2Fbearsstadium-042424-06.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba2bb57/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3840x2155+0+2/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F2e%2F99cd06a24ca3a620ff0cb49ba0d3%2Fbearsstadium-042424-06.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A rendering of a new stadium that was once proposed for the Bears on the lakefront.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided/Chicago Bears</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>That plan went nowhere in Springfield, prompting the Bears to shift their focus back to the Arlington International Racecourse site that the team purchased for $197.2 million and has already cleared for development.</p><p> The Bears are trying to work out an agreement with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2025/06/01/bears-stadium-bill-springfield" >Illinois General Assembly </a>on a so-called “mega-projects” bill that would save the team hundreds of millions of dollars by freezing property taxes and allowing the team to negotiate reduced “payments in lieu of property taxes” with suburban school districts.</p><p>The deal would also include up to $700 million in road, sewer and utility work needed to pave the way for construction of a domed stadium on the Arlington Heights site.</p><p>The Park District’s 11th hour play for a piece of stadium funding throws a potential monkey wrench into Bears stadium negotiations.</p><p>The Bears stadium bill can’t pass without substantial support from the General Assembly's Chicago lawmakers. If Chicago legislators demand a Soldier Field refresh in exchange for their votes, the heat would be on to find the money to appease Chicago.</p><h3>‘We want them to stay’</h3><p>Chicago Park District Superintendent Carlos Ramirez-Rosa said the $500 million needed to ease the notorious transportation bottleneck around Soldier Field could come from the “motor fuel tax lock box money,” the state’s road fund that has $3.5 billion that “needs to be spent down.”</p><p>The $130 million for a Soldier Field revamp could come, in part, from the penalty the Bears would pay if they terminate their lease, or if the Park District pursues the more aggressive legal theory that the Bears owe a 150% of the annual rent penalty from the moment they started looking for a new stadium.</p><p>“We believe the Bears belong here. We want them to stay in Chicago, but if that ship is sailed or sailing, then we’re prepared to plan for the future of Soldier Field to maintain it as a viable asset for large concerts, for large international and national sporting events that drives the local tourism economy, that puts heads in beds downtown and generates revenue for the city of Chicago, for the Park District,” Ramirez-Rosa said.</p><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e10000" name="image-e10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ab2a0c3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1920x1078+0+1/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Ffd%2Fa163ff2544cca35ed98baf736df3%2Fbears-stadium-rendering-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3b7378f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1920x1078+0+1/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Ffd%2Fa163ff2544cca35ed98baf736df3%2Fbears-stadium-rendering-01.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36db76a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1920x1078+0+1/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Ffd%2Fa163ff2544cca35ed98baf736df3%2Fbears-stadium-rendering-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Renderings of the proposed Chicago Bears stadium in Arlington Heights." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36db76a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1920x1078+0+1/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Ffd%2Fa163ff2544cca35ed98baf736df3%2Fbears-stadium-rendering-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d53d273/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1920x1078+0+1/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Ffd%2Fa163ff2544cca35ed98baf736df3%2Fbears-stadium-rendering-01.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36db76a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1920x1078+0+1/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Ffd%2Fa163ff2544cca35ed98baf736df3%2Fbears-stadium-rendering-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Renderings of the proposed Chicago Bears stadium in Arlington Heights.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided by Manica Architecture</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Ramirez-Rosa stressed that Soldier Field is “in great shape,” and remains the “largest non-tax revenue source” for the Chicago Park District — with $62.9 million in projected gross revenue that reduces pressure to raise property taxes and supports Park District programs citywide.</p><p>Last year alone, Soldier Field played host to thirteen concert nights, including five straight over Labor Day weekend. Beyonce concerts in May 2025 generated $85 million in hotel revenue and 98.5% occupancy in downtown hotels.</p><p>Those numbers are all the more reason to “continue to invest” in Chicago’s lakefront stadium, in part by making it “easier to get in and out” of Soldier Field, Ramirez-Rosa said.</p><p>“The plan that the Bears unveiled with the mayor would have fixed a lot of those issues," Ramirez-Rosa said. "It was a great plan that would have benefited the South Loop. It would have benefited the museum campus. It would have made it easier for people to get in and out of the museum campus and Soldier Field. Those issues don’t go away if the Bears go to Arlington Heights or Indiana.” </p><p>Senior mayoral adviser Jason Lee said the Johnson administration’s “number one focus is trying to make sure that Chicago remains in the mix to keep the Bears.”</p><p>“The partnership between the Park District and the Bears has been good for a number of reasons. We’d like to maintain it through some mechanism, and that’s what they’re having conversations in Springfield about,” Lee said. “This [Soldier Field Hail Mary] is just about saying, `We’re not going to lay down and die if it goes the other way.’ But our focus is preserving what we have.”</p><h3>Springfield waits for details</h3><p>Sources close to the Bears’ Springfield negotiations said talks were heading in a positive direction despite the frustration caused by the team’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/02/19/chicago-bears-stadium-hammond-indiana-move" >Hammond announcement </a>last week. A committee hearing was set for Thursday on the bill the Bears are seeking that would allow them negotiate payments in lieu of taxes (PILOT) to local taxing bodies, resulting in lower property tax liabilities.</p><p>But getting close to a deal behind closed doors is a long way away from locking up 60 votes in the Illinois House and 30 in the Senate, where Chicago Democrats across the Capitol remain loath to pave the Bears’ way out of town.</p><p>Some city legislators are still holding out hope the Bears will reconsider the old Michael Reese Hospital site that the team has dismissed as too narrow, but most acknowledge it’s down to Arlington Heights or Indiana. One legislator noted that Johnson has been “a complete nonentity” in stadium negotiations since the mayor’s ill-fated boosterism of the Bears proposal for a lakefront stadium. </p><p></p><p>Gov. JB Pritzker’s office wasn’t briefed on the park district funding plan. While he was optimistic about talks, Pritzker acknowledged “I really don't know whether they will choose to move out of the state.”</p><p>“We continue to have really positive discussions with the Chicago Bears, and I think you're going to see progress over time here," Pritzker said Tuesday. "I'm a Chicago Bears fan, believe very much that they ought to be based in Illinois, and I'm going to do everything I can without harming the taxpayers of the state of Illinois in the process of making sure that we can do everything we can to keep them in the state.”</p><p>State Sen. Bill Cunningham, a South Side Democrat and a key negotiator in Bears talks, said he had not heard from park district officials about the funding proposal and was “unaware of anyone who has.” Spokespeople for House Speaker Emanuel “Chris” Welch and Senate President Don Harmon said they also hadn’t reviewed the ideas.</p><p>State Sen. Robert Peters, whose district includes Soldier Field, said that without being briefed on the potential upgrades, he’d be on board with doing “whatever we need to do to make sure it remains one of the best concert venues in the country.”</p><p>“It’s a top-tier stadium for the likes of Beyonce and Taylor Swift. If you can fill it for eight to 10 more concerts, that’ll easily make up for the potential loss of Bears revenue,” Peters said. “There’s a fallacy that sports stadiums are this huge economic driver. The Bears are not that great of a driver of revenue for Soldier Field. They make a massive blackout period and take all the revenue from it.”</p><p>While Peters isn’t completely resigned to the Bears leaving the city, “we do have to have a conversation about what happens when the Bears convince someone to give them a handout,” he said.</p><p>“Indiana is willing to tax working-class people to pay for billionaires’ new stadium, and the [Arlington Heights] PILOT bill seems like it’s moving, so we're now at a point where we have to think realistically about options on what is next,” Peters said. “Infrastructure support is understandable, but I personally am never going to support tax giveaways to build a new stadium.”</p><p>Hoosier lawmakers are poised to pass legislation this week creating a new stadium finance authority and a slew of new taxes to pump upwards of $1 billion into a new stadium, along with $2 billion from the Bears.</p><p>Under the legislation that passed the Indiana House on Tuesday, the Bears would be able to buy it for a buck once 40-year bonds are paid off, backed by taxes on admissions, hotel stays, and food and beverage costs within a stadium district. In the meantime, the Bears would sign a 35-year lease that would allow the team to retain all revenues generated by the stadium.</p><p></p><p></p>
articles.author: Mitchell Armentrout

articles.title: Ald. Shirley Drewenski elected acting Harvey mayor
articles.summary: <p>The Harvey City Council elected an acting mayor one month after the death of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/30/harvey-mayor-christopher-j-clark-dead" target="_blank" >Mayor Christopher Clark</a>.</p><p>Ald. Shirley Drewenski (1st) was unanimously selected during Monday’s City Council meeting and was sworn in to serve the remainder of Clark’s term through next year.</p><p>Drewenski had previously been sworn in as mayor pro tempore after Clark’s death. Her appointment comes after two prior council votes ended in ties. A majority vote is required for the appointment.</p><p>Drewenski did not immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p>Clark died Jan. 30 and served as mayor for six years. The cause of his death has not been revealed.</p><p>A memorial service will be held for Clark on March 28, according to the city.<br></p>
articles.author: Cindy Hernandez

articles.title: ‘Abolish ICE’ entre los ganadores de concurso para nombrar camiones quitanieves de Chicago
articles.summary: <p>Uno de los quitanieves de Chicago está listo para apuntar directamente al Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE). Tras miles de votos, “Abolish ICE” (“Abolir ICE”) fue nombrado como uno de los ganadores en el concurso “Nombra un quitanieve” de la Municipalidad.</p><p>El lunes, el Departamento de Calles y Saneamiento anunció los seis nombres ganadores del cuarto concurso anual de nombres para quitanieves. Estos fueron seleccionados entre más de 13,300 propuestas y 39,000 votantes.</p><p>Los seis nombres de quitanieves que recibieron más votos también incluyeron “Stephen Coldbert”, “Pope Frío XIV”, “The Blizzard of Oz”, “Svencoolie” y “Caleb Chilliams”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-550000" name="module-550000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Chance the Scraper” recibió una mención honorífica después de terminar apenas .24% atrás de los seis primeros, según un comunicado de prensa de la ciudad.</p><p>“Quiero agradecer a la gente de Chicago por su creatividad incomparable, sentido del humor y orgullo cívico”, dijo el alcalde Brandon Johnson en un comunicado. “Estamos agradecidos e inspirados por la participación récord en el concurso de este año”.</p><p>Un número abrumador de habitantes de Chicago votó por “Abolish ICE” en las semanas previas al anuncio del lunes. Según una lista de todas las propuestas <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/01/snowplow-name-contest-streets-sanitation-ice" target="_blank" >proporcionada al Sun-Times</a>, el eslogan había sido ingresado aproximadamente 9,300 veces a finales de enero, representando aproximadamente el 70% de las propuestas. Otras propuestas, como “ICE out of Chicago” (“ICE fuera de Chicago”), también fueron nominadas.</p><p>Johnson también <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/ChicagosMayor/status/2001735382879113306" target="_blank" >sugirió</a> el nombre de “Abolish ICE” en redes sociales.</p><p>Después de que Chicago fue blanco de manera intensiva durante el Operativo Midway Blitz, la agresiva campaña de deportación de la administración de Trump el otoño pasado, relacionar el nombre de la quitanieve con el sentimiento anti-ICE se convirtió en una forma para que los habitantes de Chicago respondieran contra el Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS)</p><p>En enero, Johnson <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/01/26/pritzker-y-johnson-piden-abolir-a-ice-tras-el-segundo-tiroteo-fatal-por-parte-de-agentes-en-minneapolis" target="_blank" >pidió abolir ICE</a> después de que agentes federales dispararon y mataron al enfermero de veteranos Alex Pretti en Minneapolis.</p><p>El lunes, Lauren Bis, subsecretaria adjunta de DHS, que supervisa ICE, citó un aumento en los asaltos contra oficiales de policía en una declaración y dijo, “los ataques y la demonización de ICE son incorrectos”.</p><p>Y añadió, “En lugar de demonizar a nuestras fuerzas del orden, deberían agradecer a nuestros agentes por quitar amenazas a la seguridad pública de las calles”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
articles.author: Elleiana Green

articles.title: Fire hosting Tuesday groundbreaking ceremony for new stadium
articles.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/fire" >Fire</a> have waited a long time for an event like the one they’ll hold next week.<br> <br>The club confirmed it will host a groundbreaking ceremony Tuesday at the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/fire/2025/09/25/chicago-fire-city-council-approval-south-loop-stadium-the-78" >site of the new stadium at The 78.</a> Running from 2-4 p.m., the event’s speakers will include Mayor Brandon Johnson, Ald. Pat Dowell, Fire owner and chairman Joe Mansueto and team president Dave Baldwin. Current coach and director of football Gregg Berhalter will be in attendance, as will Fire alumni and other guests.<br> <br>The groundbreaking comes more than two years before the stadium will see Fire matches. The team expects the facility to be ready in time for the beginning of the 2028-29 season, the second season in which MLS will play from late summer to spring instead of using the spring to fall format it had followed since its inception.<br> <br>That shift signals a giant change for MLS, and the new stadium represents another transformation for the Fire under Mansueto. <br> <br>Mansueto, who is bankrolling the 22,000-seat stadium, moved the Fire from SeatGeek Stadium in Bridgeview back to Soldier Field for the 2020 season. The Fire also officially opened a new performance center last year, and in 2025 made the playoffs for the first time since 2017 while showing a willingness to spend on top players and staff.<br> <br>The stadium issue, however, is one that has plagued the club for much of its existence. <br> <br>The Fire were displaced during the Soldier Field renovations and spent all of 2002 and most of 2003 in Naperville, playing at a temporarily expanded small college football stadium. In 2006, the team went to the soccer-specific stadium in Bridgeview, where the team declined on the field and that coincided with the struggles to draw urban fans to the southwest suburb. <br> <br>In 2019, Mansueto and the Fire paid $65.5 million to alter their lease with Bridgeview and return to Soldier Field immediately. But as soon as that move was confirmed, speculation began about whether the Bears’ home would be the Fire’s actual long-term dwelling or just a stopover.<br> <br>On Tuesday, the Fire will take another step toward building that permanent residence.</p>
articles.author: Brian Sandalow

articles.title: Cook County contractor: Pappas abused, bullied executives in profane ‘tirades’
articles.summary: <p>The chief executive of a major Cook County contractor alleged longtime county Treasurer Maria Pappas repeatedly subjected company executives to bullying, threats and “obscenity-filled tirades,” according to a copy of a letter obtained by WBEZ.</p><p>For months, Pappas has blamed Texas-based Tyler Technologies for technological problems that delayed property tax revenue payments to school districts, local governments and other taxing bodies across the county.</p><p>But in a letter Monday to Pappas, Tyler CEO H. Lynn Moore Jr. bemoaned what he called the “scapegoating” of the company, which has been working for years to upgrade the county’s property tax system. Moore alleged that the treasurer has shown a “lack of accountability” for mistakes by her own office in her efforts to “deflect blame and divert attention to us.”</p><p>The focus of much of the letter, though, was on Pappas’ behavior toward Tyler team members, who Moore said had faced “attacks” from the treasurer in the past year. Moore said the situation finally prompted him to conclude that Tyler executives would stop taking calls from Pappas and would not meet with her in-person again.</p><p>Moore said Tyler had reported on their interactions with Pappas to other county officials, including an aide to county Board President Toni Preckwinkle, only to hear “other County leaders minimize your conduct as ‘that’s just Maria being Maria.’”</p><p>Moore told Pappas, “You have berated and bullied my earnest and hard-working team members without repercussions or reproach, and you have tried to sabotage this project, for the last time. Others at Cook County may have grown accustomed to your approach and consider this business as usual, but I most certainly do not.”</p><p>Moore sent copies of the 9-page letter to Preckwinkle’s office and to the county board.</p><p>On Tuesday, Pappas did not immediately respond to questions about the letter from WBEZ.</p><p>But Preckwinkle said in a statement she took the accusations seriously and would forward the letter to internal watchdogs for the county.</p><p>“The alleged language and conduct directed at Tyler Technologies by the treasurer are troubling and unacceptable,” Preckwinkle said. “Public officials are expected to lead with integrity, demonstrate respect, and uphold the highest ethical standards, fostering public confidence in government.”</p><p>Preckinkle also said, “This matter will be formally referred to the Department of Ethics and Human Rights for a thorough review and any investigation deemed necessary.”</p><p>On Tuesday, Moore also did not respond to requests for comment, but the company sent WBEZ a brief written statement: “We believe the letter fully and clearly communicates our position.”</p><p>In his letter to Pappas, Moore detailed what he said were a series of threats, insults and other verbal abuse by the treasurer directed at Tyler’s project manager in Cook County, another employee on the project and one of the company’s “most senior executives.”</p><p>In May 2025, Moore wrote, Pappas told the unnamed project manager for Cook County that another Tyler executive was “the biggest f–k-up I’ve ever seen” and she “screamed” that she would “make sure he never gets another motherf—-g contract in the world ever again.”</p><p>Moore alleged that at a meeting last spring Pappas, in front of “multiple witnesses,” berated Tyler employee Mark Hawkins: “You stood over him, thrust your finger in his face, and unleashed a torrent of insults and threats.”</p><p>Moore wrote that Pappas more recently directed her “vitriol” at another Tyler executive, Abby Diaz. During a phone call around Christmas, Moore alleged, Pappas “insulted in colorful and sometimes racial language President Preckwinkle, the Bureau of Technology and its leaders, and the County Clerk.” Preckwinkle and Clerk Monica Gordon are Black. Pappas is Greek-American.</p><p>Moore also accused Pappas of calling Diaz a “token Hispanic.”</p><p>“You characterized her perceived lack of pushback with local media as ‘a 13-year-old getting raped who just says thank you,’” Moore wrote to Pappas.</p><p>Moore said Pappas called Diaz last week and told her: “You’re a f—-g idiot. Can you speak English? Learn to speak English. I’m going to ruin you.”</p><p>Moore told Pappas her “mistreatment” of Diaz “exceeds the bounds of human decency,” and had led him to take action.</p><p>“County leadership has not been able to insulate Tyler team members from your mistreatment or misinformation,” Moore wrote. “They say they do not control you, and insinuate that this type of abuse is almost a rite of passage. I do not control you either, but I am taking steps to protect my team.”</p><p>He said any communication from Pappas would be forwarded to him and that only he and company lawyers would respond. Moore also said Tyler’s entire team on the Cook County project would be working from home this week “as I do not feel comfortable having them onsite in the days following your receipt of this letter.”</p><p>Moore also alleged that Pappas “claimed to have been ‘feeding’ information to President Preckwinkle’s primary opponent, Ald. Brendan Reilly (42nd), about the Tyler contract.”</p><p>Reilly is running to unseat Preckwinkle in the March 17 Democratic primary, and he has criticized the incumbent over the county’s problems with the computer upgrade project. Reilly has said he would consider <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/government-politics/elections/2026/02/16/cook-county-board-president-march-democratic-primary-toni-preckwinkle-brendan-reilly" target="_blank" ><u>terminating the county’s contract</u></a> with Tyler, if elected.</p><p>Reilly spokesman Phil Swibinski said Tuesday everything Reilly has said about the topic has been drawn from media reports and the campaign’s own review of the Tyler contract.</p><p>“There’s nothing that came from Maria Pappas whatsoever, ” Swibinski said.</p><p>He added that the letter from Moore “was just an attempt to deflect blame from Toni Preckwinkle. She’s the one who failed here.”</p><p>Pappas has been the Democratic treasurer since 1998. She is running unopposed in the primary for what would be her eighth term but has said she <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2025/11/18/cook-county-treasurer-maria-pappas-mayoral-campaign-chicago-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" ><u>intends to run for mayor</u></a> next year.</p><p>The delays in property tax payments have proven costly to all kinds of public units of government, especially Chicago Public Schools and suburban school districts. The technological problems were <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/12/19/property-tax-payment-delay-costs-chicago-public-schools-almost-a-quarter-million-dollars-a-day" target="_blank" ><u>costing CPS about $250,000 a day</u></a>, and Pappas said in December her office had taken <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/12/26/cook-county-property-tax-maria-pappas-tyler-technologies" target="_blank" ><u>“emergency steps”</u></a> to distribute some of the unpaid revenue, which was several months behind schedule and totaled $8 billion at one point.</p><p>“That’s simply unacceptable,” Pappas said in a statement at that time. “Local governments shouldn’t have to worry about their cash flow because a vendor after more than a decade of work has failed to deliver a working system.”</p><p>Injustice Watch and the Chicago Tribune reported last year that three Cook County and state of Illinois agencies have paid Tyler a total of <a class="Link" href="https://www.injusticewatch.org/judges/court-administration/2025/costs-triple-delays-mount-in-tyler-technologies-contracts/" target="_blank" ><u>about $250 million</u></a> for technology upgrade efforts that have suffered from lengthy delays and big cost overruns.</p><p><i>Dan Mihalopoulos is a reporter for WBEZ.</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/12/26/cook-county-property-tax-maria-pappas-tyler-technologies" >Cook County distributing $2.3 billion in property tax revenue amid long delay due to computer issues</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/12/19/property-tax-payment-delay-costs-chicago-public-schools-almost-a-quarter-million-dollars-a-day" >Property tax payment delay costs CPS almost a quarter million dollars a day</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2025/11/18/cook-county-treasurer-maria-pappas-mayoral-campaign-chicago-brandon-johnson" >Cook County Treasurer Maria Pappas says she’s running for mayor. She’ll leave it at that for now.</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
articles.author: Dan Mihalopoulos | WBEZ

articles.title: Gov. JB Pritzker announces $100 million redevelopment of Pullman's Hotel Florence
articles.summary: <p>Developers of Pullman’s historic Hotel Florence and the surrounding area announced plans to create a “mind-blowing entertainment experience” that is mixed with history and a new hub for local artists to showcase their talents — all headlined by a “world class” music venue.</p><p>The roughly $100 million revitalization project will transform the nearly 145-year-old hotel within the Pullman National Historic Park into a boutique hotel with new restaurants. The hotel’s annex will be rehabilitated and a concert hall will be added in the old Pullman Factory across the street.</p><p>Construction is set to begin in March 2027, with its completion expected in late 2028.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-290000" name="image-290000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/70ef890/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F79%2F85fda17442fa8551e9e87923a786%2Fflorence-260225-004.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cc07432/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F79%2F85fda17442fa8551e9e87923a786%2Fflorence-260225-004.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ee9ebf4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F79%2F85fda17442fa8551e9e87923a786%2Fflorence-260225-004.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Gov. JB Pritzker speaks from behind a podium about plans to redevelop the historic Hotel Florence in Pullman." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ee9ebf4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F79%2F85fda17442fa8551e9e87923a786%2Fflorence-260225-004.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8307b05/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F79%2F85fda17442fa8551e9e87923a786%2Fflorence-260225-004.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ee9ebf4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbe%2F79%2F85fda17442fa8551e9e87923a786%2Fflorence-260225-004.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gov. JB Pritzker speaks about plans to redevelop the historic Hotel Florence in Pullman.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We are breathing new life into this incredible site, while preserving a treasured part of Illinois history,” Gov. JB Pritzker said Tuesday at an event announcing the project.</p><p>The Illinois Department of Natural Resources provided $21 million in funding, making the project the largest public-private partnership for the IDNR. The department issued a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2025/01/31/wanted-a-team-to-return-the-bloom-to-pullmans-historic-hotel-florence" >Request for Solicitations last year</a>, searching for a development team to revitalize the state-owned hotel at 11111 S. Forrestville Ave. and its 1914 annex.</p><p>The state has partnered with the Chicago-based development group Celadon Partners. Scott Henry, CEO of Celadon, said his family grew up and worked in Pullman, making this project personal.</p><p>He pointed to several similarities in Chicago and the U.S. between 1881, when Hotel Florence opened, and now. Chicago was on the heels of a pandemic, American politics were “chaotic” (President James Garfield barely won his election in 1881 and was assassinated soon after) and Pullman factories had revolutionized the city’s economy and advanced the railway industry.</p><p>Henry sees similar advancements happening in the Far South Side with the development of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/technology/2025/10/09/french-pasqal-us-headquarters-south-works-quantum-computing-park" >Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park</a>, about a 15 minute drive north of the Pullman National Historic Site. The neighborhood also has an Amazon distribution center and the Pullman Community Center, 10355 S. Woodlawn Ave. The CTA Red Line expansion project also plans to extend into Pullman.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8b0000" name="image-8b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/15729ff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+625/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc3%2Fb8%2Fd31e253c4d3a81a7c6c8bacbc6d5%2Fflorence-260225-026a.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d1e42bb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+625/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc3%2Fb8%2Fd31e253c4d3a81a7c6c8bacbc6d5%2Fflorence-260225-026a.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2175b3a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+625/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc3%2Fb8%2Fd31e253c4d3a81a7c6c8bacbc6d5%2Fflorence-260225-026a.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The entryway of Hotel Florence" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2175b3a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+625/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc3%2Fb8%2Fd31e253c4d3a81a7c6c8bacbc6d5%2Fflorence-260225-026a.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36df2d8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+625/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc3%2Fb8%2Fd31e253c4d3a81a7c6c8bacbc6d5%2Fflorence-260225-026a.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2175b3a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+625/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc3%2Fb8%2Fd31e253c4d3a81a7c6c8bacbc6d5%2Fflorence-260225-026a.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The entryway of Hotel Florence</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Instead of our new guests coming here to buy Pullman cars, they’ll be treated to a mind-blowing entertainment experience in the former Pullman Factory,” Henry said. “I think of this area as becoming the beating heart of Pullman again. And if it’s going to be the beating heart of Pullman again, its soul certainly will be the Art of Culture.”</p><p>The arts organization Art of Culture is partnering with the developer to help bring talent and artists to the site's future music venue. Art of Culture CEO Heather Smith said the artistic hub only becomes Pullman’s “beating heart” because of the people it will attract.</p><p>“Art of Culture will really tap into our community and tap into what we know exists here today, and that’s the artistic energy of the South Side that sometimes needs platform and needs spaces to be highlighted,” Smith said.</p><p>She imagines a space buzzing with energy and art exhibitions, in addition to live music.</p><p>“We know it’s the vibration, the rhythm, the sounds that will come from Pullman, from this space that will be felt across the city,” Smith said.</p><p>Architecture firm Farr Associates is designing the project.</p><p>“We have a more intangible duty as architects, as designers, as historians to the translation of 150 years of cultural heritage and grassroots advocacy,” Mercedes Miley, president of Farr Associates and a Pullman resident, said.</p><p>Miley also requested residents to share with them any artifacts or photographs from past events at Hotel Florence.</p><p>“It’s a very well-documented site that as part of this project, we get to tell those stories again,” Miley said.</p><p>The hotel was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1969. It was saved from demolition when the Historic Pullman Foundation bought the hotel in 1975. The state purchased the building in 1991.</p><p>The hotel has been closed since 2000, with the exception of opening for occasional tours.</p>
articles.author: Kade Heather

articles.title: Ald. Hopkins prioritizes businesses over cyclists' safety
articles.summary: <p>The Sun-Times' recent <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/02/09/parking-scofflaws-ordinance-daniel-laspata-council-traffic-safety-committee-approval" >article</a> on the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/02/18/hemp-ban-brandon-johnson-veto-override-attempt-defeated-chicago-city-council" >rejected traffic safety</a> proposal ordinance that would have stepped-up parking enforcement in bike lanes and crosswalks sets up a false tension between the "business community" and people who want to travel around the city safely.</p><p>When 2nd Ward Ald. Brian Hopkins asks, "Am I supposed to close all of the restaurants?" he's presenting a false choice. I invite him to visit any city in Europe, where the streets with bike lanes (and even fully pedestrianized areas) are full of crowded, bustling restaurants.</p><p>I run three retail businesses in the city and am opening a fourth. We receive 40 to 50 deliveries a week and make our own deliveries. I bike to work every day. Safely getting to work without being killed or maimed by a driver is a higher priority for me than making the deliveries slightly more convenient.</p><p>We know parking in the bike lane is dangerous, as it forces cyclists unexpectedly into fast-moving car traffic. That's why we have laws against it. </p><p>We can argue whether the citizen enforcement idea is good. We can argue about whether the bike lanes should be better designed. We can argue about whether there should be more loading zones. But we should not — we cannot — say the lives of pedestrians and cyclists are just the cost of doing business.</p><p>Hopkins should worry less about getting an "angry phone call from Budweiser beer trucks and lobster companies" and worry more about getting angry calls from bereaved parents and spouses.</p><p><i>Steven Lucy, Hyde Park</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-840000" name="module-840000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Shame on U. of C. </h3><p>Universities in this country used to be leaders. They were the centers of protest against injustice and leaders in the fight for equality and fairness.</p><p>And now, the (formerly) esteemed University of Chicago has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/19/under-pressure-from-feds-university-of-chicago-cuts-ties-with-program-to-help-students-of-color-get-phds" >caved to the bullying and blackmail </a>of the Trump administration by severing its long and productive partnership with the PhD Project aimed at diversifying the pool of students who pursue postgraduate degrees.</p><p>This is depressing, disheartening and disappointing! Why is U. of C. allowing this administration to define an outstanding program as "discriminatory"? Clearly, it is not. It is righting a long-standing wrong by allowing outstanding students with limited resources to have a chance to fulfill their potential, which benefits our country in myriad ways. Maybe one of those students will cure cancer or develop a viable system of fusion energy.</p><p>When our institutions fail, autocracy thrives. You have failed your country, University of Chicago.</p><p><i>Carol Kraines, Deerfield</i></p><h3>Repeal of climate change finding destructive to economy </h3><p>In the real estate industry, everything starts with a solid foundation. Federal regulations are no different. For nearly two decades, the Environmental Protection Agency’s endangerment finding was the legal bedrock for limiting greenhouse gas emissions from cars and trucks. Recently, the Trump administration <a class="Link" href="https://e2.org/releases/epa-repeals-endangerment-finding-raises-costs-for-businesses-consumers/" target="_blank" >repealed the endangerment finding for greenhouse gas emissions and eliminated clean vehicle standards,</a> delivering a potentially fatal blow to the foundation of our economy.</p><p>This is not just a fight for automakers or environmental groups. In the U.S., <a class="Link" href="https://www.epa.gov/greenvehicles/fast-facts-transportation-greenhouse-gas-emissions" target="_blank" >vehicles are the leading source of greenhouse gas emissions</a>; emissions that are already reshaping the built environment and shrinking our wallets.</p><p>Hurricane Helene caused <a class="Link" href="https://thehill.com/homenews/state-watch/5523815-hurricane-helene-asheville-western-north-carolina-recovers/" target="_blank" >nearly $80 billion in damage</a> and killed more than 250 people. Last year’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.climatecentral.org/climate-matters/2025-in-review" target="_blank" >Los Angeles wildfires</a> destroyed more than 16,000 homes and businesses.</p><p>My company, Banner Real Estate Group, has had to halt construction due to previous wildfires in California. We’ve developed projects in Florida impacted by rising sea levels; wind and hail have delayed our work in Dallas; <a class="Link" href="https://www.extremeweatherwatch.com/cities/phoenix/yearly-days-of-110-degrees" target="_blank" >extreme heat</a> impedes attempts to erect apartment complexes in Arizona. Rebuilding gets harder and <a class="Link" href="https://www.morningstar.com/news/marketwatch/20250208206/the-end-of-insurance-climate-change-is-destroying-homeowners-and-insurers" target="_blank" >more expensive</a> every time for companies and consumers alike.</p><p>From a business perspective, climate‑driven disruptions also lead to <a class="Link" href="https://www.sciencefocus.com/news/climate-change-income" target="_blank" >lost productivity and wages</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://impact.economist.com/projects/trade-in-transition/climate_change/" target="_blank" >supply chain disruptions</a>. Transportation is one of the most weather‑sensitive parts of development, according to<i> </i><a class="Link" href="https://impact.economist.com/projects/trade-in-transition/climate_change/" target="_blank" >The Economist</a>. When those costs spike, every project becomes more expensive.</p><p>Insurance markets are already buckling under emissions-driven losses, with insurers raising premiums and <a class="Link" href="https://yalelawjournal.org/essay/the-uninsurable-future-the-climate-threat-to-property-insurance-and-how-to-stop-it" target="_blank" >fleeing major markets like Florida and California</a>, as well as <a class="Link" href="https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20240318-climate-change-is-fuelling-the-us-insurance-problem" target="_blank" >Texas and Louisiana</a>. That’s a harbinger for the entire economy. Reinsurance firm Swiss RE estimates that greenhouse gas emissions could <a class="Link" href="https://www.swissre.com/dam/jcr:b257cfe9-68e8-4116-b232-a87949982f7c/nr20210421-ecc-publication-en.pdf" target="_blank" >reduce the U.S. gross domestic product by 10% annually by 2050</a>.</p><p>Revoking the endangerment finding for cars and trucks doesn't just change policy; it removes the legal framework that has guided nearly 20 years of vehicle investment and planning; sidelining capital and delaying much-needed growth.</p><p>That’s why business leaders, in real estate and other industries, should care about the Trump administration’s vehicle emissions and endangerment policy. The consequences reach far beyond the tailpipe, shaping broader market stability, supply chain reliability, the cost of capital and the resilience of the communities we’re all trying to build.</p><p><i>Milton Pinsky, chairman, Banner Real Estate Group LLC</i></p><h3>Name airport after Jackson </h3><p>The coverage of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-rainbow-push-obituary" >Rev. Jesse Jackson’s</a> career and national impact on racial equality reminds us that he will long be remembered as one of the most impactful individuals ever from Chicago. Surely, he deserves a meaningful, lasting, memorial in his home city. I would suggest renaming Midway Airport the Reverend Jackson Memorial Airport, giving Chicago O'Hare and Jackson airports.</p><p><i>Joseph Kimmell, Gold Coast</i></p><h3>Olympic omission </h3><p>Congratulations to all the athletes who participated in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/olympic-sports" target="_blank" >2026 Winter Olympics</a> in Italy. They gave it their all, and that is what sport is, or should be all about.</p><p>I am sad, nevertheless, that the International Olympic Committee violated the spirit of the Olympics — the idea that it should be a competition for athletes from all over the world — by its shameful exclusion of Russia. No one should be excluded from the Games because of politics.</p><p><i>Bert Rice, Edgewater</i></p><h3>Name change</h3><p>Would a name change be in order if <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/02/19/chicago-bears-stadium-hammond-indiana-move" >Hammond</a> happens? Perhaps Da Chicagiana Bearz?</p><p><i>John McClelland, Evanston</i><br></p>
articles.author: Letters to the Editor

articles.title: Mentors can make the difference for young Black men in college. Here's how to be a good one.
articles.summary: <p>Black boys and men have to <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/12/18/in-austin-neighborhood-of-chicago-black-mentors-are-making-college-possible" target="_blank" ><u>scale high walls</u></a> to get into college and complete their degree.</p><p>They are more likely than their peers to juggle caring for elders or younger siblings with classes. They’re often the first in their family to pursue higher education, and frequently come up against the perception that they are not “college material.” Across the country, they are the least likely student group to finish a degree within six years.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://uknowledge.uky.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1111&context=epe_etds" target="_blank" ><u>Research has</u></a> found that mentors, especially those who are also Black and male-identifying, can help, whether that’s by sharing how they navigated similar obstacles or by connecting mentees to tutoring, mental health care and even food.</p><p>But people who work with mentoring initiatives say there are more young people in Chicago who could benefit from this support than there are mentors. That's especially true in Black and segregated communities where students are less likely to have access to adults with postsecondary experience who might know what it’s like to wade through complicated higher education systems.</p><p>That’s why local advocates are asking Chicagoans of all backgrounds to put themselves forward, either by volunteering with a mentoring organization or by connecting with young people through their churches, alma maters or fraternities and sororities.</p><p>“If you can be dedicated and trustworthy, if you have the time and are reliable, please, please sign up to be a mentor with whatever organization that you want,” said Nakia Samuels, who directs youth programming for the nonprofit <a class="Link" href="https://chiul.org/mentoring/" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Urban League</u></a>, which is launching a mentorship program this spring.</p><p>WBEZ spoke with Samuels and others about the characteristics that make for great mentors, and how you can offer your time if you’ve never been a mentor before.</p><h3><b>Everyone has something to offer as a mentor</b></h3><p>Maybe you’re into crafting or opera and you worry you won’t be able to relate to a young Black student.</p><p>Chances are, there is a student out there who shares your interests, Samuels said. And even if they don’t, she said, that’s not what matters most.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-be0000" name="image-be0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/311cf93/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5707x3203+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F08%2F14c6461840ada9fd8c72a5c0c0c1%2Faustinmentors-121925-007.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f56a464/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5707x3203+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F08%2F14c6461840ada9fd8c72a5c0c0c1%2Faustinmentors-121925-007.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0007d1a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5707x3203+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F08%2F14c6461840ada9fd8c72a5c0c0c1%2Faustinmentors-121925-007.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A Black man wearing glasses, a grey suit and a blue tie is seated in a cafe and speaking with others at his table." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0007d1a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5707x3203+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F08%2F14c6461840ada9fd8c72a5c0c0c1%2Faustinmentors-121925-007.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0ea389e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5707x3203+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F08%2F14c6461840ada9fd8c72a5c0c0c1%2Faustinmentors-121925-007.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0007d1a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5707x3203+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F08%2F14c6461840ada9fd8c72a5c0c0c1%2Faustinmentors-121925-007.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Professor and pastor Charles Brown speaks with a mentee and his own mentor at a cafe in the Austin neighborhood. Brown has mentored young Black men through a college mentorship program and his church.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“A good mentor is somebody who is going to be respectful,” Samuels said. “They're going to be consistent, reliable, present. They're going to keep their commitments. They're going to show up.</p><p>A mentor can help their mentee figure out how they are going to pay for their next semester of college, or help them think of places to find community on their predominantly white campus.</p><p>The time commitment will vary: Some organizations require mentors to check in with mentees once a week or once every other week. But consistency is more important than frequency.</p><p>“Even one meaningful conversation every few months can be impactful,” said Charles Brown, an Austin-based college professor and pastor who runs a mentoring program at Malcolm X College and mentors young Black men at his church.</p><h3><b>Good mentors come ready to listen and know when to set boundaries</b></h3><p>Cortez Watson, Jr., who leads the <a class="Link" href="https://forms.office.com/Pages/ResponsePage.aspx?id=woFKdEFXOUebLd1qNyPl8kX7mJGe2o9KpXyp04OLqhxUN1pNN09NT0ZDTk1LRlZMSFdESTNJVTZXRi4u" target="_blank" ><u>Young Black Men Of Honor</u></a> mentoring program through the South Side-based nonprofit Black Star Project, said there is no one right way to mentor. But mentors should approach the relationship with curiosity and openness, and start by getting to know their mentee and how to relate to them.</p><p>“It's really just starting where you're at,” he said, without assuming you’ll immediately know how to solve your mentee’s problems. “Then lots of questions, not lots of answers.”</p><p>Sometimes, Samuels said, a mentee needs someone who will really listen to them — without checking their phone or multi-tasking — more than they need a solution.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-fullBleed><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-6e0000" name="module-6e0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><div class="PageListAI" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-hide-reading-time="true" style=" --titleColor:#eb1010 "><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-100000" name="list-100000"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> More reporting from The Persistence Project </div> </div> </div> <div class="PageListAI-items" data-with-borders> <div class="PageListAI-items-item"> <div class="PagePromoB"> <div class="PagePromo-media"><a class="Link" aria-label="In Austin, Black mentors are making college possible" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/12/18/in-austin-neighborhood-of-chicago-black-mentors-are-making-college-possible" ><picture data-crop="medium-4x3"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="328" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a885f25/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8163x5464+0+0/resize/490x328!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F61%2Fe7d978234a5da0405be5cc6eb847%2Faustinmentors-121925-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMjhweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="328" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f492eb3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8163x5464+0+0/resize/490x328!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F61%2Fe7d978234a5da0405be5cc6eb847%2Faustinmentors-121925-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMjhweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A young Black man wearing a blue long-sleeve shirt looks at an older Black man sitting next to him who is wearing a suit and tie." width="490" height="328" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f492eb3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8163x5464+0+0/resize/490x328!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F61%2Fe7d978234a5da0405be5cc6eb847%2Faustinmentors-121925-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMjhweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </a></div> <div class="PagePromoB-content"> <div class="PagePromo-category"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education" >Education</a> </div> <div class="PagePromo-title"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/12/18/in-austin-neighborhood-of-chicago-black-mentors-are-making-college-possible" >In Austin, Black mentors are making college possible</a> </div> <div class="PagePromo-description">Black men in the West Side community face some of the biggest hurdles to getting a degree. Just one out of four who make it to college graduate in six years.</div> <div class="PagePromo-byline"><div class="PagePromo-date"> <bsp-timestamp data-timestamp=1766057400000 data-promo="true"> <template data-date-tpl>[month] [day], [year], [hour]:[minute][ampm] [timezone]</template> <span data-date> </span> </bsp-timestamp><span class="dot"></span></div> <div class="PagePromo-author"> <span>By </span><strong><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/lisa-kurian-philip-wbez" >Lisa Kurian Philip | WBEZ </a></strong> </div> <div class="PagePromo-readingTime"> <span class="dot-divider"></span> <svg class="PagePromo-readingTime-icon"> <use xlink:href="#icon-clock-reading"></use> </svg> <span class="PagePromo-readingTime-text"> <bsp-time-to-read data-article-word-count="1901"></bsp-time-to-read> read </span> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div></div> </div><p>“Because maybe I just need somebody to talk to and just unload for 10 or 15 minutes,” she said.</p><p>No matter how often a mentor plans to check in, Samuels said, it’s important to be upfront about how much time and support you can offer.</p><p>“If there's something beyond what the mentor can do or what they can speak to, there will be other resources,” she said.</p><p>Maybe a mentee needs mental health counseling or is dealing with food insecurity. If that’s beyond a mentor’s expertise, they can turn to their network of friends and colleagues to see if they can connect students to the resources they need.</p><h3><b>Tell your mentee what they’re good at </b></h3><p>Samuels said this one is especially important for Black children and boys because, often, they aren’t told their strengths.</p><p>“They don't hear, ‘This is what you're great at,’ Samuels said. “It's always, ‘Don't, don't, don't.’”</p><p>Similarly, Watson, Jr. said mentors aren’t there to judge, but to offer young people a safe place to be themselves and talk about their hardships and their mistakes. He takes that approach at Young Black Men Of Honor.</p><p>“The standard is high, the learning is rich, the outcomes are important,” he said. “But first and foremost, y'all free to be kids up in here.”</p><h3><b>Don’t expect anything in return</b></h3><p>Your mentee may not always take your advice, and it can be hard to watch them screw up or go through tough times.</p><p>“You may not even get a thank you,” Samuels said.</p><p>But mentors who have done this work say they learn so much from their mentees, and there’s no feeling quite like helping a young person get their college acceptance letter or cross the commencement stage.</p><p>“Your heart's got to be in the right place, because the work is hard work,” Watson, Jr. said.</p><p>Brown, the college professor, says it’s important to remember that not every mentor-mentee relationship is forever, and that’s okay.</p><p>“As students grow, their needs change,” he said. “Outgrowing a mentor doesn’t mean the relationship failed. It means it worked.”</p><p><i>Lisa Kurian Philip covers higher education for WBEZ, in partnership with </i><a class="Link" href="https://www.opencampusmedia.org/" target="_blank" ><i><u>Open Campus</u></i></a><i>.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-fullBleed><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b00000" name="module-b00000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Interested in becoming a mentor? This list is a good place to start</h3><br><h3>South Side</h3><a class="Link" href="https://chiul.org/mentoring/" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Urban League</u></a><br>4510 S. Michigan Ave.<br><a class="Link" href="mailto:nsamuels@chiul.org" target="_blank" ><u>nsamuels@chiul.org</u></a><br><br><a class="Link" href="https://www.blackstarproject.org/2021/young-black-men-of-honor#:~:text=The%20YBMOH%20program%20connects%20participants,positive%20forces%20in%20their%20communities." target="_blank" ><u>Young Black Men of Honor</u></a><br>3509 South King Dr., Suite 2B<br><a class="Link" href="mailto:communityinfo@blackstarproject.org" target="_blank" ><u>communityinfo@blackstarproject.org</u></a><br><br><h3>West Side</h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.collegementoringexperience.org/" target="_blank" ><u>College Mentoring Experience</u></a><br>5100 W. Harrison St.<br><a class="Link" href="mailto:info@collegementoringexperience.org" target="_blank" ><u>info@collegementoringexperience.org</u></a><br><br><a class="Link" href="https://www.ccc.edu/malcolm-x/departments/iaam/" target="_blank" ><u>I am an African American Male</u></a><br>1900 W. Jackson Blvd.<br><a class="Link" href="mailto:cbrown218@ccc.edu" target="_blank" ><u>cbrown218@ccc.edu</u></a><br><br><h3>Greater Chicago area</h3><a class="Link" href="https://bbbschgo.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Big Brothers Big Sisters of Metropolitan Chicago</u></a><br>130 S. Jefferson St. Suite 200<br><a class="Link" href="mailto:bbbschgo@bbbschgo.org" target="_blank" ><u>bbbschgo@bbbschgo.org</u></a><br><br><a class="Link" href="https://imentor.org/where-we-work/chicago" target="_blank" ><u>iMentor</u></a><br>20 N. Wacker Dr., 12th Floor<br><a class="Link" href="mailto:nlongino@imentor.org" target="_blank" ><u>nlongino@imentor.org</u></a><br><br><a class="Link" href="https://www.urbanmalenetwork.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Urban Male Network</u></a><br>7637 S. Laflin St.<br><a class="Link" href="mailto:cturner@urbanmalenetwork.org" target="_blank" ><u>cturner@urbanmalenetwork.org</u></a><br><br><a class="Link" href="https://www.100bmc.org/mentoring" target="_blank" ><u>100 Black Men of Chicago</u></a><br>308 S. Jefferson St.<br>South Side – Claude Gibson: <a class="Link" href="mailto:claude.gibson@100bmc.org" target="_blank" ><u>claude.gibson@100bmc.org</u></a><br>South Suburbs – Rodney White: <a class="Link" href="mailto:rodney.white@100bmc.org" target="_blank" ><u>rodney.white@100bmc.org</u></a><br>Brogrammers – Aaron Dorsey: <a class="Link" href="mailto:aaron.dorsey@100bmc.org" target="_blank" ><u>aaron.dorsey@100bmc.org</u></a></div> </div><p></p>

class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Matt Rourke/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wjtltdd-l-i/" target="_blank" >Takeaways from Trump’s State of the Union address </a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wjtltdd-l-d/" target="_blank" >Tina Sfondeles</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wjtltdd-l-h/" target="_blank" >Lynn Sweet</a></h5><p><b>SOTU speech</b>: As Chicagoans affected by President Donald Trump’s "Operation Midway Blitz" sat as guests in the U.S. House chamber, the president declared the country was in a "golden age" and boasted about border security, tax cuts and his targeted enforcement of those who lack legal status.</p><p><b>Key takeaways</b>: During his nearly two-hour speech, Trump bashed the U.S. Supreme Court — with four justices in attendance — and promised to ignore the high court's ruling last week striking down his tariffs. He said tariffs could replace income taxes, though that is not a serious policy proposal. He also claimed to have brought down grocery prices, which <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wjtltdd-l-k/" target="_blank" >is not the case in Chicago</a>. Find <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wjtltdd-l-u/" target="_blank" >more takeaways here</a>. For a fact check, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wjtltdd-l-o/" target="_blank" >NPR has annotated the speech</a>.</p><p><b>Inside the House</b>: U.S. Reps. Ilan Omar (D-Minn.) and Rashida Tlaib (D-Mich) both shouted at Trump from their seats, as Trump talked about illegal immigration and doubled down on attacking so-called Democratic sanctuary cities, including Chicago. U.S. Rep. Lauren Underwood, a Democrat from Naperville, left the chamber during the speech. So did Rep. Al Green (D-Texas). Sen. Tammy Duckworth and U.S. Reps. Mike Quigley, Sean Casten, Delia Ramirez, Jan Schakowsky and Eric Sorensen boycotted the address.</p><p><b>Local guests</b>: U.S. Rep. Jesus "Chuy" Garcia brought Marimar Martinez, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wjtltdd-l-b/" target="_blank" >a woman shot five times</a> by U.S. Border Patrol agents, as his guest. U.S. Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi brought University of Illinois at Chicago student Jaime Coria Jr., <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wjtltdd-l-n/" target="_blank" >whose father was detained</a> and sent to Mexico City.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-i-wjtltdd-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>CITY HALL 🏛️</h4><div
