data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8b1d8b1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/9281x5209+145+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb4%2Fdf%2F963afe6649edbc84e5682a44ca87%2Ferie-neighborhood-house-center.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rendering of La Villita Erie House</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided by Lohengrin Foundation</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Cristina De La Rosa, executive director of Erie Neighborhood House, said community members have continuously expressed a need for safety. The nonprofit hosted focus groups and distributed surveys to hear more from community members about what a safe space for residents would look like and what sort of programming they’d like to see at the new center.</p><p>“We wanted to be really intentional,” De La Rosa said. “We really wanted to make sure that when people walk into the building, they felt like they could see themselves there — that it was designed for them.”</p><p>The result is a center with a full-size gymnasium, flexible classrooms, an arts studio, private counseling and therapy offices, teen and tech lounges, community kitchen, outdoor green spaces, rooftop playground and children’s garden.</p><p>Erie Neighborhood House already offers a suite of youth programming, but the new center will allow the organization to offer full-day after school programming, De La Rosa said. And in the summer, school-aged children can stay during the week and receive breakfast, lunch and a snack.</p><p>Erie Neighborhood House hopes to break ground this fall and open within a year of its groundbreaking. The $22 million project will expand opportunities for the nonprofit to partner with other organizations in the area — like One Lawndale.</p><p>“We don't see this as two separate neighborhoods,” Townsell said of North Lawndale and Little Village. “We see this as one Lawndale and folks coming together around supporting young people.”</p><p>Rodriguez said the foundation didn’t intend to choose two winners, let alone two from adjacent neighborhoods. But the award is helping to connect both projects with possible capital partners, doubling its intended impact.</p><p>North Lawndale and Little Village have higher numbers of youth and youth violence, according to the foundation. The number of youth the projects would serve, along with each project's feasibility, helped elevate the two proposals.</p><p>There were more than 70 submissions for Thriving Youth, Stronger Communities, which showcases the excitement in supporting youth communities, Rodriguez said.</p><p>“There's no limit on the ideas or the need. It's only a limit on the resources that are available,” he said.</p>
articles.author: Abby Miller

articles.title: Ald. Shirley Drewenski elected acting Harvey mayor
articles.summary: <p>The Harvey City Council elected an acting mayor one month after the death of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/30/harvey-mayor-christopher-j-clark-dead" target="_blank" >Mayor Christopher Clark</a>.</p><p>Ald. Shirley Drewenski (1st) was unanimously selected during Monday’s city council meeting and was sworn in to serve the remainder of Clark’s term through next year.</p><p>Drewenski had previously been sworn in as mayor pro tempore after Clark’s death. Her appointment comes after two prior council votes ended in ties. A majority vote is required for the appointment.</p><p>"I accept this responsibility with humility and a deep sense of duty to the residents of Harvey. Our focus remains on stability, transparency, and continuing the work of city government while honoring the legacy of Mayor Christopher Clark," Drewenski said in a statement. </p><p>"This is a time for unity and respect. I am committed to working collaboratively with the City Council, city staff, and our community to ensure continuity in leadership and service. Above all, we will continue to keep Mayor Clark’s family in our thoughts as we move forward together."</p><p>Clark died Jan. 30 and had served as mayor for six years. The cause of his death has not been revealed.</p><p>A memorial service will be held for Clark on March 28, according to the city.<br></p>
articles.author: Cindy Hernandez

articles.title: Trump declara supuesta ‘edad de oro’ de Estados Unidos mientras seis demócratas de Illinois boicotean su discurso
articles.summary: <p>Mientras los habitantes de Chicago afectados por el “Operativo Midway Blitz” del presidente Donald Trump se sentaban como invitados en la Cámara de Representantes, el presidente declaró que el país estaba en una “edad de oro” y se jactó de la seguridad fronteriza, los recortes de impuestos y su aplicación selectiva de la ley contra quienes carecen de estatus legal.</p><p>Casi una hora después del discurso del Estado de la Unión, el más largo en la historia, el presidente pidió a los miembros que se pusieran de pie si creían que “el primer deber del gobierno estadounidense es proteger a los ciudadanos estadounidenses, no a los inmigrantes ilegales”. Los demócratas permanecieron sentados, algunos moviendo la cabeza, mientras que los republicanos se levantaron.</p><p>Los representantes Ilan Omar, demócrata por Minnesota, y Rashida Tlaib, demócrata por Michigan, gritaron a Trump desde sus asientos, mientras él hablaba sobre la inmigración ilegal y se reafirmaba en su ataque contra las denominadas ciudades santuario del Partido Demócrata, incluyendo Chicago.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d50000" name="module-d50000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“También les pido que terminen con las mortales ciudades santuario que protegen a los criminales y que impongan serias sanciones a los funcionarios públicos que bloqueen la deportación de inmigrantes criminales, en muchos casos, capos de drogas, asesinos en todo nuestro país”, dijo Trump. “Están bloqueando la deportación de estas personas de nuestro país, y deberían sentirse avergonzados”.</p><p>Y a pesar de haber tomado medidas para restringir la entrada de personas al país, a menudo en nombre de proteger la seguridad nacional, Trump habló sobre dejar entrar a las personas en el país “legalmente”.</p><p>“Siempre permitiremos que las personas entren legalmente”, agregó Trump. “Personas que amarán a nuestro país y trabajarán arduamente para mantenerlo”.</p><p>Los comentarios de Trump sobre sus medidas de inmigración se dieron cuando el representante Jesús “Chuy” García llevó a Marimar Martínez, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatos" target="_blank" >una mujer a la que agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza le dispararon cinco veces</a>, como su invitada.</p><p>El representante Raja Krishnamoorthi llevó como invitado al <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/25/estudiante-de-uic-asiste-al-estado-de-la-union-tras-la-deportacion-de-su-padre" target="_blank" >estudiante de la Universidad de Illinois en Chicago, Jaime Coria Jr, cuyo padre fue detenido</a> por agentes federales durante el clímax del Operativo Midway Blitz y enviado a más de 2,000 millas de distancia a la Ciudad de México.</p><p>García publicó una foto de Martínez en su cuenta de X.</p><p>“Mientras su propio gobierno sigue desacreditando su nombre, estoy orgulloso de su valentía al compartir su historia —y orgulloso de tenerla como mi invitada en el SOTU (Discurso sobre el Estado de la Unión)”, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/RepChuyGarcia/status/2026485393734951227?s=20" target="_blank" >escribió García</a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Marimar Martinez—shot five times by a CBP agent, who later bragged about it—is in the House chambers for the State of the Union.<br>As her own government continues to smear her name, I’m proud of her courage in sharing her story—and proud to have her as my guest at the SOTU. <a href="https://t.co/UM163kS9DW">pic.twitter.com/UM163kS9DW</a></p>— Congressman Chuy García (@RepChuyGarcia) <a href="https://twitter.com/RepChuyGarcia/status/2026485393734951227?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">February 25, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>La representante Lauren Underwood, demócrata por Naperville, la jefa de la campaña de reclutamiento de candidatos de los demócratas de la Cámara, abandonó la cámara durante el discurso de Trump. Underwood sacudió la cabeza mientras se levantaba para irse.</p><p>La demócrata de Illinois se despidió de algunas de sus colegas mujeres demócratas. Las mujeres, que llevaban vestimenta blanca en homenaje al movimiento sufragista, intercambiaron risas antes de que Underwood saliera rápidamente por la parte trasera.</p><p>También lo hizo el representante Al Green, demócrata por Texas, quien fue escoltado por seguridad tras sostener un letrero que decía “Las personas negras no son simios”, que le fue quitado por el líder de la mayoría en la Cámara, Steve Scalise. Esto se refirió a un video <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2026/02/06/donald-trump-racist-video-barack-michelle-obama-depiction-primates" target="_blank" >racista</a> que Trump compartió a principios de mes que incluía una representación del expresidente Barack Obama y Michelle Obama como simios en una jungla.</p><p>La senadora Tammy Duckworth, junto con los representantes Mike Quigley, Sean Casten, Delia Ramírez, Jan Schakowsky y Eric Sorensen, boicotearon el discurso anual, con Duckworth calificándolo el lunes como “otro mitin de campaña lleno de mentiras”.</p><p>"La madre de un ángel", Jacqueline Medina, fue una de las muchas invitadas que Trump trajo al discurso. La hija de Medina, Lizbeth, fue supuestamente asesinada por un hombre mexicano que carecía de estatus legal en diciembre de 2023.</p><p>“Su madre destrozada está en la galería para recordarle a todos en esta cámara exactamente por qué estamos deportando a los criminales inmigrantes ilegales en números récord,” dijo Trump entre aplausos de los republicanos. “Y los estamos sacando de aquí rápido”.</p><p>Antes de la retórica política, Trump trató de pintar un panorama optimista sobre sus logros del primer año —y las bajas calificaciones de aprobación— antes de las elecciones de medio término de noviembre. El presidente alabó los aranceles generalizados y lamentó la decisión de la Corte Suprema que los anuló, calificándola de “desafortunada”, mientras la cámara mostraba a los jueces de la Corte Suprema en el recinto.</p><p>“Está salvando a nuestro país”, dijo Trump sobre los aranceles, mientras también los calificaba de “protectores de la paz”.</p><p>El presidente presentó al equipo olímpico ganador de la medalla de oro del hockey sobre hielo masculino en la Cámara, recibiendo aplausos, y anunció que otorgará la Medalla Presidencial de la Libertad al portero del equipo, Connor Hellebuyck.</p><p>Trump recibió una ovación poco común de algunos demócratas, incluyendo la senadora Elizabeth Warren, demócrata por Massachusetts, cuando dijo que deseaba que el Congreso aprobara una medida para detener el comercio de acciones con información privilegiada.</p><p><i>Con información de AP </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
articles.author: Tina Sfondeles

articles.title: Jury awards $5.7 million to family targeted in botched police raid on Chicago’s South Side in 2018
articles.summary: <p>A federal jury on Wednesday awarded $5.7 million to a family targeted in a wrongful raid nearly a decade ago in which Chicago SWAT officers were accused of storming into their home without knocking, aiming guns at the children and forcing their grandmother outside in her underwear.</p><p>The three-week trial at the Dirksen Federal Courthouse took jurors back to a time when lawyers for the Tate family argued there was a widespread pattern of excessive force against children that went unchecked because of the cops’ so-called code of silence.. </p><p>The case centers on a set of search warrants executed on Aug. 9, 2018, in the New City neighborhood. Officers were told a convicted felon lived in one home in the 5000 block of South Hermitage Avenue and kept drugs and weapons in Ebony Tate’s home next door, records show.</p><p>Tate, her four minor children and their 55-year-old grandmother reported hearing loud explosives, but no knock announcing their entrance. The officers pushed them out of the home at gunpoint, according to the family’s lawyers. The grandmother, Cynthia Eason, wasn’t given time to grab clothes and was forced out on the sidewalk in just a t-shirt and underwear.</p><p>They weren’t considered suspects and no contraband was found.</p><p>“This family has scars,” Al Hofeld Jr., a lawyer for the family, said earlier this week. “You can see and feel this family’s pain … for almost eight years they have been asking for justice in this case.”</p><p>The raid happened before the Chicago Police Department was placed under a federal consent decree mandating sweeping changes, including reforms to its policies and practices for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/07/16/johnson-police-raid-reforms-anjanette-young" target="_blank" >executing search warrants</a>.</p><p>Those reforms were only enacted after troubling raids garnered media coverage and drew outrage. Anjanette Young, who was undressing when officers conducted a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2023/6/15/23763033/anjanette-young-chicago-police-board-alex-wolinski-botched-raid" target="_blank" >botched raid of her apartment</a> in 2019, became a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2024/02/16/anjanette-young-chicago-police-no-knock-warrants-botched-raid" target="_blank" >crusader against the use of no-knock warrants</a>.</p><p>Lawyers for the city and the officers have offered a drastically different version of events on the day the Tate’s home was raided. They argue the officers didn’t point weapons at any members of the family and were serving a valid search warrant.</p><p>They also deny a pervasive pattern of excessive force against children at the time.</p><p>“Those men are the best of the best,” Marion Claire Moore, a lawyer for the city of Chicago, said of the officers. “They did not target their weapons at the children during that search warrant.”</p><p>There's no body camera footage of the raid because the department didn’t have the ability or resources to give SWAT officers cameras at the time, a city lawyer said.</p><p>During the trial, Hofeld tried to call former Mayor Rahm Emanuel to testify about the police department's code of silence, which he’d famously acknowledged during a speech he gave after the fatal police shooting of Laquan McDonald.</p><p>U.S. District Judge John Tharp originally allowed for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/01/29/former-mayor-rahm-emanuel-could-be-on-the-hook-to-testify-about-cpds-code-of-silence-in-federal-trial" target="_blank" >Emanuel’s potential testimony</a>, but <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/01/30/federal-judge-changes-his-mind-spares-ex-chicago-mayor-rahm-emanuel-from-testifying-about-code-of-silence" target="_blank" >reversed course days later</a>.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/6/23/23770506/anjanette-young-search-warrant-reforms-chicago-police-department-fewer-raids-homes-al-hofeld" target="_blank" >After Anjanette Young search warrant reforms, a massive drop in number of Chicago Police Department raids on homes</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2024/02/16/anjanette-young-chicago-police-no-knock-warrants-botched-raid" target="_blank" >https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2024/02/16/anjanette-young-chicago-police-no-knock-warrants-botched-raid</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2024/02/16/anjanette-young-chicago-police-no-knock-warrants-botched-raid" target="_blank" >Rahm Emanuel spared from testifying about police ‘code of silence’ as federal judge changes his mnd</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
articles.author: Sophie Sherry

articles.title: Mayor Johnson defends choice to lead city's Animal Care and Control, despite conditions at city-run shelter
articles.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson on Wednesday rallied behind his permanent choice to lead Chicago’s Commission on Animal Care and Control, amid concerns about high euthanasia rates and shelter conditions that neglect and endanger dogs, cats and volunteers.</p><p>Susan Capello spent three years as acting executive director before Johnson gave her the permanent job on Feb. 12.</p><p>Six days later, 17 alderpersons signed letter to Johnson at a City Council meeting demanding that the mayor meet an April 1 deadline to launch a national search for a “dedicated, knowledgeable executive director with a proven track record.”</p><p>The letter was circulated by Alds. Gilbert Villegas (36th) and Ruth Cruz (30th). They cited a host of concerns raised by animal care advocates and current and former volunteers at the city-run shelter at 2741 S. Western.</p><p>They ranged from high euthanasia rates and poor living conditions to three years of “steadily increased” dogs bites that have jeopardized public safety. The letter also claimed that Chicago police officers are frequently asked to cover animal care and control duties that are best handled by trained employees.</p><p>Despite those concerns, Johnson’s stood firmly behind Capello, and blamed the challenges she faces on the affordability crisis in Chicago and around the nation.</p><p>“Between 2023 and 2025, intake increased by nearly 5,000 additional cats and dogs. Higher food and veterinarian costs have led more Chicagoans to make the tragic decision to relinquish their animals, contributing to this intake growth,” the mayor said.</p><p> With intake rising, transfers to the city’s rescue partners have surged to their highest levels in a decade. Euthanasia rates remained flat from 2024 to 2025, when adoption rates rose to their highest rate in department history, the mayor said.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2025/07/25/pet-owner-surrender-dogs-cats-overwhelming-shelters-rescues" >Pet owner surrender rates are up, overwhelming Chicago shelters</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Cappello described said the pressure the high-volume municipal shelter is under as “real and constant.” Dogs and cats arrive every day, “often in crisis.” No animal gets turned away.</p><p>“Our doors are open to everything that comes in. We do take sick animals on a regular basis. Sometimes, sick animals stay here too long and can get sick. We don’t have the option of closing our door and saying, `We’re not going to take those today,’“ Cappello said. “We do our best… But with that sheer number of animals, you’re going to have illness. It’s going to happen. Our medical staff is on top of that. They look at it as much as they can. ”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-be0000" name="image-be0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3c5491c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2F12%2F5591dc854c668cf116e296da074c%2Fmayorpets-260226-08.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9d518ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2F12%2F5591dc854c668cf116e296da074c%2Fmayorpets-260226-08.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f7307f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2F12%2F5591dc854c668cf116e296da074c%2Fmayorpets-260226-08.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Executive Director of Chicago Animal Care and Control Susan Cappello speaks at a press conference at Chicago Animal Care & Control on Wednesday, Feb. 25, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f7307f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2F12%2F5591dc854c668cf116e296da074c%2Fmayorpets-260226-08.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d3f9a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2F12%2F5591dc854c668cf116e296da074c%2Fmayorpets-260226-08.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f7307f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2F12%2F5591dc854c668cf116e296da074c%2Fmayorpets-260226-08.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Executive Director of Chicago Animal Care and Control Susan Cappello speaks at a press conference at Chicago Animal Care & Control on Wednesday. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Cappello acknowledged that many of the 240 dogs at the city-run shelter are only being walked once a week. They spend most of their time cooped up in cages. Her goal, she said, is to change that.</p><p>“Every dog gets a walk every day. That can only help. The fresh air. The outside. The interaction with people. Make them more adoptable. Make them more visible. That’s our goal for this year — and it’s a big one,” Cappello said.</p><p>Cruz said Capello was warned about the lack of exercise and dog-walking at a City Council hearing on shelter conditions last year, and waited until now to do something about it.</p><p>“I don’t understand waiting three years for the mayor to appoint someone that he describes as a remarkable,” Cruz told the Sun-Times.</p><p>Cruz said she’s been bombarded with complaints from animal rights advocates and volunteers, who have described “unsafe and unsanitary conditions for the animals” and a “toxic” environment for volunteers.</p><p>“At any point, if volunteers disagree or bring new ideas and the leadership doesn’t agree with them, then they’re asked not to come back again,” Cruz said. “There’s a toxic culture... and fear of retaliation.”</p><p>Villegas acknowledged that there have been chronic complaints about conditions at the city's animal shelter for decades under a host of different leaders, as well as complaints about funding for the facility. But he said when volunteers demand a nationwide search, the city owes it to those animal advocates to conduct one.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-eb0000" name="image-eb0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/142c04c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff1%2Fff%2Fc0ff32444b47b2ffe3d5ec7cd041%2Fmayorpets-260226-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88bf9d3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff1%2Fff%2Fc0ff32444b47b2ffe3d5ec7cd041%2Fmayorpets-260226-19.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c44199/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff1%2Fff%2Fc0ff32444b47b2ffe3d5ec7cd041%2Fmayorpets-260226-19.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A worker changes paperwork on a dog cage at Chicago Animal Care & Control on Wednesday, Feb. 25, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c44199/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff1%2Fff%2Fc0ff32444b47b2ffe3d5ec7cd041%2Fmayorpets-260226-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eaa4df6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff1%2Fff%2Fc0ff32444b47b2ffe3d5ec7cd041%2Fmayorpets-260226-19.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c44199/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff1%2Fff%2Fc0ff32444b47b2ffe3d5ec7cd041%2Fmayorpets-260226-19.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A worker changes paperwork on a dog cage at Chicago Animal Care & Control on Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"We're talking about volunteers who have been fired by Animal Care and Control. It's a toxic environment there. Volunteers there are not wanting to volunteer because of all types of issues there," Villegas said. "Volunteers, organizers and animal advocates have brought concerns to us. All they're asking for is a national search to determine what type of talent is potentially out there."</p><p>WGN Radio's Steve Dale, a certified animal behavior consultant, joined the mayor Wednesday to defend Cappello, saying many landlords prohibit dogs or require a fee for dogs over a certain weight.</p><p>"That's a national issue — it's not only a Chicago issue," Dale said. "And there are lots of individuals working to try to do something about it." </p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/02/24/pet-surrender-unwanted-pets-fat-cat-rescue-wadsworth" >Pet surrenders surge during hard times — but there is a right way and a wrong way</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
articles.author: Fran Spielman

articles.title: Waymo takes step toward deploying autonomous vehicles in Chicago
articles.summary: <p>Waymo’s self-driving vehicles are hitting Chicago streets — but with human drivers, for now.</p><p>The company, a subsidiary of Google’s parent Alphabet, said that human drivers on Wednesday began driving its vehicles to help the company map the city "to lay the early groundwork for operations in Chicago."</p><p>The cars will be used east of the Kennedy and Dan Ryan expressways, from the South Loop to Wrigleyville. A timeline for the test wasn’t provided.</p><p>“We are excited to begin laying the early groundwork for our ride-hailing operations in Chicago, and look forward to serving the city in the future,” the company wrote on its blog.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson’s office says the city was given a heads up. The Chicago test comes a day after the company announced it was dispatching its robotaxis in four more cities in Texas and Florida.</p><p>“As of now, autonomous vehicles have not been authorized within the City of Chicago or the State of Illinois,” Johnson’s office said in a statement. “The City looks forward to engaging with commuters, workers, transit advocates and industry leaders as we pursue constructive dialogue with our partners in Springfield and an equitable path forward.”</p><p>State Rep. Kam Buckner, D-Chicago, has filed a bill in Springfield to allow autonomous vehicles to be tested in Cook County.</p><p>"This progress is a vital step toward safer streets and more accessible transportation for all our neighborhoods," Buckner was quoted as saying in a statement from Waymo.</p><p>The company has touted the service as a way to combat drunk driving and give autonomy to epileptic residents who can’t drive.</p><p>Buckner didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p>Waymo’s robotaxis have experienced problems that have triggered community backlashes and regulatory inquiries.</p><p>Last year in San Francisco, a Waymo robotaxi ran over and killed a 9-year-old cat beloved around its neighborhood. Many of the self-driving vehicles later contributed to traffic jams during an extended power outage when they stalled at intersections with darkened traffic signals. The National Transportation Safety Board is investigating complaints in Austin, Texas, about Waymo robotaxis operating unsafely around school buses carrying children.</p><p>While Waymo was first granted California permits in 2018, the West coast state rewrote some of its laws earlier this year after police in San Bruno, south of San Francisco, tried to ticket a self-driving vehicle for an illegal U-turn earlier this year. The police department said in a social media post that officers stopped the vehicle, but did not write a ticket as their “citation books don’t have a box for ‘robot,’” opting instead to contact Waymo about the “glitch.”</p><p>Christina Whitehouse, founder of the cyclist advocacy group Bike Lane Uprising, said the company started off on the wrong foot by unloading its initial fleet in a bike lane, per videos posted to social media.</p><p>“It shows they didn't put any effort into how they're going to be running this, and they'll do whatever they want while they're here,” Whitehouse said.</p><p>She had a list of concerns, namely “opaque” operations and reporting, worrying that more cyclists could be hit with less accountability. A California cyclist is <a class="Link" href="https://bayareabicyclelaw.com/bay-area-bicycle-law-files-lawsuit-against-waymo-and-alphabet-after-san-francisco-bike-lane-collision/" target="_blank" >currently suing the company</a> after a passenger opened the door after the vehicle parked in a bike lane, hitting the cyclist and throwing her off her bike, only to be hit by another Waymo vehicle that was pulling over to drop off a passenger.</p><p>California’s DMVs have been regulating the company’s vehicles for eight years. But the state's regulations for robotaxi companies say they only have to report crashes during testing, which must be reported within 10 days, and an annual report on “disengagements,” or when a human driver manually takes over the car. Revisions to the rules are expected later this year.</p><p>Cost is another question. </p><p>Data from Obi, a rideshare price comparison company, found that Waymo rides were an average of 12.7% <a class="Link" href="https://rideobi.com/tesla/" target="_blank" ><u>more expensive</u></a> than an Uber and 27.3% more expensive than a Lyft. However, the price gap is smaller for shorter rides — all of which are more expensive than the city’s transit system, which Whitehouse worried would lose legislative attention or resources to a “new and shiny distraction.”</p><p>“It would be great if we could put this energy into the public transportation system,” Whitehouse said. “It has been put on the backburner for so many years, and (public transportation) has the opportunity to be great, accessible and affordable for people. … It would be great if we had working elevators at our CTA stations.”</p><p><i>Contributing: Associated Press</i></p>
articles.author: Violet Miller

articles.title: CPS eyes changes to ChiArts conservatory model, upsetting parents who want more arts during school day
articles.summary: <p>The unique conservatory model at the Chicago High School for the Arts will likely change when the district takes over management of the school later this year, a possibility that’s upsetting parents and students who hoped the arts program would remain as is.</p><p>Right now, ChiArts is privately managed and students spend three hours every afternoon in a conservatory where they focus on an arts discipline, such as dance, visual arts or theater. They also take five hours of academic classes.</p><p>But under the potential new structure, there would be 30 minutes less of conservatory instruction and those lessons would be embedded throughout the day. Chicago Public Schools officials say they have to shorten the school’s day to comply with union agreements, but students will have the opportunity to participate in up to 100 minutes of additional arts instruction after school.</p><p>“In order to make it sustainable and to have the program be part of the CPS family of fine and performing arts schools, there are certain guardrails and things that need to be changed,” Conrad Timbers-Ausar, CPS’ acting chief portfolio officer, said during a virtual community meeting about the transition Wednesday evening.</p><p>CPS did not permit reporters for the Sun-Times and WBEZ to attend the meeting, but we obtained an audio recording of it.</p><p>Parents and teachers expressed frustration at that explanation, saying the district hadn’t been creative enough in looking for ways to maintain the current conservatory model.</p><p>“Making it an after-school program does not keep it ChiArts,” one parent said during the meeting.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/11/03/chicago-school-board-chiarts-epic-budgets" target="_blank" ><u>The school board voted to absorb ChiArts</u></a> in November after the private manager of the contract school, which is similar to a charter school, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/10/03/chiarts-board-not-renewing-contract-with-cps-citing-unsustainable-deficit-sparking-questions-about-future" target="_blank" ><u>decided it could no longer run it, citing financial deficits</u></a>. More than 530 students attend the school from across the city. Students audition to get in.</p><p>In an interview with the Sun-Times, Timbers-Ausar said the presentation wasn't a firm plan but rather an outline for how to maintain the instructional time afforded by the conservatory model while abiding by state law, district policies and other limitations.</p><p>It also takes into account the district's own financial troubles as it faces a deficit of more than $500 million headed into next school year, he said. The district plans to seek more feedback before coming up with a concrete plan.</p><p>How students are taught through the conservatory is also likely to shift. The school hires professional artists to instruct students in their disciplines. But when CPS runs the school, a state-certified teacher needs to oversee the classes.</p><p>The district is recommending that licensed teachers pair up with teaching artists to satisfy graduation requirements. Timbers-Ausar said the district is working to help teaching artists stay on at the new school. As the new school begins to hire staff, current teachers and teaching artists will be first in line for interviews, he added.</p><p>Earlier this week, Casey Fuess was introduced as the school’s incoming principal.</p><p>Fuess is a former choir director at Lindblom Math & Science Academy who spent the last five years as an assistant principal at CPS’ virtual academy, <a class="Link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/caseyfuess/" target="_blank" ><u>according to his LinkedIn profile. </u></a></p><p>When CPS runs the school, it will be funded like the district’s other 72 fine and performing arts schools. Currently, CPS pays for the academic portion of a student's day, while the money for ChiArts’ conservatory program comes from private donors.</p><p>The name of the school will likely have to change due to trademark issues, Timbers-Ausar said.</p><p>Parent Isabela Cabrera said in an interview that the conservatory model has provided emotional stability for her daughter, who is a freshman at ChiArts. She travels to the school in Humboldt Park from the South Shore neighborhood, but said the program makes the long commute worth it.</p><p>“You see the kids coming out of school very happy, enjoying life,” she said.</p><p>Cabrera and other parents said they will continue to advocate to keep the conservatory program unchanged by organizing protests and pushing state legislators to allow the school to have longer hours.</p><p>A group of parents, students and staff plans to demonstrate outside the school on March 3 to call on the district to work with the school community to preserve the current conservatory model.</p><p>“We have to keep fighting for this,” Cabrera said.</p>
articles.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

articles.title: Two Cook County property tax appeal commissioners face strong challengers in March 17 primary
articles.summary: <p>Despite wielding enormous power — to change the tax bills of any property owner — the Cook County Board of Review long has been treated as a relatively obscure backwater of local government.</p><p>That has not been the case, though, in the past few years, with the three-member Board of Review becoming the subject of plenty of headlines.</p><p>There was the viral Chicago police body-camera video of Board of Review Commissioner Samantha Steele, who’s been charged with drunk driving in a crash on the North Side — and repeatedly told arresting officers that she was a county elected official.</p><p>There also was the divisive issue of how much in property taxes the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2024/02/22/bears-stadium-arlington-heights-property-tax-review" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Bears should pay</u></a> for the former Arlington Park racecourse site, which the team bought in hopes of building a new stadium in the northwest suburbs.</p><p>And then there are the ever-rising tax bills for homeowners and businesses with pockets not nearly as deep as an NFL franchise. The Board of Review has <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/cook-county/2024/12/12/here-are-more-than-a-dozen-ways-cook-countys-property-tax-system-could-be-more-fair" target="_blank" ><u>openly feuded</u></a> with Cook County Assessor Fritz Kaegi over who’s to blame for the situation.</p><p>Now, two of the three commissioners — Steele and George Cardenas, a veteran Chicago politician — are facing strong challengers in the March Democratic primary.</p><p>Steele faces career Democratic operative Liz Nicholson, while Cardenas is opposed by Juanita Irizarry, a longtime community activist from the Humboldt Park neighborhood who once led the influential Friends of the Parks in Chicago.</p><p>In her first term in office, Steele gained the widest notoriety after she smashed a vehicle she was driving while allegedly drunk, and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2024/11/23/cook-county-politician-board-of-review-samantha-steele-chicago-police" target="_blank" ><u>defiantly told the cops</u></a>, “I’m an elected official,” according to the body-cam video recording of the incident. The case against Steele is pending in Cook County Circuit Court, but she’s fighting the charge.</p><p>Also in 2024, Steele <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2024/12/20/cook-county-board-of-review-samantha-steele-jon-snyder" target="_blank" ><u>fired an aide</u></a> after WBEZ reported that the employee had pleaded guilty in federal court and testified in a corruption trial in northwest Indiana against his brother.</p><p>Last year, the county paid <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/cook-county/2025/01/15/a-former-cook-county-agency-employee-will-get-180-000-in-a-legal-settlement" target="_blank" ><u>a $180,000 settlement</u></a> to another former Steele aide at the Board of Review who sued her, the agency and her chief of staff in a federal whistleblower lawsuit.</p><p>Nicholson accused Steele of “horrific leadership.”</p><p>“I’m the solution,” said Nicholson, a South Side native. “She's the problem, and calls a lot of undue attention to elected officials. It’s so bad.”</p><p>The challenger said this was the right time for her to step forward after three decades as a top adviser to former Illinois Senate President John Cullerton.</p><p>Steele, who lives in Evanston, is in her first term, having been elected in 2022 to represent the board district covering the North Side and northern suburbs. She said she was not surprised to encounter a strong backlash after she unseated an incumbent Board of Review commissioner.</p><p>“I'm not surprised that the Democratic Party didn't endorse me because in Cook County politics, you know the saying is who brought you here, and nobody brought me there,” Steele said.</p><p>She said her frequent clashes with the other two board members — and ethics accusations from the county’s inspector general — distracted from a record of serving with technocratic skill, parlaying her experience in the tax appeals industry. Before moving to the Chicago area and running for office, Steele was the assessor of Indiana’s Tippecanoe County for one term.</p><p>Steele said she intends to fight the DUI charge in Cook County court aggressively but added, “I regret the whole thing. Absolutely. It just was not handled well, but I can't talk about it because it is still a pending legal matter, and once it's been resolved, I'm happy to discuss at great lengths with you.</p><p>“But I can tell you that it has been very traumatic. I received death threats.”</p><p>Cardenas has endorsed Nicholson. The former Chicago alderman — a Navy veteran who served five terms in the City Council — said Nicholson is “more mature” than Steele.</p><p>“I don't see eye-to-eye with Commissioner Steele, by any means,” Cardenas said. Cardenas has his own challenger with many powerful allies. Irizarry’s backers include U.S. Reps. Jesus “Chuy” Garcia, Delia Ramirez and Danny Davis and nine Chicago City Council members.</p><p>Irizarry is best known for successfully opposing “Star Wars” creator George Lucas’ <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/chicago/2016/06/30/head-of-friends-of-the-park-its-on-mr-lucas" target="_blank" ><u>plan for a museum</u></a> near the lakefront a decade ago. But she said she also has vast experience trying to stop rising property taxes and zoning changes in gentrified, historically Hispanic neighborhoods such as Wicker Park.</p><p>Irizarry said Cardenas entered politics through the scandal-plagued political machine of then-Mayor Richard M. Daley.</p><p>“George Cardenas himself came up in the Hispanic Democratic Organization, HDO, and has been very proximate to, if not directly involved in, a lot of scandals along the way,” she said.</p><p>But Cardenas said he was proud to enjoy the endorsements of Democratic Illinois House Speaker Emanuel “Chris” Welch and the county’s Democratic organization. Cardenas also said he had done much in office to help homeowners in minority communities fight property tax increases.</p><p><i>Dan Mihalopoulos is a reporter for WBEZ.</i><br></p>
articles.author: Dan Mihalopoulos | WBEZ

articles.title: CPS to shut down financially distressed ASPIRA campuses, scattering students to new schools
articles.summary: <p>Chicago Public Schools officials told leaders of the ASPIRA charter school network Wednesday night that the district is transferring students out of their two high schools for the remainder of the school year.</p><p>This is an unprecedented move by the school district that will disrupt the education of some 545 students.</p><p>CPS officials told the financially distressed charter network that it was taking this action to “safeguard students’ educational stability” and to make sure it’s complying with state and federal laws. ASPIRA has been relying on cash advances from CPS to make payroll, but the state limits how much CPS can do that.</p><p>“To be clear, CPS is not revoking ASPIRA’s charter at this time, but is moving forward with a student transition process as oversight, due to the emergency situation created by ASPIRA’s failure to demonstrate financial sustainability, which places the education of ASPIRA’s students at risk,” CPS told ASPIRA’s board president David Rodriguez and other ASPIRA board members in a letter.</p><p>Seniors from ASPIRA’s two high schools will be at the CPS board meeting Thursday to try to convince members to change their minds. Just last month, some board members said they were reluctant to keep giving ASPIRA cash advances, even as they sympathize with how this decision impacts students.</p><p>Eighteen-year-old Angeline Melody Solis said she wants the board to keep that top of mind.</p><p>“We don't control the money and where the money goes so it really sucks that at the end of the day, we face the most consequences,” she told WBEZ in an interview Wednesday. “The students have so many concerns about college and their credits. There's just so many things that should have been taken into account, besides the money.”</p><p>CPS officials made this decision after they gave ASPIRA a Tuesday deadline to find outside funding to make it through the school year and to submit a financial plan for the future. ASPIRA responded by saying it was trying to get a bridge loan and would fundraise, according to documents provided to WBEZ by CPS.</p><p>ASPIRA leaders also demanded that CPS give the charter network four years of retroactive funding to make up for what ASPIRA’s leaders believe is a chronic underfunding of all charter schools.</p><p>CPS officials rejected this, saying there is no way to give back funding once the budget for past years is closed. They also said that ASPIRA’s response to CPS’ demands were insufficient.</p><p>The back-and-forth between ASPRIA and CPS has been going on for months, with both sides blaming each other for the network’s financial problems.</p><p>CPS contracts with private organizations to run charter schools and, at the recommendation of CPS leaders, board members renewed ASPIRA’s contract in April 2025.</p><p>As it did with many recent charter renewals, CPS gave ASPIRA a shorter two-year contract and imposed several conditions, including that it participate in a “financial remediation” program that requires the network to submit monthly cash flow statements so the district can conduct additional oversight.</p><p>But the charter network had financial struggles throughout the school year. In November, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/11/19/chicago-public-schools-aspira-charter-network-finances-cps-investigation" target="_blank" ><u>WBEZ and the Sun-Times previously reported</u></a>, CPS sent ASPIRA a letter after a months-long investigation blasting the charter network’s leadership for “a failure of governance” and “a lack of financial proficiency necessary to successfully run a school.”</p><p>ASPIRA’s executive director Edgar Lopez responded that the charter school network did not have enough money to make payroll through the end of the school year. He said the charter network needed $4.5 million.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/11/25/cps-to-help-aspira-make-payroll-but-charter-operators-financial-woes-remain" target="_blank" ><u>CPS has given ASPIRA $2.25 million in cash advances</u></a> “to support payroll and continuity of student services,” according to CPS.</p><p>But, when Lopez announced last month that he needed even more to get through the school year, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/02/11/cps-charter-school-cant-make-payroll-insolvent-closed-march-aspira-chiarts-cpsboard" target="_blank" ><u>board members pushed back</u></a> that it would be against the law to continue sending the charter network extra funding.</p><p>State law requires school districts to give charters between 97% and 103% of what they provide for district-run schools. CPS would be over that limit if it kept advancing ASPIRA money.</p><p>Board members also said they worried about the precedent they were setting.</p><p>In just the last two years, four private organizations with contracts to run public schools in Chicago have said they no longer want to operate them.</p><p>Charter schools’ budgets are closely tied to student enrollment and, with an overall <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/09/25/chicago-public-schools-enrollment-drops-restarts-decrease-migrant-students-latino-immigration" target="_blank" ><u>decline in the CPS student population</u></a>, charters are having trouble filling their seats. ASPIRA has the CPS permission to admit up to 1,600 students at its two high schools, but this year started out with less than 600. Charter school operators also cite other issues that have added to their financial troubles, including rising labor costs.</p><p>Board members are <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/11/04/cps-chiarts-epic-academy" target="_blank" >taking over management</a> of the Chicago High School for the Arts and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2024/12/21/cps-votes-to-spare-7-acero-charter-schools-from-closure-plans-to-absorb-5-in-2026" target="_blank" >five schools run by the Acero School</a>s charter network. But CPS is letting two other Acero schools and a small Southeast Side charter school called EPIC close.</p>
articles.author: Sarah Karp | WBEZ

articles.title: Fundación de Chicago otorga $15 millones para la construcción de dos centros comunitarios en el lado oeste
articles.summary: <p>Los dos hijos de Richard Townsell crecieron practicando deportes, lo que a menudo significaba conducir de 45 minutos a una hora para asistir a prácticas fuera de los límites de la ciudad.</p><p>Y si la práctica no implicaba viajar, requería un poco de trabajo adicional.</p><p>“Entreno [lucha libre] en la Chicago Hope Academy. Practicamos en la cancha de baloncesto, y tenemos que enrollar los tapices al final de cada práctica”, dijo Townsell, director ejecutivo de Lawndale Christian Development Corp. “Tenemos una instalación vieja, y ese es el caso para muchos atletas de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago. No tienen una instalación permanente”.</p><p>Pero eso pronto cambiará para muchos estudiantes y jóvenes en el lado oeste.</p><p>La Fundación Lohengrin, con sede en Chicago, anunció el miércoles que Lawndale Christian Development y Erie Neighborhood House recibirán $10 millones y $5 millones, respectivamente, para la construcción de dos nuevos centros comunitarios y juveniles en el lado oeste.</p><p>El premio proviene de la iniciativa “Thriving Youth, Stronger Communities”, que se lanzó en 2024 para ayudar a crear entornos donde los jóvenes de Chicago puedan crecer y prosperar.</p><p>Lawndale Christian Development y Erie Neighborhood House son los primeros en recibir el premio, dijo Mark Rodríguez, director ejecutivo de la Fundación Lohengrin.</p><p>Los $10 millones apoyarán el proyecto de Lawndale Christian Development, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/11/20/plan-commission-approves-depaul-practice-facility-morgan-park-commons" target="_blank" >One Lawndale</a>, que fue aprobado por el Concejo Municipal a finales de 2025.</p><p>El premio “hace posible [One Lawndale]”, dijo Richard Townsell, director ejecutivo de Lawndale Christian Development.</p><p>“Con la Fundación Lohengrin siendo la primera en entrar, ayuda a asegurar a todos los demás”, dijo Townsell. Añadió que un premio de este tamaño puede ayudar a atraer inversores y financiamiento adicionales.</p><p>One Lawndale, localizado en la esquina de Springfield Avenue y Cermak Road, será un complejo deportivo pionero para el lado oeste. El centro deportivo y de recreación bajo techo de $38 millones permitirá la práctica de deportes durante todo el año, con dos campos de césped interior, seis canchas de baloncesto de tamaño completo, una sala de boxeo y acondicionamiento físico, un estudio de lucha libre, un salón multipropósito, áreas de comida y bar y una tienda profesional.</p><p>Las ligas deportivas, los programas de mentoría y otras iniciativas estarán dirigidas por socios liderados por personas afroamericanas y latinas de North Lawndale y La Villita (South Lawndale).</p><p>Uno de los objetivos de la iniciativa “Thriving Youth, Stronger Communities” es crear espacios seguros para los jóvenes para ayudar en la prevención de la violencia.</p><p>Townsell cree que One Lawndale logrará eso ya que también ofrecerá programas de nutrición, salud mental y mentoría.</p><p>“Pensamos que algunas de las mejores acciones de prevención de la violencia que se pueden hacer son trabajar con jóvenes antes de que se vean involucrados en comportamientos por aburrimiento o porque realmente no tienen nada más que hacer”, agregó Townsell. “Para nosotros, poder tener algo así en el vecindario que sea de alta calidad y que tenga todos los demás apoyos necesarios, creemos que será el mejor esfuerzo en prevención de la violencia y construcción de comunidad”.</p><p>La Fundación Lohengrin está ayudando al desarrollador a encontrar otros socios fundacionales para cerrar su brecha de financiamiento, dijo Townsell. Espera comenzar la construcción a finales del verano o principios del otoño. Se prevé que esté terminado para el otoño o invierno de 2027.</p><p>Erie Neighborhood House utilizará su premio de $5 millones para el Centro de Erie House en La Villita.</p><p>La organización de servicios sociales ha estado en La Villita durante aproximadamente 20 años. Actualmente alquila un edificio en el vecindario.</p><p>La Villita Erie House, ubicada en 2653-59 S. Kildare Ave., permitirá a la organización no lucrativa ampliar su huella en el vecindario, tanto en el número de personas a las que sirve como en un espacio más grande y permanente. El edificio de cinco pisos tendrá espacios para niños, adolescentes y otros miembros de la comunidad, con el concepto final moldeado por la opinión de la comunidad.</p><p>Cristina De La Rosa, directora ejecutiva de Erie Neighborhood House, dijo que los miembros de la comunidad han expresado continuamente la necesidad de seguridad. La organización no lucrativa organizó grupos focales y distribuyó encuestas para escuchar más de los miembros de la comunidad sobre cómo sería un espacio seguro para los residentes y qué tipo de programación les gustaría ver en el nuevo centro.</p><p>“Queríamos ser realmente intencionales”, agregó De La Rosa. “Realmente queríamos asegurarnos de que cuando las personas ingresaran al edificio, sintieran que podían verse a sí mismas allí, que estaba diseñado para ellas”.</p><p>El resultado es un centro con un gimnasio de tamaño completo, salones flexibles, un estudio de artes, oficinas de consejería y terapia privadas, salones para adolescentes y tecnología, cocina comunitaria, espacios verdes al aire libre, un parque infantil en la azotea y un jardín infantil.</p><p>Erie Neighborhood House ya ofrece una serie de programas juveniles, pero el nuevo centro permitirá a la organización ofrecer programación de todo el día después de la escuela, dijo De La Rosa. Y durante el verano, los niños en edad escolar pueden quedarse durante la semana y recibir desayuno, almuerzo y un refrigerio.</p><p>Erie Neighborhood House espera comenzar la construcción este otoño y abrir dentro de un año después de su inauguración. El proyecto de $22 millones expandirá las oportunidades para que la organización no lucrativa se asocie con otras organizaciones en la zona como One Lawndale.</p><p>“No vemos esto como dos vecindarios separados”, dijo Townsell sobre North Lawndale y La Villita. “Vemos esto como un Lawndale y personas uniéndose para apoyar a los jóvenes”.</p><p>Rodríguez destacó que la fundación no tenía la intención de elegir a dos ganadores, y mucho menos dos de vecindarios adyacentes. Pero el premio está ayudando a conectar ambos proyectos con posibles socios de capital, duplicando su impacto previsto.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ff0000" name="image-ff0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b285353/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1235x693+0+4/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2Fc6%2F9880670a427aa01581599b9b39cd%2Fone-lawndale-recreation-center.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6b9c6dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1235x693+0+4/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2Fc6%2F9880670a427aa01581599b9b39cd%2Fone-lawndale-recreation-center.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bf91fe7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1235x693+0+4/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2Fc6%2F9880670a427aa01581599b9b39cd%2Fone-lawndale-recreation-center.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Boceto del centro comunitario One Lawndale de la Christian Development Corp. de North Lawndale, un proyecto que reemplazaría un antiguo lote de almacenamiento de la CTA. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bf91fe7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1235x693+0+4/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2Fc6%2F9880670a427aa01581599b9b39cd%2Fone-lawndale-recreation-center.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c08ca3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1235x693+0+4/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2Fc6%2F9880670a427aa01581599b9b39cd%2Fone-lawndale-recreation-center.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bf91fe7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1235x693+0+4/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2Fc6%2F9880670a427aa01581599b9b39cd%2Fone-lawndale-recreation-center.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Boceto del centro comunitario One Lawndale de la Christian Development Corp. de North Lawndale, un proyecto que reemplazaría un antiguo lote de almacenamiento de la CTA.<br></p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Proporcionada por la Fundación Lohengrin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>North Lawndale y La Villita tienen mayores números de jóvenes y violencia juvenil, según la fundación. El número de jóvenes que los proyectos servirían, junto con la viabilidad de cada proyecto, ayudó a elevar las dos propuestas.</p><p>Hubo más de 70 presentaciones para “Thriving Youth, Stronger Communities”, lo que muestra el entusiasmo por apoyar a las comunidades jóvenes, dijo Rodríguez.</p><p>“No hay límite en las ideas o en la necesidad. Sólo hay un límite en los recursos disponibles”, agregó.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
articles.author: Abby Miller

articles.title: Chicago's 311 system turns into a 'black hole' for residents' nonemergency complaints
articles.summary: <p>Chicago’s 311 system was created to prevent people who live and work in the city from calling 911 for quality-of-life concerns that aren't emergencies. But according to outgoing Inspector General Deborah Witzburg, the system has turned into a “black hole” that leaves people in the dark about the status of the city’s response to their complaints — and fosters distrust of government.</p><p>Witzburg reached that conclusion after a comprehensive audit of the 28-year-old system, which is swamped with as many as 2.3 million requests annually for routine city services.</p><p>After a citywide survey of users of the 311 system and in-depth conversations with aldermanic staff in five wards, Witzburg identified a host of problems that prevents 311 from living up to its potential.</p><p>Residents and businesses are not updated consistently about the status of their complaints or the city’s response. Those complaints can range from a broken street light or gaping pothole to missing, stolen or broken garbage carts, as well as rats in alleys.</p><p></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2025/12/12/chicago-kicks-off-snow-season-record-311-calls-ice-uncleared-sidewalks-roads" >Chicago kicks off snow season with record number of 311 calls over uncleared sidewalks, roads</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Staffing constraints hamstring the city’s ability to pinpoint patterns of requests and improve services. Exacerbating the staffing shortage is the fact that 311 system employees are required to assist more than 40 city departments and local government agencies with their 311 system needs, but have “limited capacity to do so.”</p><p>Managing limited city resources more efficiently is “explicitly part of 311’s mission,” but no one is responsible for using the mountain of information from 311 requests to “deliver improved customer service.”</p><p>“People make a 311 request for a city service, go look at the status of their online request and see that it has been coded 'completed.’ But then, they look out their front window and they see that the pothole is still gaping in their street,” Witzburg told the Chicago Sun-Times. “If people are asking the government for help, and they are not getting the help that they need, and they’re not able to get any information about why they’re not getting help, or when it might come, we are risking public trust in the provision of city services.”</p><p>To eliminate the “black hole” of service requests, Witzburg recommended that the Office of Emergency Management and Communications that operates both 911 and 311 “clarify its public resources about the service request process” and communicate more timely and accurate information to residents and businesses about the status of their service requests.</p><p>At a time when many private-sector businesses are using artificial intelligence to handle most, if not all, of their customer service requests, Witzburg also pushed for 311 staff to implement “technology solutions” to help multiple city departments “respond to requests efficiently in the field.”</p><p>The audit also recommends a staffing analysis and that a so-called “service advocacy unit” hold regular meetings with city departments, in part to identify patterns of complaints that might trigger a realignment of city resources devoted to chronic problems.</p><p>In its official response to Witzburg’s findings and recommendations, OEMC noted that the service advocacy unit “has not existed for several years.” But the department has attempted to fill the void by sending monthly emails to city departments “notifying them of open service requests.” Training is also provided to departments twice a month.</p><p>“Management recognizes the importance of a centralized service advocacy function in supporting departments and maximizing the value of 311 data. However, the Service Advocacy Unit is no longer in existence. The responsibilities… have been absorbed into existing operational roles with limited capacity,” the OEMC response states.</p><p>“Management agrees that more structured and consistent engagement with departments would be beneficial and will continue to explore opportunities with existing resources to improve communication regarding system settings, service level agreement and best practices.”</p><p>In 1998, Mayor Richard M. Daley created the 311 system — at a cost of $4 million — to take the load off 911. At the time, 911 was handling 3.7 million calls a year, with as many as 40% nonemergencies.</p><p>The nonemergency number was launched after a massive campaign to persuade Chicagoans to stop doing what 4,000 people did every day: call 911 about routine problems.</p><p>In 2015, a City Council rebellion forced Mayor Rahm Emanuel to shelve his controversial plan to privatize 311 and find another way to bankroll a sorely needed system upgrade after alderpersons argued that services so pivotal to their residents must be provided by Chicagoans who know the city and its neighborhoods.</p><p>Three years later, Emanuel flipped the switch on an overhaul that not only allowed Chicagoans to text their service requests, along with photographic evidence, but also let the city text those same residents back to let them know when crews would do the work. Residents were supposed to get a text when work was completed.</p>
articles.author: Fran Spielman

articles.title: CPS approves calendars for next 2 school years with longer summer break
articles.summary: <p>The Chicago Board of Education on Thursday approved the academic calendars for the next two school years without adjusting the end of an academic quarter so it ends before winter break, as some board members had suggested considering.</p><p>The first day of school next fall is set for Aug. 24, and the last day will be on June 11, 2027. It’s the sixth straight year in which the district kicks off classes before Labor Day.</p><p>The district unveiled the calendars last month and presented them at a CPS board meeting but sought further feedback from community members after board members raised questions about the timing around winter break and the end of the second quarter.</p><p>The second academic quarter ends after the break, on Jan. 22, 2027. Board members were concerned that students, particularly high schoolers, wouldn’ t be able to reset and wind down during their vacation because they’d be worried about coming back to end-of-quarter tests.</p><p>In response, the district opened a survey from Jan. 21 through Jan. 27 to ask community members to choose between three calendar options: the one recommended by the district and two that would have started classes earlier in August to complete the second quarter before winter break.</p><p>Nicole Milberg, CPS chief of teaching and learning, presented the findings at a board meeting earlier this month. More than 22,000 people responded, and 55% preferred the original schedule recommended by the district, she said.</p><p>Milberg acknowledged at that meeting that not everyone will be satisfied with the schedule.</p><p>“There is no perfect calendar, no matter how many times we go to the drawing board. There will never be a calendar that meets every stakeholder's preferences,” Milberg said.</p><p>The schedule allows students to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/01/12/chicago-public-schools-releases-2026-2027-calendar-with-longer-summer-break" target="_blank" ><u>enjoy their typical number of summer vacation days</u></a> after last year’s shortened break of just 67 days. The last day of the current school year is on June 4, resulting in 81 days of vacation before classes begin again in late August.</p><p>School started later in the 2024-25 academic year because of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2024/2/1/24058117/cps-calendar-2024-25-school-year-dnc-push-back-date" target="_blank" ><u>Democratic National Convention</u></a>, resulting in a shorter break. In summer 2024, students also had 81 days of break.</p><p>For the 2026-2027 school year students will continue to get a week off for Thanksgiving break, as well as a day off for the congressional midterm elections on Nov. 3.</p><p>Winter break is scheduled for Dec. 21 through Jan. 1, 2027, while spring break will be from March 22-26, 2027.</p><p>The start of the 2027-2028 school year is set for Aug. 23, 2027, and the last day will be on June 9, 2028.</p><p>Each of the newly approved calendars add up to 176 student attendance days, with 14 non-student attendance days for staff training, school improvement work and parent-teacher conferences.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink"
